gmw_00006050.name: J0842+0547
gmw_00006050.overview: J0842+0547 is a single, undisturbed galaxy hosting a double-peaked active galactic nucleus [arXiv:2209.02717]. It shows no close companion within 3 arcseconds [arXiv:2209.02717], is classified as having an optical outflow [arXiv:2209.02717], and has no detected 21-cm absorption [arXiv:2209.02717].

sources.summary: The paper describes J0842+0547 as a single, undisturbed double-peaked AGN with no close Keck-detected companion, optical outflow classification, and no detected 21-cm absorption.

gmw_00006051.name: J1203+1319
gmw_00006051.overview: J1203+1319 is a Seyfert 2 active galactic nucleus [arXiv:1708.01185] that appears as a single, undisturbed optical system [arXiv:2209.02717] but is a candidate dual active galactic nucleus [arXiv:2209.02717]. It hosts symmetric, S-shaped precessing radio jets [arXiv:1612.06750, arXiv:1708.01185] that extend up to 3 kiloparsecs [arXiv:1801.03451].

sources.summary: The paper presents VLA observations of 2MASXJ12032061+1319316, finding a double-peaked emission-line galaxy with S-shaped helical radio jets, a compact core, two hotspots, and precession near 10^5 years.

quotes.quote: We present high resolution radio continuum observations with the Karl G. Jansky Very Large Array at 6, 8.5, 11.5 and 15 GHz of the double-peaked emission-line galaxy 2MASXJ12032061+1319316. The radio emission has a prominent S-shaped morphology with highly symmetric radio jets that extend over a distance of 1.5^ (1.74 kpc) on either side of the core of size 0.1^ (116 pc). The radio jets have a helical structure resembling the precessing jets in the galaxy NGC 326 which has confirmed dual active galactic nuclei (AGN). The nuclear bulge velocity dispersion gives an upper limit of (1.56 0.26 ) 10 ^8 M _ for the total mass of nuclear black hole(s). We present a simple model of precessing jets in 2MASXJ1203 and find that the precession timescale is around 10 ^5 years: this matches the source lifetime estimate via spectral aging.

The galaxy 2MASXJ1203 is part of a larger ongoing study of DPAGN at 6 GHz using the EVLA (Project ID: VLA/15A-068) (Rubinur K. et al. 2016B in preparation). Our preliminary data analysis at 6 GHz revealed that the nuclear emission had an interesting S-shaped morphology and two hotspots. On analysing additional archival data at higher frequencies (8.5 and 11.5 GHz), we clearly resolved the compact core and found S-shaped radio jets.

sources.summary: The paper describes 2MASX J1203 as a Seyfert 2 double-peaked AGN with symmetric S-shaped radio jets, consistent with precession from binary or dual SMBH scenarios.

quotes.quote: 2MASX J1203 2MASX J1203 (or 2MASX J12032061+1319316) also hosts a Seyfert 2 nucleus and shows double peaked emission lines in its SDSS spectrum (Figure 1) (for details see Rubinur et al. 2017), the only difference being that the peaks are not equal in this AGN. We carried out high resolution, radio observation of 2MASX J1203 using the Karl G. Jansky Very Large Array (EVLA) at 6 and 15 GHz in July, 2015 and May, 2016 respectively. We also used VLA archival data at 8.5 and 11.5 GHz, from March, 2014 observations.

We found that the radio emission has a prominent S-shaped morphology at all frequencies. The S-shaped jets are very symmetric and extend out to a distance of 1.5 ^ (or 1.74 Kpc) on either side of a core of size 0.1 ^ (or 116 pc where the galaxy distance is assumed to be 245 Mpc) (Figure 2). The structure is similar to precessing radio jets observed at larger scale in galaxies (e.g. NGC 326) and our precession model gives a precession timescale of 10 ^5 years. We also obtained a similar age from spectral aging analysis using the spectral index maps. We find that the precessing jets could be due to binary or dual SMBHs, a single SMBH with a tilted accretion disk or a dual SMBH in which a previous close passage of the SMBH caused the precession of the radio jets.

sources.summary: The paper identifies 2MASX J12032061+1319316 as a DPAGN with two compact EVLA components that resolve into S-shaped jet hotspots, a 3 kpc jet extent, and a young CSO-like age from precession/spectral aging.

quotes.quote: The DPAGN, 2MASX J12032061+1319316 (J1203 for short), showed two compact radio components in its 6 GHz EVLA image (Rubinur et al. 2017). The components were separated by 0.7 ^ . We obtained higher-resolution 8-12 GHz EVLA archival data of this galaxy and found that the two components were actually two hot-spots of an S-shaped radio jet (see Figure 1). The core size is 100 parsec and the total extent of the jets is 3 kpc.

We have used the relation from van der Laan Perola (1969) to derive the electron lifetimes; these also turn out to be 10 ^5 yrs. As the precession and spectral aging models give the same age of 10 ^5 yrs, we concluded that J1203 is a young radio source and most likely a compact symmetric object (CSO; see details in Rubinur et al. 2017).

sources.summary: The paper describes J1203+1319 as a single optical system whose Keck imaging shows double structure making it a candidate dual AGN, with no detected 21-cm absorption.

For the remaining three sources, J0842+0547, J1203+1319, J1243-0058, where we do not detect absorption, we place 3 upper limit on the integrated optical depth for a velocity width of 100 .

gmw_00006052.name: J1517+3353
gmw_00006052.overview: J1517+3353 is a radio-loud active galactic nucleus that exhibits bipolar radio jets [arXiv:2209.02717]. The system features strong 21-cm absorption, with a weaker blueshifted component that may trace neutral outflowing gas driven by the jets [arXiv:2209.02717].

sources.summary: The paper describes J1517+3353 as a radio-loud AGN with strong two-component 21-cm absorption; optical and VLA data favor an outflow scenario, with blueshifted absorption possibly tracing neutral outflowing gas.

quotes.quote: The \ 21-cm absorption towards J1517+3353 (see top panel of Fig. fig:det_hi_spec ) is best fit with two Gaussian components. The strongest component is redshifted by 136 \ from the systemic redshift (based on average of the two \ lines), while the weaker component is at 13 . The absorption arises kinematically between the two \ emission peaks. The velocity width ( 90) of the absorption is 281 . This is one of the strongest \ 21-cm absorption detected in low- z radio-loud AGN, where the integrated optical depth corresponds to \ 2 10^ 22 \ for \ = 100 K and \ = 1.

Recall that the double-peaked emission in this system is classified as likely originating due to outflows based on optical long-slit spectroscopy comerford2018 . In addition, multi-band (1.4, 5, 8 and 22 GHz) VLA images show bipolar radio jets aligned with the ionized gas, further supporting the radio jet-driven outflow scenario rosario2010b . The weaker, blueshifted \ absorption component could be tracing the neutral outflowing gas in this case.

gmw_00006053.name: NGC 6908
gmw_00006053.overview: NGC 6908 is a lenticular S0 galaxy [arXiv:0802.1713]. It is actively interacting with the neighboring galaxy NGC 6907, a process that began approximately 34 million years ago [arXiv:0802.1713].

sources.summary: The paper argues NGC 6908 is a real S0 galaxy interacting with NGC 6907, distinguishing its absorption-line velocity from emission produced in NGC 6907 disk gas.

quotes.quote: Taking into account the rotational velocity of NGC 6907 and the velocity derived from the absorption lines for NGC 6908, we verified that the relative velocity between these systems is lower than 60 km s ^ 1 . The emission lines observed in the direction of NGC 6908, not typical of S0 galaxies, have the same velocity expected for the NGC 6907 rotation curve. Some emission lines are inside their equivalent absorption lines, reinforcing the idea they were not formed in NGC 6908. Finally, the H i profile exhibits details of the interaction, showing three components: one for NGC 6908, another for the excited gas in the NGC 6907 disk and a last one for the gas with higher relative velocities left behind NGC 6908 by dynamical friction, used to estimate the time when the interaction started in (3.4 0.6) 10^7 years ago.

In a recent paper, Madore et al. (2007) revealed that NGC 6908, once classified as a PofG (part of a galaxy), is in fact a lenticular S0(6/7) galaxy, as indicated by its exponential brightness profile observed in several filters. Based on long-slit observations, these authors found different radial velocities for NGC 6908 lines in absorption (3113 73 km s ^ 1 ) and in emission (3060 16) km.s ^ 1 .

gmw_00006054.name: NGC 5580
gmw_00006054.overview: NGC 5580 is a lenticular galaxy located within a loose group of galaxies [arXiv:1402.5528]. It is surrounded by steep-spectrum, extended radio bubbles that likely originated from a past episode of active galactic nucleus activity [arXiv:1402.5528].

sources.summary: The paper identifies NGC 5580 as a lenticular galaxy whose past AGN activity likely produced the surrounding steep-spectrum radio bubbles, making it a dying radio galaxy outside a dense cluster.

quotes.quote: The galaxy pair NGC 5580 and NGC 5588 are part of a loose group of galaxies. They are surrounded by steep-spectrum, extended radio emission which was previously suggested to be a down-scaled example of Mpc-size radio haloes present in galaxies clusters. We present a multi-frequency study of the radio-emission aimed to clarify its nature. The source has been observed with the Giant Meterwave Radio Telescope ( ) at 235, 325 and 610 MHz and the images obtained were combined with archival data to cover the frequency range 150--1400 MHz. The new observations revealed the presence of a second, fainter lobe on the South-East of NGC 5580.

It shows one large bubble with no apparent internal structure, encompassing the lenticular galaxy NGC 5580 and the spiral galaxy NGC 5588. The two galaxies sit at the edges of this extended emission, which has been previously classified as an uncommon example of radio halo in a galaxy group Delain2006 . The new observations revealed unambiguously the presence of a second bubble of similar size as the first one, located to the South-East of NGC 5580. The spectral shape of the source shows a flattening of the SED around both galaxies. The nature of this emission is probably related to a single past episode of AGN activity from NGC 5580, which would be able to explain both the source morphology and spectrum. In this case, the presence of NGC 5588 would be a coincidence and NGC 5580 would be a notable case of dead radio galaxy located outside a dense cluster.

gmw_00006055.name: NGC 5588
gmw_00006055.overview: NGC 5588 is a barred-spiral galaxy that shows no signs of recent interaction or confirmed nuclear activity in optical images [arXiv:1402.5528]. Its detected radio emission is attributed to star formation activity occurring within its bar and spiral arms [arXiv:1402.5528].

sources.summary: The paper treats NGC 5588 as a spiral companion projected on the radio bubble; its own radio spectrum is attributed to star formation rather than the dead-AGN emission.

quotes.quote: The two large, bright galaxies are NGC 5580 (South-East) and NGC 5588 (North-West) and they are in projection located at the edges of the main bubble . The first is a lenticular galaxy while the second is a barred-spiral. None of them have confirmed nuclear activity nor signs of recent interaction in the optical images. Superimposing the radio contour on the optical image, clear emission from NGC 5588 is detected. Since we have previously subtracted all the point-like sources, we believe this emission originates by star formation activity in the arms and the bar of the spiral galaxy rather than from a compact AGN. The point-like emission from NGC 5588 is in fact only 2.7 mJy (at 323 MHz), while the extended emission associated with the galaxy is about an order of magnitude stronger.

Isolating the flux associated with the spiral galaxy NGC 5588 we extracted the spectrum shown in Fig. fig:sed-NGC5588 ( = -0.81 0.13 ), which is the combination of the extended emission due to the large bubble , plus the emission due to star formation in NGC 5588 itself. Zone 1 Zone 2 NGC5588 In Fig. fig:spidx-map we show a pixel-by-pixel spectral index map limited to all the pixels having a value larger then 1 in every map. The map does not show any clear linear or circular gradient. However, a sensible flattening in the spectral index is detected in the region around both galaxies. A flattening in the spectral index is usually interpreted as emission coming from younger population of CRe, as the higher-energetic CRe are depleted faster. When this happens and the particle energization has stopped, the spectra develop an exponential cut-off at the frequency _b which moves towards lower frequencies as the time passes following _b t^ 2 B^ 3 , where t is the time from the CRe injection and B is the magnetic field intensity. We calculated the averaged equipartition magnetic field and the energy content of the brightest part of the source (zone 1 in Fig. fig:masks ). We used the revised equipartition formula presented in . We assumed the emitting region to have a depth l = 87.5 kpc, which is the average diameter of the source. The ratio between CR electrons and protons has been set to K_0 = 100 and we used a spectral index of = -0.87 . Given these parameters, the estimated magnetic field is B_ eq = 2.5 \ G and the total energy content is E_ tot = 5.1 10^ 57 erg, which is typical for radio galaxies . As a final note, we underline that given the spectral steepness, which implies strong losses, the equipartition values should be regarded only as tentative estimates. Although we obtained several images and the spectral information of our target, it is still hard to ultimately classify it. A first possibility is that the emission is a down-scaled version of the Mpc-scale radio haloes present at the centre of merging galaxy clusters. This classification was first proposed by . As also noticed by the authors, no sign of high X-ray emission is visible in the survey. Although the exposure time is low ( <1 ks), this is enough to put a constraint on the X-ray luminosity of L_X < 41.2 erg/s. This puts the source much below the known radio--X-ray correlation for radio haloes e.g., and references and therein Feretti2012 . There is evidence of radio haloes more radio-luminous than predicted e.g., Abell 523; Giovannini2011, Farnsworth2013 . These powerful radio halos associated with low-density environments could be either young halos, or be related to clusters at a specific time of the merger event which triggered the halo formation (namely when particle acceleration processes are at a higher efficiency). However, these cases lie one order of magnitude above the radio--X-ray correlation, while the object of this paper is at least two further orders of magnitude brighter in radio then what is predicted. This consideration would be true even if the emission comes from an unknown background galaxy cluster (assuming a negligible k-correction). Another difference compared to cluster radio halos is that the source is not centrally located in the potential well of the galaxy group (see Fig. fig:galaxygroup ). Finally, the source global spectral index is flatter compared with normal radio halo spectral indexes which at low frequencies are usually in the range 1.2 to 1.8 Feretti2012 . A second possibility is that the emission is an extreme case of what are known as ``Taffy'' galaxies Hummel1986, Condon1993, Condon2002, Drzazga2011 . The angular projected distance between the two galaxies is 4 .78 = 62 kpc, which is larger than the typical distances of the ``Taffy'' galaxies ( 10 kpc). The proper distance of the two galaxies is unknown. When at the same distance, the two galaxies will have a differential line-of-sight velocity of 189 km/s based on the redshift difference, which is compatible with the velocity dispersion of the group which they belong 461 km/s; VanDriel2001 . However, the fainter/steeper filament towards the East would be hard to explain and the extended emission does not resemble a bridge connecting the two galaxies, like previous discovered examples of Taffy galaxies. Observation of emission lines as can be used to estimate galaxies relative motion, distance and distortion due to interaction. This could help understanding whether the two galaxies had a recent close passage as requested by this scenario. A third possibility is that we are observing a case of a dead radio galaxy. Since dead radio galaxies are usually found in dense galaxy clusters Murgia2011 and not in loose galaxy groups, this case is somewhat uncommon. However, major clues which brought us to favour this thesis are the source's brightness and spectral morphology. Without claiming complex projection effects, we could explain the two bubbles as a relic of NGC 5580 AGN activity. However, in this case, the strong difference in surface brightness is uncommon (but not unique) and the presence of NGC 5588 just a chance alignment. Source spectra suggest a general steepening (i.e. ageing) as we move away from NGC 5580 which would be in line with the proposed scenario. The flattening in the spectral index map around NGC 5588 would be caused by the the dominating emission from the star formation in the spiral galaxy NGC 5588, over the dead-AGN radiation.

gmw_00006056.name: BZQ J1128 + 5925
gmw_00006056.overview: BZQ J1128 + 5925 is a compact extragalactic radio source [arXiv:1105.4718] and blazar [arXiv:2108.09054] that exhibits strong, fast intraday variability [arXiv:2108.09054]. High-resolution observations reveal an east-west core-jet structure [arXiv:0909.4615], with historical variations in both its core size and total core flux density [arXiv:0909.4615].

sources.summary: The paper presents VLBA monitoring of J1128+592, finding an east-west core-jet structure, no short-term structural change, and archival core flux and size changes that may explain quenching of intraday variability.

quotes.quote: We have presented new results derived from our 3 frequency (5/8/15 GHz) polarization-sensitive VLBA observations of the IDV source J1128+592. An aim of these observations was to discover whether source structural changes are responsible for the decrease in the strength of IDV observed in this source during 2007-2008. Our data uncovered an east-west oriented jet-core structure of the source. For the entire six epochs of VLBA data (spanning 0.6 yrs), we were unable to detect any significant change in the total intensity VLBI structure of the source.

Compared with archival VLBA data, we found that the total flux density of the core changed significantly. This agrees with our single-dish monitoring result, which included a flare-like brightening in J1128+592 during early 2006. Compared to archival data, the size of the core at 5 GHz also increased significantly, which may explain the quenching of the intraday variability. Although we did not detect any structural changes in the source during our observations, the core size was found to be excessively large at 15 GHz.

sources.summary: The paper analyzes J 1128+5925 as a compact extragalactic radio source with densely sampled radio variability and a low fractal dimension, implying higher predictability than optical IDV signals.

quotes.quote: As a second example of the determination of the fractal dimension we consider the short time-scale radio variations of the compact extragalactic radio source J 1128+5925 ( Gab ). The flux density variability of J 1128+5925 was monitored with dense time sampling between 2.7 and 10.45 GHz. For our analysis we use the data observed with the 100 m Effelsberg radio telescope of the Max-Planck-Institut fur Radioastronomie at 2.70 GHz, 4.85 GHz, and 10.45 GHz. Ten observing sessions that lasted several days during the period between 2004 - 2006 were performed.

On the other hand the radio band emission pattern of the compact extragalactic radio source J 1128+5925 shows a relatively low fractal dimension of the order of D=1.25 , with a much higher predictability index.

sources.summary: The paper describes BZQ J1128 + 5925 as a blazar with strong fast IDV in both sessions, slightly increased flux, and prior seasonal IDV timescale variations.

quotes.quote: BZQ J1128 + 5925 : the blazar shows a strong fast IDV in both sessions (middle panel of Figure fig1 ), its m' increases from 3.54 in session 1 to 5.18 in session 2. Its mean flux density increases slightly from 0.621 0.023 Jy in session 1 to 0.630 0.033 Jy in session 2. This fast IDV was first found with Urumqi 25 m telescope, and it has shown seasonal variations of IDV timescales and can be well fitted with the annual modulation model .

gmw_00006057.overview: The object 1357 + 769 is a strongly variable blazar [arXiv:2108.09054]. It has been newly identified as a strong intraday variability source, exhibiting a declining mean flux density and modified modulation index [arXiv:2108.09054].

sources.summary: The paper highlights 1357 + 769 as a strongly variable blazar, newly discovered as a strong IDV source, with declining modified modulation index and mean flux density.

quotes.quote: Here, we only show a few sources for example, three sources with strong variability---1357 + 769, J1128 + 5925 and WISE J092915.43 + 501336.0 in Figure fig1 , two counterexamples---B2 1239 + 376 and 1633 + 382 with no sign of variability in Figure fig2 .

1357 + 769 : this blazar shows strong IDVs in both sessions (upper panel of Figure fig1 ), the m' decreases from 5.28 in session 1 to 3.02 in session 2, while the mean flux density decreases from 0.461 0.025 Jy in session 1 to 0.411 0.013 Jy in session 2.

Seven new IDV sources are detected in our observations. We find that the three strongest IDVs appear in relatively lower flux density <S> < 0.7 Jy in the sample, in which blazar 1357 + 769 is discovered as strong IDV for the first time.

gmw_00006058.name: MMJ065837 5557.0
gmw_00006058.overview: MMJ065837 5557.0 is a high-redshift submillimeter galaxy with an estimated photometric redshift of approximately 2.9 [arXiv:0904.1203]. It is a background luminous infrared galaxy that is gravitationally lensed and amplified by the foreground Bullet Cluster [arXiv:0904.1203].

sources.summary: The paper treats MMJ065837 5557.0 as a lensed background LIRG/submillimeter galaxy, measuring BLAST fluxes, estimating zphot about 2.9, and deriving a lensing-corrected FIR luminosity near 10^12 solar luminosities.
sources.paper_coordinate_text: RA Centroid Position & 6^ h 58^ m 37. ^ s 31 (J2000) Dec Centroid Position & 55 57 1 5 (J2000)

quotes.quote: The high apparent flux and close proximity to a critical line of magnification on the lensing model motivated the authors to conclude that the source is likely to be a background galaxy amplified by the foreground mass. This AzTEC source (MMJ065837 5557.0) is coincident with a LIRG detected by IRAC (which possibly has multiple infrared images of the galaxy), with a redshift z_ phot ^ IR =2.7 0.2 Wilson2008,Gonzalez2009 . We present the 250, 350, and 500 \ measurements of a bright source (BLAST J065837 555708) located in the direction of the Bullet Cluster.

The resulting color corrected flux densities for the the source are S_ 250 = 94 30 , S_ 350 = 96 27 , and S_ 500 = 110 21 mJy. The BLAST colors sample the peak of the source spectrum, and 250--1100 \ flux densities place strong constraints on its photometric redshift. Using a Monte Carlo photometric redshift method Hughes2002, Aretxaga2003 , we have calculated a redshift z_ phot = 2.9^ +0.6 _ 0.3 .

The redshift probability distribution is inconsistent with the redshift of the Bullet Cluster ( z 0.3 ), ruling out the possibility that the millimeter AzTEC source is associated with the foreground mass. Assuming the best-fit photometric redshift we have derived the observed FIR luminosity of the source, in the absence of lensing; to be L_ FIR ^ obs = 2 10^ 13 .

gmw_00006059.name: Source 4
gmw_00006059.overview: Source 4 is a spectroscopically confirmed member galaxy of the Bullet Cluster [arXiv:0904.1203]. It is a dominant source of emission at 250 and 350 microns, and it contributes to the 24-micron emission in its region [arXiv:0904.1203].

sources.summary: The paper identifies Source 4 as a spectroscopically confirmed Bullet Cluster member galaxy whose 24 micron and shorter-wavelength BLAST emission contaminate the lensed background LIRG measurements.
sources.paper_coordinate_text: Submillimeter flux densities in each band associated with the dominant 24 \ cluster member galaxy at RA (J2000) = 6:58:37.40, Dec (J2000) = 55:57:24, identified as Source 4 in Figure fig:thumbs and Table table:photom .

quotes.quote: Though a number of these galaxies likely contribute to the submillimeter detection, sources 2, 3, and 4 clearly dominate the emission at 24 \ and line up with the centroid peaks of the submillimeter maps. Sources 2 and 3 are likely double images of the lensed background LIRG Wilson2008,Gonzalez2009 , while Source 4 is a spectroscopically confirmed cluster member Mehlert2001 . We estimate the submillimeter flux densities of each of the lensed high-redshift millimeter source detected by AzTEC and the foreground dominant cluster member galaxy by simultaneously fitting the PSF to the BLAST maps at the positions of both sources using the maximum-likelihood algorithm presented in Scott2002 .

The submillimeter photometry of the LIRG and the bright cluster member galaxy are given in Table table:data and Table table:data2 respectively. The measured 500 \ emission is dominated by an association with the millimeter AzTEC source. However, our estimates of the shorter wavelength contributions to the flux densities at 250 and 350 \ favor the cluster member galaxy as the dominant source of emission.

gmw_00006060.name: NGC4733
gmw_00006060.overview: NGC 4733 is a low-luminosity elliptical galaxy located in the Virgo cluster [arXiv:astro-ph/0102374]. It is a barred, early-type galaxy viewed at a very low inclination that contains two stellar components with opposite angular momenta [arXiv:1102.3801].

sources.summary: The paper highlights NGC4733 as a low-inclination barred early-type with apparent non-regular low-velocity rotation, significant misalignment, and evidence for two components with opposite angular momenta.

quotes.quote: There are three exceptions of which one deserves special attention: a barred NRR/LV (NGC4733), which is seen at very low inclination and, hence, likely an object with intrinsic rotation.
NGC4733, also in a less densely populated region, was mentioned above as a barred galaxy.
The latter study also showed that NGC4733, a 2 galaxy which does not show evidence of a counter-rotation on the velocity map also consists of two components with opposite angular momenta.

sources.summary: The paper describes NGC 4733 as a low-luminosity Virgo elliptical with only 28 globular-cluster candidates and too few clusters for firm bimodality conclusions.

quotes.quote: NGC 4733 This galaxy is a low luminosity Virgo elliptical. We detect only 28 GC candidates, too few to draw conclusions about bimodality although most clusters appear to belong to a blue peak.

The colors of both red and blue GCs are correlated with host galaxy M_B and central velocity dispersion ( _0 ) with a >90 probability, while trends with the Mg2 index and host galaxy colors are only weak and, in the case of Mg2, mostly driven by the poor GC system of NGC 4733.

gmw_00006061.name: NGC 3099
gmw_00006061.overview: NGC 3099 is classified as an elliptical galaxy [arXiv:1412.6556]. It is identified as a Type-I shell galaxy candidate with previously unreported shell structures [arXiv:1412.6556].

sources.summary: The paper treats NGC 3099 as a Type-I shell galaxy candidate, using SDSS/Galfit photometry and visual shell counts to estimate MOND asymptotic circular and shell-expansion velocities.

quotes.quote: In this section, we roughly estimate the asymptotic circular and shell expansion velocities for several real Type-I shell galaxies assuming MOND gravity. The shells in galaxies NGC 3923 (e.g., prieur88 ) and NGC 7600 (e.g., turnbull ) have been known for a long time. In spite of their prominence, the shells in NGC 410 and NGC 3099 have not been reported yet. For obtaining the surface density profile of NGC 3923, we fitted the profile to its near-infrared image taken by the Spitzer space telescope. We used the same data, M/L ratio, and the galaxy distance from Earth as in bil13 .

For NGC 7600, NGC 410, and NGC 3099, we started from their SDSS (Sloan Digital Sky Survey, see, e.g., sdssdr10 ) images in the g , , and i bands. We fitted each of them by the one-component profile in Galfit Penggalfit . The integrated magnitudes in the g , , and i bands, and the corresponding color indices were determined from these data. The approximate M/L ratios were obtained from the color indices using the tables of sdssml . We assumed a constant M/L across the whole volume of the galaxies.

gmw_00006062.name: NGC 7600
gmw_00006062.overview: NGC 7600 is a nearby elliptical galaxy [arXiv:1111.2864] classified as a Type-I shell galaxy [arXiv:1412.6556]. Its halo features a complex system of concentric shells, which are interpreted as the remnant of a merger or accretion event [arXiv:1111.2864].

sources.summary: The paper uses NGC 7600 as a known Type-I shell galaxy, deriving SDSS/Galfit photometry and adopting shell radii to estimate MOND velocity scalings.

quotes.quote: In this section, we roughly estimate the asymptotic circular and shell expansion velocities for several real Type-I shell galaxies assuming MOND gravity. The shells in galaxies NGC 3923 (e.g., prieur88 ) and NGC 7600 (e.g., turnbull ) have been known for a long time. In spite of their prominence, the shells in NGC 410 and NGC 3099 have not been reported yet. For obtaining the surface density profile of NGC 3923, we fitted the profile to its near-infrared image taken by the Spitzer space telescope. We used the same data, M/L ratio, and the galaxy distance from Earth as in bil13 .

For NGC 7600, NGC 410, and NGC 3099, we started from their SDSS (Sloan Digital Sky Survey, see, e.g., sdssdr10 ) images in the g , , and i bands. We fitted each of them by the one-component profile in Galfit Penggalfit . The integrated magnitudes in the g , , and i bands, and the corresponding color indices were determined from these data. The approximate M/L ratios were obtained from the color indices using the tables of sdssml . We assumed a constant M/L across the whole volume of the galaxies.

sources.summary: The paper presents deep observations of NGC 7600 shells and interprets its halo features as a CDM-like accretion or merger remnant resembling simulated shell formation.

quotes.quote: We present new deep observations of `shell' structures in the halo of the nearby elliptical galaxy NGC 7600, alongside a movie of galaxy formation in a cold dark matter universe ( http://www.virgo.dur.ac.uk/shell-galaxies ).
The disruption of a massive clump on a near-radial orbit creates a complex system of transient concentric shells which bare a striking resemblance to those of NGC 7600.
With the aid of the simulation we interpret NGC 7600 in the context of the CDM model.

gmw_00006063.name: NGC 1389
gmw_00006063.overview: NGC 1389 is a luminous barred lenticular galaxy in the Fornax cluster [arXiv:1906.08187] that hosts a massive, metal-rich nuclear star cluster [arXiv:2104.06412]. The galaxy contains a nuclear disc-like structure [arXiv:1906.08187] and exhibits bright K-band surface-brightness fluctuations, which may indicate recent metal-enriched star formation [arXiv:0806.2658, arXiv:astro-ph/0107357].

sources.summary: The paper treats NGC 1389 as an outlier with K-band surface-brightness fluctuations too bright for its UV color, possibly reflecting metallicity, recent star formation, or TP-AGB lifetime effects.

quotes.quote: Note the outlying cases of NGC 1387, NGC 4552, and NGC 1389 with an infrared effective magnitude that is 0.7 mag too bright for their UV excess. The relevant case of the merger radio-galaxy NGC 1316 (Fornax A) is also singled out in the plot, where the arrow indicates that a ``bluer'' (1550- V ) color might be more appropriate for this galaxy (with a more negligible impact on K _s , though), as a consequence of a strong internal absorption due to the observed presence of dust lanes. Excluding these controversial objects (discussed in more detail in Sec. 4), the data sample correlates fairly well ( = -0.75 , see arrow), indicating a less deployed AGB for UV-enhanced galaxies.

If galaxy mass ( alias _v ) is considered as the leading physical parameter to compare outlier location with respect to the bulk of the galaxy distribution (see, for instance Fig. 7), one must conclude that both NGC 4552 and NGC 1389 seem to have a brighter AGB tip rather than a bluer (1550-V) color. With regard to NGC 1389, liu02 find that the K_s SBFs of NGC 1389 are also too bright compared to its ( V - I ) color, a fact that would be consistent with either a higher than average metallicity given the age of its most recent burst of star formation or a longer lifetime of its TP-AGB stars mouhcine05 .

On the other hand, NGC 1387 and NGC 1389 are lenticular galaxies, like the merger remnant NGC 1316, but so are NGC 3384 and NGC 4406, both of which do not deviate from the correlation. The other galaxies in our sample are all genuine ellipticals rc3 , except for NGC 224 ( alias M 31), which is so close, however, that bulge SBFs can be measured without any significant contamination from the disk. At any rate, NGC 1387 and NGC 1389 constitute privileged candidates for any future ``in-depth'' investigation.

sources.summary: FCC 193 is described as a luminous barred lenticular ancient infaller, where the bar affects photometric and kinematic axes and a nuclear disc-like structure has lower dispersion.

quotes.quote: FCC 193 (NGC 1389) -- Fig. fig:FCC193map FCC 193 is one the most luminous barred lenticulars inside the virial radius of the Fornax cluster. It is located at R_ proj =0.4^ from FCC 213 (NGC 1399) on the south-western cluster side. It is an ancient infaller which belongs to the north-south clump within the high-density region of the cluster. As in FCC 190, the presence of the bar for R 30 arcsec is evident in the difference of 20^ between PA_ kin and PA_ phot .

The stellar velocity map shows a nuclear disc-like structure ( R 5 arcsec) with a maximum rotation velocity v_ max 100 km s ^ 1 . This feature is aligned with the major axis of the outer disc and is characterised by a smaller velocity dispersion ( 90 km s ^ 1 ) with respect to the regions of the bar/outer disc.

sources.summary: The paper places FCC 193 among the most massive sample galaxies, with a very massive, old, metal-rich nuclear star cluster interpreted through rapid early central enrichment and efficient formation.

quotes.quote: Most massive galaxies The most massive galaxies in our sample ( M_ gal > 10^ 10 M_ ), FCC 47, FCC 170, and FCC 193, also host the most massive NSCs ( > 10^8 M_ ). Their NSCs are significantly more metal-rich than the hosts and any GCs, and are older than the host galaxies. This could be explained by an inside-out formation scenario, where the central regions of the galaxies assembled first and were rapidly enriched due to efficient star formation, while enrichment in the outskirts was slower and less efficient.

In , we argued that the old age and high metallicity of the NSC in FCC 47 indicates an efficient formation at early times. Similar arguments were made in the detailed analysis of FCC 170 by using the same F3D MUSE data which showed that FCC 170 assembled very rapidly and efficiently at early times (see also Poci2021 ). Their SFH of the central region of FCC 170 is similar to the one presented here.

sources.summary: The paper reports a poor power-spectrum fit for NGC 1389, excludes it as a very bright K-band SBF outlier, and links such bright fluctuations to recent metal-enriched star formation.

quotes.quote: For NGC 1389, the quality of the power spectrum fit appears to be somewhat poor. The S/N in the PSF used for the fit was good, so potentially the problem arises from a mismatched PSF. However, with only one PSF available in the field, we cannot directly determine if this was the origin of the problem.
This resulted in the exclusion of NGC 1389 and 1419, both of which have very bright band fluctuations ( 6.6 mag); these galaxies are discussed in the next section.
In addition, the modeling suggests that the galaxies with unusually bright band SBFs, such as NGC 1389 and 1419, result from recent star formation involving gas which was enriched in metals.

sources.summary: The paper includes NGC 1389 among the Fornax ellipticals needing light profiles and identifies Nova 2 as clearly associated with that galaxy near its half-light radius.

quotes.quote: This area includes the central dominant (cD) galaxy NGC 1399 and three other elliptical galaxies: NGC 1404, NGC 1387, and NGC 1389.
To answer these questions, we required the light profiles of the four galaxies in our survey region: NGC 1399, NGC 1404, NGC 1389, and NGC 1387.
We also see that Nova 2 is clearly associated with NGC 1389: it is close to the half-light radius, but the association of the other novae is not straightforward.

gmw_00006064.name: U4-16795
gmw_00006064.overview: U4-16795 is a massive star-forming galaxy at a redshift of z=2.53 [arXiv:1703.10197]. It features a compact, dusty star-forming core with rapidly rotating molecular gas and a low gas-to-dynamical mass fraction [arXiv:1703.10197].

sources.summary: The paper presents U4-16795 as one of two z=2.53 massive star-forming galaxies with a compact dusty core, high stellar mass, measured SFR, low gas fraction, and rotation-dominated molecular gas.

quotes.quote: We select two galaxies at z=2.53 (U4-16795 and U4-16504) from Subaru narrow-band imaging in the SXDF field 2013ApJ...778..114T . The narrow-band based redshift has uncertainties of z 0.02 . The two galaxies are located within the primary beam of ALMA Band-3 receivers (a beam width at half power of 1 ) as the projected separation is 9 7. Both galaxies have a compact dusty star-forming core, which is probed by 870 m dust continuum emission 2017ApJ...834..135T . We compute the stellar mass using the FAST spectral energy distribution fitting code 2009ApJ...700..221K and the 3D-HST multi-wavelength photometric catalog 2014ApJS..214...24S using the stellar population synthesis models of 2003MNRAS.344.1000B , exponentially declining star formation histories, and dust attenuation law of 2000ApJ...533..682C . The total stellar mass is (M_*/M_ )=11.26 0.15 for U4-16795 and (M_*/M_ )=11.25 0.15 for U4-16504.

U4-16795 is detected in a deep Herschel PACS 160 m map from archival data (see 2011A&A...532A..90L for the methodology) and U4-16504 is detected in a deep Spitzer MIPS 24 m map (PI: J. Dunlop). Following the recipes of 2011ApJ...738..106W , we derive SFRs of (SFR/ M_ yr ^ 1 )=2.62 0.1 for U4-16795 and (SFR/ M_ yr ^ 1 )=2.37 0.25 for U4-16504 from a combination of the rest-frame 2800 and infrared luminosities. The targets are located on the massive end of the star-forming main sequence at z 2 e.g., 2014ApJS..214...15S .

Adopting the starburst conversion factor to derive the CO-based gas mass, we find the gas-to-dynamical mass fraction to be 15^ +4 _ 5 for U4-16795 and 12^ +4 _ 5 for U4-16504 and the gas depletion timescale to be M_ gas /SFR=46 11 Myr and 79 46 Myr, respectively. When the uncertainties of the conversion factor are taken into consideration, those in the gas mass estimate become larger. Using ALMA observations of CO J=3-2 emission, we find that the compact molecular gas in the star-forming cores of two massive galaxies is rapidly rotating. This has implications for both the formation and subsequent evolution of the cores of massive galaxies. The formation mechanism appears to be dissipative and the observed rotation indicates that at least some angular momentum is preserved in the star-forming molecular gas. Simulations show this could happen due to a gas-rich merger or disk instabilities e.g., 2015MNRAS.450.2327Z,2015MNRAS.449..361W . The two massive star-forming galaxies are both rotation-dominated with v/ =7.0^ +2.5 _ 2.8 and v/ =4.1^ +1.7 _ 1.5 .

gmw_00006065.name: U4-16504
gmw_00006065.overview: U4-16504 is a massive star-forming galaxy at redshift z=2.53 [arXiv:1703.10197]. It features a compact dusty core [arXiv:1703.10197] and contains rapidly rotating molecular gas with a low gas-to-dynamical mass fraction [arXiv:1703.10197].

sources.summary: The paper presents U4-16504 as one of two z=2.53 massive star-forming galaxies with a compact dusty core, high stellar mass, measured SFR, low gas fraction, and rotation-dominated molecular gas.

quotes.quote: We select two galaxies at z=2.53 (U4-16795 and U4-16504) from Subaru narrow-band imaging in the SXDF field 2013ApJ...778..114T . The narrow-band based redshift has uncertainties of z 0.02 . The two galaxies are located within the primary beam of ALMA Band-3 receivers (a beam width at half power of 1 ) as the projected separation is 9 7. Both galaxies have a compact dusty star-forming core, which is probed by 870 m dust continuum emission 2017ApJ...834..135T . We compute the stellar mass using the FAST spectral energy distribution fitting code 2009ApJ...700..221K and the 3D-HST multi-wavelength photometric catalog 2014ApJS..214...24S using the stellar population synthesis models of 2003MNRAS.344.1000B , exponentially declining star formation histories, and dust attenuation law of 2000ApJ...533..682C . The total stellar mass is (M_*/M_ )=11.26 0.15 for U4-16795 and (M_*/M_ )=11.25 0.15 for U4-16504.

U4-16795 is detected in a deep Herschel PACS 160 m map from archival data (see 2011A&A...532A..90L for the methodology) and U4-16504 is detected in a deep Spitzer MIPS 24 m map (PI: J. Dunlop). Following the recipes of 2011ApJ...738..106W , we derive SFRs of (SFR/ M_ yr ^ 1 )=2.62 0.1 for U4-16795 and (SFR/ M_ yr ^ 1 )=2.37 0.25 for U4-16504 from a combination of the rest-frame 2800 and infrared luminosities. The targets are located on the massive end of the star-forming main sequence at z 2 e.g., 2014ApJS..214...15S .

Adopting the starburst conversion factor to derive the CO-based gas mass, we find the gas-to-dynamical mass fraction to be 15^ +4 _ 5 for U4-16795 and 12^ +4 _ 5 for U4-16504 and the gas depletion timescale to be M_ gas /SFR=46 11 Myr and 79 46 Myr, respectively. When the uncertainties of the conversion factor are taken into consideration, those in the gas mass estimate become larger. Using ALMA observations of CO J=3-2 emission, we find that the compact molecular gas in the star-forming cores of two massive galaxies is rapidly rotating. This has implications for both the formation and subsequent evolution of the cores of massive galaxies. The formation mechanism appears to be dissipative and the observed rotation indicates that at least some angular momentum is preserved in the star-forming molecular gas. Simulations show this could happen due to a gas-rich merger or disk instabilities e.g., 2015MNRAS.450.2327Z,2015MNRAS.449..361W . The two massive star-forming galaxies are both rotation-dominated with v/ =7.0^ +2.5 _ 2.8 and v/ =4.1^ +1.7 _ 1.5 .

gmw_00006066.name: SDSS J1051+4017
gmw_00006066.overview: SDSS J1051+4017 is a broad-absorption line quasar [arXiv:1009.3928]. Individual spectral fitting of the source reveals a well-measured X-ray spectrum with a photon index near 2 [arXiv:1009.3928].

sources.summary: The paper individually fits SDSS J1051+4017, finding a well-measured X-ray spectrum, a photon index near 2, little change after intrinsic absorption is added, and no bias to the joint fit.

quotes.quote: We only did spectral fitting individually for one source (SDSS J1051+4017) which has 210 counts. Excluding this source in the joint fitting does not significantly change the best-fit parameters, which indicates this source does not introduce biases into the joint fitting.

For the individually fitted source, SDSS J1051+4017, the best-fit photon index is =1.99^ +0.21 _ 0.21 without adding intrinsic absorption. This value is consistent with that from band-ratio analysis (see Table 3). After adding the intrinsic absorption component, the photon index changes insignificantly to =2.06^ +0.37 _ 0.26 . The power-law becomes slightly steeper.

gmw_00006067.name: FGCE 0745
gmw_00006067.overview: FGCE 0745 is an edge-on spiral galaxy [arXiv:1406.3574] that acts as a nearby, gas-rich satellite to NGC 2865 [arXiv:1406.3574]. The galaxy contains substantial neutral hydrogen gas [arXiv:1406.3574] and hosts nineteen detected H-alpha emitters [arXiv:1406.3574].

sources.summary: The paper identifies FGCE 0745 as NGC 2865's nearby gas-rich satellite, detects many H-alpha emitters in it, estimates its metallicity, and describes it as an edge-on spiral with substantial H I.

quotes.quote: Out of the 26 H emitters, 19 are in the satellite galaxy FGCE 0745 and seven are intergalactic H II regions scattered over the south tail of the H I gas around NGC 2865. We found that the intergalactic H II regions are young ( < 200 Myr) with stellar masses in the range 4 10 ^3 M _ to 17 10 ^6 M _ . These are found in a region of low H I gas density, where the probability of forming stars is expected to be low. For one of the intergalactic H II regions we estimated a solar oxygen abundance of 12 + log(O/H) 8.7. We also were able to estimate the metallicity for the satellite galaxy FGCE 0745 to be 12 + log(O/H) 8.0.
Continuum sources with line emission. These are spectra from the satellite galaxy FGCE 0745, with an average radial velocity of 2878 km s ^ 1 .
schiminovich95 also detected the presence of a gas-rich satellite galaxy, FGCE 0745, located 6 \ to the southeast of NGC 2865. It is an edge-on spiral galaxy containing 4.4 10 ^8 M _ of H I .

gmw_00006068.name: NGC 3511
gmw_00006068.overview: NGC 3511 is a highly inclined nearby disk galaxy [arXiv:2306.13012]. It is located in close proximity to the companion galaxy NGC 3513 and hosts an extraplanar, thick gaseous disk [arXiv:2306.13012].

sources.summary: The paper studies NGC 3511 as an inclined nearby disk galaxy whose broad ionized-gas component indicates an extraplanar thick gaseous disk supported partly by turbulence.

quotes.quote: NGC 3511 is a nearby disk galaxy ( z 0.0037 ; Koribalski2004 ) that is highly inclined with an inclination angle of i = 74.5^ ( Makarov2014 ). It is in close proximity to the companion galaxy NGC 3513, with a projected separation of d_ proj = 50 kpc. NGC 3513 is a nearly face-on ( i = 39.0^ ) SBc spiral at z 0.00398 Kaldare2003 .

We show that in NGC 3511, the broad component has three distinctive characteristics in comparison to the narrow component: (1) elevated [ N II ] 6583/H and [ S II ] 6716/H line ratios, (2) significantly higher velocity dispersions (a median _ Broad = 24 compared to _ Narrow = 13 ), and (3) a rotational velocity lag. Together, these characteristics support an origin in an extraplanar, thick gaseous disk.

Based on measurements of the eDIG velocity dispersion in NGC 3511, we demonstrate that turbulent motions increase the scale height by at least a factor of a few above the thermal scale height, with h_ z 0.2 - 0.4 kpc at R = 3 - 5 kpc.

gmw_00006069.name: NGC 3513
gmw_00006069.overview: NGC 3513 is a nearly face-on SBc spiral galaxy with a stellar mass of $10^{9.2}\ M_{\odot}$ [arXiv:2306.13012]. It is a lower-mass companion to NGC 3511, exhibiting a star formation rate of $0.2\ M_{\odot}\text{ yr}^{-1}$ and localized outflows from its galactic disk [arXiv:2306.13012].

sources.summary: The paper treats NGC 3513 as NGC 3511's lower-mass companion, finding broad emission consistent with localized outflows from its galactic disk.

quotes.quote: It is in close proximity to the companion galaxy NGC 3513, with a projected separation of d_ proj = 50 kpc. NGC 3513 is a nearly face-on ( i = 39.0^ ) SBc spiral at z 0.00398 Kaldare2003 . NGC 3511 and NGC 3513 have star formation rates (SFRs) derived jointly from the far-ultraviolet (FUV) and mid-infrared (MIR) of SFR = 0.8 M_ yr^ 1 and SFR = 0.2 M_ yr^ 1 , respectively Leroy2019, Leroy2021 . NGC 3511 and NGC 3513 have stellar masses of = 10^ 10.0 M_ and = 10^ 9.2 M_ , respectively, with a mass ratio of 6:1 Leroy2019, Leroy2021 .

In NGC 3513, the broad component is consistent with localized outflows making their way out of the galactic disk. Our findings demonstrate that gas circulation at the disk-halo interface is present in both galaxies.

NGC 3511 has S_ 60 / S_ 100 = -0.41 and L_ FIR / D_ 25 ^2 = 0.70 ; NGC 3513 has S_ 60 / S_ 100 = -0.37 and L_ FIR / D_ 25 ^2 = 0.94 , where the latter quantity inside the log has units of 10^ 40 erg s ^ 1 kpc ^ 2 .

gmw_00006070.name: SDSS J120753.12+150221.2
gmw_00006070.overview: SDSS J120753.12+150221.2 is an X-ray-bright Seyfert 1 galaxy located at a redshift of z=0.08558 [arXiv:2110.06939]. Its X-ray spectrum features a soft excess and a continuum characterized by a power-law plus blackbody component [arXiv:2110.06939].

sources.summary: The paper identifies SDSS J120753.12+150221.2 as an X-ray-bright Seyfert 1 with a soft excess, a power-law plus blackbody continuum, and measured 2-10 keV flux and luminosity.

quotes.quote: SDSS J120753.12+150221.2. This source is an X-ray bright Seyfert 1 galaxy at redshift z=0.08558 ( oh2015 ). Its X-ray spectrum is characterised by a soft excess with R_ S/P =0.16 . The broadband ( E=0.3-10 keV observer frame) continuum is well described by a power law ( =2.06 0.06 ) modified by Galactic absorption, plus a blackbody component ( kT = 120_ 20 ^ +10 eV). In the 2-10 keV energy interval, we measure a flux F_ 2-10 10^ 12 erg cm ^ 2 s ^ 1 and a luminosity L_ 2-10 1.9 10^ 43 erg s ^ 1 .

gmw_00006071.name: 2MASX J12071559+1512158
gmw_00006071.overview: 2MASX J12071559+1512158 is a Seyfert 1 galaxy located at a redshift of z=0.10701 [arXiv:2110.06939]. Its soft X-ray spectrum exhibits absorption below 1 keV rest frame, which is modeled with a photoelectric absorber [arXiv:2110.06939].

sources.summary: The paper classifies 2MASX J12071559+1512158 as a Seyfert 1 whose soft X-ray spectrum requires absorption, then reports its fitted continuum, absorber, flux, and luminosity.

quotes.quote: 2MASX J12071559+1512158. This source is classified as a Seyfert 1 galaxy ( toba2014 ) at redshift z=0.10701 . Its soft X-ray spectrum is characterised by absorption below 1 keV rest frame. A good fit of the broadband continuum consists of a power law component with = 1.8 0.2 . The absorption feature in the soft X-rays is modelled with a photoeletric absorber ( zwabs component in XSPEC) with a column density of N_ H = (0.7 0.2) 10^ 22 cm ^ 2 .
The source has a flux F_ 2-10 5.7 10^ 13 erg cm ^ 2 s ^ 1 and a luminosity L_ 2-10 1.6 10^ 43 erg s ^ 1 .

gmw_00006072.name: SDSS J094012.29+461850.3
gmw_00006072.overview: SDSS J094012.29+461850.3 is a quasar located at a redshift of z=0.7112 [arXiv:2110.06939]. Its X-ray broadband continuum is characterized by a partial-covering cold absorber [arXiv:2110.06939].

sources.summary: The paper treats SDSS J094012.29+461850.3 as a QSO whose X-ray continuum needs a partial-covering cold absorber, and reports its covering factor, column density, flux, and luminosity.

quotes.quote: SDSS J094012.29+461850.3. This QSO is located at redshift z=0.7112 ( paris2014 ). To fit the X-ray broadband continuum, we first considered a simple model consisting of a power law modified by Galactic absorption. However, such a model resulted in a poor fit of the underlying continuum. We suspected the discrepancy could arise from the deficit below 2 keV rest frame. So we included a partial covering, cold absorption component in the model.

The covering factor and column density measured with XMM-Newton are C_ f =0.7_ 0.2 ^ +0.1 and N_ H = 1.7_ 1.1 ^ +1.5 10^ 22 cm ^ 2 , respectively. The primary continuum is described by a power law with photon index = 1.4 0.3 . We estimate a flux F_ 2-10 7.1 10^ 14 erg cm ^ 2 s ^ 1 and a luminosity L_ 2-10 1.2 10^ 44 erg s ^ 1 .

gmw_00006073.name: SDSS J120558.16+412825.3
gmw_00006073.overview: SDSS J120558.16+412825.3 is a Seyfert 1 galaxy located at a redshift of z=0.22648 [arXiv:2110.06939]. Its X-ray spectrum lacks a soft excess and is well-fit by a power law modified by Galactic absorption [arXiv:2110.06939].

sources.summary: The paper describes SDSS J120558.16+412825.3 as a Seyfert 1 whose XMM-Newton spectrum lacks a required soft excess and is fit by a Galactic-absorbed power law.

quotes.quote: SDSS J120558.16+412825.3. This source is a Seyfert 1 galaxy at redshift z=0.22648 ( toba2014 ). The XMM-Newton data do not require the presence of a soft excess and a model consisting of a power law, modified by Galactic absorption, with photon index = 1.8 0.2 provides a best fit to the X-ray spectrum. We estimate a flux F_ 2-10 1.2 10^ 13 erg cm ^ 2 s ^ 1 , which implies a luminosity L_ 2-10 1.8 10^ 43 erg s ^ 1 . The net pn/MOS1/MOS2 photon counts in the broadband E=0.3-10 keV observer-frame energy interval are 121/96/53.

gmw_00006074.name: NVSS J231604-202748
gmw_00006074.overview: NVSS J231604-202748 is the brightest cluster member of Abell 2566 [arXiv:2405.10475]. It is currently interacting with a neighboring cluster galaxy, SDSS J231605.02-202748.4, in a likely minor merger [arXiv:2405.10475].

sources.summary: The paper identifies NVSS J231604-202748 as Abell 2566's brightest cluster member, interacting with a neighboring system and offset from the X-ray peak in a likely minor merger.

quotes.quote: This image clearly reveals interaction of the brightest cluster member ``BCG'' (NVSS J231604-202748; 2MASS J23160504-2027485) with a neighboring system ``S'' (SDSS J231605.02-202748.4). The two objects have a physical separation of 4.7 arcsec ( 7 kpc), while the X-ray peak of the cluster (denoted by cross mark) is about 4.6 arcsec ( 6.8 kpc) north-west of the BCG. Photometric redshift of the source S is 0.062581 2022ApJS..259...35A , while that of the BCG is 0.08247, implying that the source ``S'' is indeed a cluster member.

This study also witness an offset of 4.6 (6.8 kpc) between the BCG and the X-ray peak, and interaction of the BCG with a sub-system in the central region, pointing towards the origin of the spiral structure due to a minor merger.

sources.summary: The paper describes SDSS J231605.02-202748.4 as a neighboring system interacting with Abell 2566's BCG, separated by about 7 kpc and photometrically consistent with cluster membership.

quotes.quote: This image clearly reveals interaction of the brightest cluster member ``BCG'' (NVSS J231604-202748; 2MASS J23160504-2027485) with a neighboring system ``S'' (SDSS J231605.02-202748.4). The two objects have a physical separation of 4.7 arcsec ( 7 kpc), while the X-ray peak of the cluster (denoted by cross mark) is about 4.6 arcsec ( 6.8 kpc) north-west of the BCG. Photometric redshift of the source S is 0.062581 2022ApJS..259...35A , while that of the BCG is 0.08247, implying that the source ``S'' is indeed a cluster member.

This study also witness an offset of 4.6 (6.8 kpc) between the BCG and the X-ray peak, and interaction of the BCG with a sub-system in the central region, pointing towards the origin of the spiral structure due to a minor merger.

gmw_00006075.name: ESO 338-IG004
gmw_00006075.overview: ESO 338-IG004 is a starburst galaxy that hosts a nuclear Lyman-alpha emitting source [arXiv:2001.07807]. It contains an unresolved, non-nuclear X-ray source that is spatially distinct from the nucleus and is considered a candidate intermediate-mass black hole [arXiv:2001.07807].

sources.summary: The paper reports ESO 338-IG004 as XMM-unresolved with a hard hardness ratio, but argues its non-nuclear X-ray source is unrelated to the nuclear Ly-alpha emitter.

quotes.quote: An additional 4 sources are detected only by XMM, which has much larger PSFs that cannot resolve point sources in these galaxies: ESO 338-IG004, LARS 07, and LARS 08 are unresolved, and only IRAS 08339 + 6517 is extended.

Similarly, ESO 338-IG004 has a hard HR, consistent with a candidate LLAGN (Table chartable ), but oskinova19 recently showed that this is a non-nuclear X-ray source, which is spatially unrelated to the nuclear LAE. They suggest that the object is an intermediate-mass black hole (IMBH) candidate.
In particular, we detect an X-ray source brighter than 10^ 40 \ that may be associated with the \ emission in each of the 12 \ sources, except for LARS 08, LARS 09, and ESO 338-IG004.

gmw_00006076.name: LARS 09
gmw_00006076.overview: LARS 09 is a luminous infrared galaxy [arXiv:1511.05406] with a highly irregular, disturbed morphology [arXiv:1511.05406, arXiv:1803.07207] and an edge-on orientation [arXiv:2110.01626]. It is a starbursting system [arXiv:1308.6578] undergoing an advanced merger [arXiv:1511.05406] that involves a closely interacting pair [arXiv:1511.05406] and a third companion galaxy [arXiv:1511.05406, arXiv:1803.07207].

sources.summary: The paper describes LARS 9 as a luminous infrared galaxy in an advanced merger, with irregular morphology, multiple ionized components, and Ly escape mainly in the southeastern tail.

quotes.quote: LARS 9 ( IRAS 08208+2816 ) This luminous infrared galaxy has highly irregular morphology. Numerous star forming knots are visible in H along two arms of large extend that are connected to central, very bright H nucleus. We note that at the end of the southern-tail a foreground star appears in projection; contributions from this star have been masked out in the PMAS data. In LARS 9 Ly is almost everywhere absorbed along the line of sight towards the star forming regions.
From our PMAS observation we conclude that LARS 9 is a closely interacting pair of galaxies in an advanced stage of merging. The interaction scenario is also supported by our observations.
These observations therefore strongly suggests that a third system is significantly involved in the interaction.

sources.summary: The paper singles out LARS09 as an edge-on galaxy whose Ly-alpha morphology gives an extremely large fitted circular halo scale length but a more reasonable isophotal scale length.

quotes.quote: Finally, LARS09 is excluded since its fitted r_ sc ^ Ly is huge (due to its Ly morphology, with bright emission in the north and south of the centre and little sign of decreasing SB at large radii, see Figs. paper_example_lya_fuv_radial and morph_paper_example ). The bin size of the circular annuli used for the SB profiles will certainly affect the S/N of individual data points, but using larger bins does not change this selection. Removing all these galaxies from the initial 45 galaxies in our sample, brings us to 28 galaxies that are explored in this study.

For example, in the extreme case of the edge-on galaxy LARS09, where the measured r_ sc ^ Ly is too large ( 60 kpc, due to the morphology of the galaxy), it has a more reasonable measured r_ sc ^ iso (2.8 kpc).

LARS09 is here marked as an excluded galaxy, even though it has a bright enough halo to get a profile fit (see Section rsc_result_sec ). Because it has an extremely large fitted scale length, the over-subtraction uncertainty becomes very large ( 70 ).

sources.summary: The paper reports J082355.0 + 280621.8 as a spatially extended Lyman-break analog with extended X-ray emission, low nuclear contribution, and multiple luminous ULX candidates.

quotes.quote: We typically extracted counts within a 5 aperture to sufficiently include the extent of the optical emission, except in the case of J082355.0 + 280621.8 where we used a 10 20 (radius) elliptical aperture to account for the larger extent of emission in this galaxy (see third panel in Figure fig:images ).

Benefitting from the high angular resolution capability of , three of the six LBAs in our sample, VV 114, Haro 11, and J082355.0 + 280621.8 appear to have extended X-ray emission (see Figure fig:LBA3 ), and we can distinguish multiple X-ray point sources, all of which are luminous enough to qualify as ULXs ( >10^ 40 ). The nuclear regions, potentially associated with AGN, contribute only < 15 to the 2--10 keV emission in each case, immediately indicating that the elevated /SFR for those LBAs cannot be attributed to AGN activity.

In Figure fig:LBA3 , the images of extended X-ray emission in three LBAs (VV 114, Haro 11 and J082355.0 + 280621.8) display multiple off-nuclear point sources with 10^ 40 , presumed to be ULXs, associated with each galaxy. Based on their SFRs and the analysis of HMXBs in star-forming galaxies by , we would have expected fewer ULXs with >10^ 40 in these sources than what we observe (five, three and three off-nuclear sources with >10^ 40 in VV 114, Haro 11and J082355.0 + 280621.8, respectively).

sources.summary: LARS 09/IRAS 0820+2816 is presented as an extended late-type starbursting spiral and radio source, with nuclear absorption and faint emission that traces optical structure rather than a halo.

quotes.quote: LARS 09 IRAS 0820+2816 is a very extended late-type starbursting spiral. It is also an NVSS and 5C radio source. It emits , but at the brightest UV levels is very faint, showing mostly \ absorption in the nucleus. A fuzz of \ emission is clearly visible but does not behave as an extended halo, and instead broadly traces out the optical structure. Its \ curve-of-growth is remarkable, showing \ in absorption in the central regions, that gets stronger out to radii of 3 kpc.

sources.summary: LARS09 has a complex disturbed morphology with multiple star-forming regions and a western companion-like object whose colors and H I data indicate interaction.

quotes.quote: LARS09 The bright star in the vicinity of LARS09 was masked out before any of the profiles were obtained. A complex, disturbed morphology with several SF regions is revealed by all analysis tools. The three contours in panel D of Fig. fig:lars09 are at 24.8 , 25.8 , and 26.8 mag/arcsec ^2 . The faintest contour engulfs a second galaxy to the west of LARS09. The RGB image shows that this galaxy has similar colors in UBI to LARS09, and is therefore unlikely to be a background galaxy. Indeed, H I data shows that this object is interacting with LARS09 (Cannon et al., in prep).

sources.summary: The paper highlights LARS 09 as an exception: its nuclear X-ray source is unrelated to Ly-alpha emission in the southern outskirts, despite possibly being unresolved.

quotes.quote: We see that spatially resolved observations are essential, since in at least one galaxy, LARS 09, the \ and X-ray emission are completely unrelated (Figure f_img2 ): the X-ray source is in the nucleus, while the \ is in the southern outskirts of the galaxy.
One exception is LARS 09, which has a nuclear X-ray source while the \ source is at large galactocentric radius.
In particular, we detect an X-ray source brighter than 10^ 40 \ that may be associated with the \ emission in each of the 12 \ sources, except for LARS 08, LARS 09, and ESO 338-IG004.

gmw_00006077.name: PKS 0219-164
gmw_00006077.overview: PKS 0219-164 is a blazar classified as a BL Lacertae object [arXiv:1708.06157]. It exhibits stronger variability in gamma-ray emission than in radio emission, and its radio light curves show a strong quasi-periodic oscillation with a periodicity of approximately 270 days [arXiv:1708.06157].

sources.summary: The paper analyzes PKS 0219-164 radio and gamma-ray variability, reporting stronger gamma-ray variability, softer-when-brighter behavior, and a significant 270-day radio quasi-periodic oscillation.

quotes.quote: In this work, we explore the long-term variability properties of the blazar PKS 0219-164 in the radio and the ray regime, utilizing the OVRO 15 GHz and the /LAT observations from the period 2008--2017. We found that ray emission is more variable than the radio emission implying that ray emission possibly originated in more compact regions while the radio emission represented continuum emission from the large scale jets. Also, in ray the source exhibited spectral variability characterized by the softer-when-brighter trend, a less frequently observed feature in the high energy emission by BL Lacs.

In radio, using Lomb-Scargle periodogram and weighted wavelet z-transform, we detected a strong signal of quasi-periodic oscillation (QPO) with a periodicity of 270 26 days with possible harmonics of 550 42 and 1150 157 days periods. At a time when detections of QPOs in blazars are still under debate, the observed QPO with high statistical significance ( 97 - 99 global significance over underlying red-noise processes) and persistent over nearly 10 oscillations could make one of the strongest cases for the detection of QPOs in blazar light curves.

gmw_00006078.name: BRI 0952-0115
gmw_00006078.overview: BRI 0952-0115 is a lensed quasar located at a redshift of z = 4.4337 [arXiv:0904.3793]. Observations with the APEX telescope detected strong [CII] fine-structure line emission from this high-redshift source [arXiv:0904.3793].

sources.summary: The paper reports APEX detection of strong [CII] emission from lensed high-redshift galaxy BRI 0952-0115 and compares its enhanced [CII]/FIR ratio with local and high-redshift galaxies.

quotes.quote: We report the detection of the [ C II ] 157.74 m fine-structure line in the lensed galaxy BRI 0952-0115 at z=4.43, using the APEX telescope. This is the first detection of the [ C II ] line in a source with L_ FIR < 10^ 13 L_ at high redshift. The line is very strong compared to previous [ C II ] detections at high-z (a factor of 5--8 higher in flux), partly due to the lensing amplification.

The quasar BRI 0952-0115 at z=4.4337 is an optimal source for this test. This is a lensed quasar with a magnification factor in the range = 2.5-8 (see Appendix), whose mm and submm fluxes (fitted with a modified black body) indicate an intrinsic (de-magnified) far-IR luminosity L_ FIR 4 10^ 12 L_ corrected for our adopted cosmology priddey01 . This quasar has also been detected in the molecular transition CO(5--4) gui99 that provides an accurate determination of its redshift, which is required for the search of the [ CII ] within the narrow band offered by current submm receivers.

BRI 0952-0115 (RA(J2000) = 09:55:00.1, DEC(J2000) = 01:30:07.1) was observed with the Swedish Heterodyne Facility Instrument SHFI, vassilev08 on the APEX telescope in six observing runs, from November 1 ^ st to December 18 ^ th 2008 (a short run on October 23 ^ rd was totally discarded because of bad weather conditions), for a total of about 22 hours of observations, including sky observations and overheads, resulting in 5 hours of net on-source integration.

gmw_00006079.name: d0958+66
gmw_00006079.overview: The object d0958+66 is a blue compact dwarf galaxy located in the M81 group [arXiv:0805.1250, arXiv:1309.4130]. It contains young and intermediate-age stellar populations [arXiv:1309.4130], with active star formation indicated by Balmer absorption, H-alpha emission, and blue colors [arXiv:0805.1250, arXiv:1309.4130].

sources.summary: The paper presents d0958+66 as a blue compact dwarf candidate with group-supporting velocity, Balmer absorption, H emission, blue color, weak [NII], and an inferred metallicity upper limit of 0.3 solar.

quotes.quote: Three new M81 candidate member galaxies (d1028+70, d0958+66, d1012+64) are of the poorly understood blue compact dwarf class. Spectra of these galaxies were obtained with Subaru/FOCAS and BTA/SCORPIO. Radial velocities of +60 , 100 , and +150 km s ^ 1 support group membership for d0958+66, d1028+70 and d1012+64 respectively. All spectra exhibit strong Balmer absorption lines indicative of recent star formation. The BTA 6m spectra also find H emission in all three cases indicating active star formation, in agreement with the blue (V-I) 0.8 colors found for these galaxies.

However, we note that the [ NII ] 6584 line is quite weak in comparison to H , especially for d0958+66. We estimate an upper limit for the line ratio of 0.05 for this galaxy. From the relations of kd02 and pp04 , this leads to an upper limit for the metallicity of 0.3 Z _ .

sources.summary: The paper confirms d0958+66 as an M81-group blue compact dwarf with compact size, blue stars, young and intermediate-age populations, and HI detected by GMRT.

quotes.quote: BCDs: d1012+64, d0958+66, d1028+70 Three of the new objects were considered to be blue compact dwarf galaxies (BCDs) in paper 1: d0958+66, d1012+64, and d1028+70. All three are compact in size with effective radii 220-250 pc and contain significant numbers of blue stars. For the most part these galaxies are found towards the periphery of the 2nd turnaround radius of the group, as can be seen in the distribution maps in Figure 3d1 , although d0958+66 does lie closer to the core region with a physical separation from M81 of only 180 kpc.

All three host significant young populations in addition to well developed RGBs. Main sequence ( F606W-F814W < 0 ), blue loop, and red supergiant stars are visible indicating very recent or ongoing star formation. We find 24, 56, and 40 stars with colors bluer than F606W-F814W < 0.6 and magnitudes brighter than the TRGB, indicative of a young population, for d1012+64, d0958+66, and d1028+70, respectively, whereas all dSph and transition type objects exhibit no such stars in the CMD with the exception of d1014+68 which has 2.

gmw_00006080.name: d1012+64
gmw_00006080.overview: The galaxy d1012+64 is a blue compact dwarf candidate located in the M81 group [arXiv:0805.1250, arXiv:1309.4130]. It features a face-on neutral hydrogen disk [arXiv:1207.3023] and exhibits active star formation [arXiv:0805.1250, arXiv:1309.4130] with high-velocity gas indicating mechanical feedback [arXiv:1207.3023].

sources.summary: d1012+64 has a face-on HI disk and the clearest feedback signature in the sample, with high-velocity neutral gas near recent star formation.

quotes.quote: The only evidence we see of mechanical feedback form star formation on the HI distribution is in the galaxy d1012+64. Figure fig:chmap shows the HI channels maps for this galaxy at an intermediate resolution (chosen to optimise between localising the HI emission to a particular region and having enough signal-to-noise ratio to pick up the emission). The stellar disc, and the integrated velocity field at the same resolution (moment 1 map) are shown for reference. The velocity field shows a South-East to North alignment as one moves forward in the velocity space. The channels of interest are the last two ones shown in the Figure, with central heliocentric velocities of 211.5 and 214.7 .

Here the HI suddenly moves out of the South-East to North flow for increasing velocity, and returns to the central region of the HI disc of the galaxy. The noteworthy fact is that the HI emission in these two channels is right at the edge of the stellar disc, and overlapping with the emission coming from recent star formation (compare with Figure 3, 2b and 2c). To confirm that there is high velocity HI concentrated in only one region, we plot the HI spectrum for four different regions around the peak of HI emission in Figure fig:spreg .

sources.summary: The paper identifies UGC 5497 (d1012+64) as a blue compact dwarf candidate whose spectra, +150 km/s radial velocity, Balmer absorption, H emission, and blue color support M81 group membership and active star formation.

quotes.quote: The galaxy UGC 5497 (d1012+64) was observed on 20 October 2007 with an exposure time of 900 sec under 2.5^ seeing.

Three new M81 candidate member galaxies (d1028+70, d0958+66, d1012+64) are of the poorly understood blue compact dwarf class. Spectra of these galaxies were obtained with Subaru/FOCAS and BTA/SCORPIO. Radial velocities of +60 , 100 , and +150 km s ^ 1 support group membership for d0958+66, d1028+70 and d1012+64 respectively. All spectra exhibit strong Balmer absorption lines indicative of recent star formation. The BTA 6m spectra also find H emission in all three cases indicating active star formation, in agreement with the blue (V-I) 0.8 colors found for these galaxies.

sources.summary: The paper confirms d1012+64 as an M81-group blue compact dwarf, alias UGC 5497, with ACS imaging, young stars, an RGB, and low HI mass.

quotes.quote: BCDs: d1012+64, d0958+66, d1028+70 Three of the new objects were considered to be blue compact dwarf galaxies (BCDs) in paper 1: d0958+66, d1012+64, and d1028+70. All three are compact in size with effective radii 220-250 pc and contain significant numbers of blue stars. For the most part these galaxies are found towards the periphery of the 2nd turnaround radius of the group, as can be seen in the distribution maps in Figure 3d1 , although d0958+66 does lie closer to the core region with a physical separation from M81 of only 180 kpc.

All three host significant young populations in addition to well developed RGBs. Main sequence ( F606W-F814W < 0 ), blue loop, and red supergiant stars are visible indicating very recent or ongoing star formation. We find 24, 56, and 40 stars with colors bluer than F606W-F814W < 0.6 and magnitudes brighter than the TRGB, indicative of a young population, for d1012+64, d0958+66, and d1028+70, respectively, whereas all dSph and transition type objects exhibit no such stars in the CMD with the exception of d1014+68 which has 2.

gmw_00006081.name: d1028+70
gmw_00006081.overview: The galaxy d1028+70 is a blue compact dwarf located in the M81 group [arXiv:1309.4130, arXiv:0805.1250]. It features a slightly inclined neutral hydrogen disk [arXiv:1207.3023] and hosts a young stellar population alongside active, recent star formation [arXiv:1309.4130, arXiv:0805.1250].

sources.summary: The paper confirms d1028+70 as a compact M81-group blue compact dwarf with a young population, a developed RGB, recent star formation, Balmer absorption, and HI.

quotes.quote: BCDs: d1012+64, d0958+66, d1028+70 Three of the new objects were considered to be blue compact dwarf galaxies (BCDs) in paper 1: d0958+66, d1012+64, and d1028+70. All three are compact in size with effective radii 220-250 pc and contain significant numbers of blue stars. For the most part these galaxies are found towards the periphery of the 2nd turnaround radius of the group, as can be seen in the distribution maps in Figure 3d1 , although d0958+66 does lie closer to the core region with a physical separation from M81 of only 180 kpc.

All three host significant young populations in addition to well developed RGBs. Main sequence ( F606W-F814W < 0 ), blue loop, and red supergiant stars are visible indicating very recent or ongoing star formation. We find 24, 56, and 40 stars with colors bluer than F606W-F814W < 0.6 and magnitudes brighter than the TRGB, indicative of a young population, for d1012+64, d0958+66, and d1028+70, respectively, whereas all dSph and transition type objects exhibit no such stars in the CMD with the exception of d1014+68 which has 2.

sources.summary: The paper classifies d1028+70 as a blue compact dwarf candidate in the M81 group, with spectra, a +100 km/s radial velocity, Balmer absorption, H emission, and blue color indicating recent star formation.

quotes.quote: Three new M81 candidate member galaxies (d1028+70, d0958+66, d1012+64) are of the poorly understood blue compact dwarf class. Spectra of these galaxies were obtained with Subaru/FOCAS and BTA/SCORPIO. Radial velocities of +60 , 100 , and +150 km s ^ 1 support group membership for d0958+66, d1028+70 and d1012+64 respectively. All spectra exhibit strong Balmer absorption lines indicative of recent star formation. The BTA 6m spectra also find H emission in all three cases indicating active star formation, in agreement with the blue (V-I) 0.8 colors found for these galaxies.

sources.summary: d1028+70 is a low-mass M81-group BCD with a slightly inclined HI disk, offset FUV and emission peaks, and no clear HI feedback signature.

quotes.quote: The inclination angle estimated in such a manner is highly uncertain, and is only used here to quantify what is apparent from looking at the HI discs of the galaxies: d0958+66 and d1012+64 have very face-on HI discs and d1028+70 has a slightly inclined disc.

A few things are apparent from the figures: (i) although the star forming disc is at the central peak of the HI profile when the HI is imaged at a low resolution (Figure fig:uv5ov ), the peaks of FUV and emission don't concur with the peaks of the HI emission when HI is images at high resolution. (ii) There are offsets between the peaks of the \ emission and that of the FUV emission, these are most pronounced in d0958+66 and d1028+70. (iii) The stellar disc as appears in the RGB images is better traced by the FUV emission.

But even though our galaxies represent some of the smallest mass, actively star forming systems, evidence for feedback between the star formation on the neutral gas distribution in two of them (d0958+66 and d1028+70) is not clearly evident.

gmw_00006082.name: d0959+68
gmw_00006082.overview: Located along the tidal stream between M81 and NGC 3077, d0959+68 is a likely tidal dwarf galaxy [arXiv:0805.1250, arXiv:1309.4130]. Its stellar population is dominated by young, blue stars from a starburst that occurred 30 to 70 million years ago [arXiv:0805.1250, arXiv:1309.4130].

sources.summary: The paper identifies d0959+68 as a possible M81 tidal dwarf with recent 30-70 Myr star formation and a nearby HI cloud that may be displaced gas from its young stellar population.

quotes.quote: One of our candidate objects, d0959+68, was first noted by durrell in a search for intragroup stars in the field of the M81-NGC 3077 HI tidal tail. It has recently undergone a burst of star formation as evidenced by the large population of bright blue stars seen in Figure colim . durrell match isochrones to the CMD of this object and find evidence that star formation occurred 30-70 Myr ago, after the formation of the HI tidal arm.

One of these clouds is just offset from d0959+68, about 1^ .5 arcmin to the SW, several times the measured optical half-light radius. This HI cloudlet has a radial velocity distinct from both the Milky Way and M81 tidal arm. If it turns out the optical component has a similar radial velocity as the HI cloud, it would be likely that the two were associated. It may be possible that, like the Local Group Phoenix dwarf yswd07 , blow out from supernovae energetics from this young stellar population has displaced the neutral hydrogen gas.

sources.summary: The paper treats d0959+68 as a likely tidal dwarf or transition object near M81, with uncertain RGB-based distance and a CMD dominated by young stars.

quotes.quote: A third object, d0959+68, was originally noted by durrell . In a survey for resolved red giant stars near the core of the M81 Group, they discovered a concentration of blue stars which they suggested was a likely tidal dwarf within the group.

For d0959+68 the presence of an RGB is more questionable. Since all candidates were centered in the WFC chip 2, we show the CMDs for stars in the bottom detector for several cases in Figure ACSfldcmd . Hints of an RGB in d0959+68 may indicate that the target extends onto the other CCD.

The majority of stars associated with this object are blue, young, main sequence and blue loop stars.
