We obtain that the continuum level is uncertain of about 5 for CDFS-4417, and 10 for the combined spectrum, and that this uncertainty is fairly constant with wavelength.

Fig. 1501 and Fig. 789 show the comparison of the observed spectrum of CDFS-4417 (solid line) and the composite spectrum CDFS-comb (solid line) with the theoretical model (dotted line), computed for five different metallicities for the indices considered to compute the stellar metallicity. Among the five models plotted in the figures, the metallicities that most closely match the observations are 0.2 Z_ , in all the spectra regions considered. Because in our spectra we did not observe any emission at 1533 , we calculated the metallicity of the spectrum CDFS-4417 applying the Equation equ2 , and we found a value of Z=0.26Z_ 0.12 .

sources.summary: The paper treats CDFS-4417 as a high-SFR object whose X-ray emission appears stellar rather than nuclear, with X-ray and far-infrared measurements indicating intense dust-obscured star formation.

quotes.quote: A 3.1 detection for CDFS-4417 was found by Fiore2012 in the 4Ms CDFS X-ray data. On the basis of the X-ray colours and of the low X-ray to z and H band flux ratios, they conclude that the X-ray emission is due to stellar sources rather than to a nuclear source. This conclusion is also supported by the absence of AGN features in the available optical spectrum.
The luminosity log(L _X )=42.5 (2-10 keV) of object CDFS-4417 translates into SFR _ X 630 200 M_ /yr using the conversion by Ranalli2003 .
A check on the deep PACS data obtained by combining GOODS-Herschel and PEP observations Magnelli2013 shows a significant detection for CDFS-4417 in both the 100 m (3.3 ) and 160 m (3.8 ) bands.

sources.summary: The paper includes Candels-5001 as a z=3.472 BCG candidate detected in CO(4-3), with X-ray and radio measurements that leave AGN contamination possible.

quotes.quote: As discussed in Castignani2019 they are i) the z=1.7 BCG observed in CO(2 1) by Webb2017 , which belongs to the Webb2015 sample of distant BCGs, similarly to our two targets; ii) the z = 1.99 BCG candidate of the cluster ClJ1449+0856, detected in CO(4 3) and CO(3 2) by Coogan2018 ; iii) MRC 1138-262 at z = 2.2 (i.e., the Spider Web galaxy), detected in CO(1 0) by Emonts2013, Emonts2016 ; and iv) Candels-5001 at z = 3.472 , detected in CO(4 3) by Ginolfi2017 .

Candels-5001 at z=3.47 has an X-ray (2-10 keV) luminosity of 10^ 42.5 erg/s which Fiore2012 attributed to stellar sources. However the authors also report for the source a 1.4 GHz radio flux of (15.3 6.3) Jy, which implies a rest frame 1.4 GHz luminosity density L_ 1.4 GHz =1.2 10^ 24 W/Hz, To estimate L_ 1.4 GHz we have assumed a radio spectral flux density at the observer frame frequency , expressed as S_ ^ . We have adopted a spectral index =0.8 , see also Sect. sec:sample . which is more consistent with those of distant low-luminosity radio galaxies e.g., Chiaberge2009 , more than starbursts, which typically have L_ 1.4 GHz <10^ 23 W/Hz e.g., Sargsyan1and_Weedman2009 . Therefore the possibility that an AGN is hosted by Candels-5001 cannot be firmly excluded.

gmw_00006124.name: FCC 119
gmw_00006124.overview: FCC 119 is a faint, low-inclination S0 early-type galaxy [arXiv:1906.08187, arXiv:2205.15331] with irregular central isophotes [arXiv:1906.08187]. Its nuclear star cluster contains ionized gas and a young stellar population, indicating recent or ongoing in-situ star formation [arXiv:2104.06412].

sources.summary: FCC 119 is described as a faint early-type intermediate infaller with low rotation and dispersion, irregular central isophotes, altered line strengths, and central ionised gas.

quotes.quote: FCC 119 -- Fig. fig:FCC119map FCC 119 is one the faintest ETG of the F3D sample. It is an intermediate infaller located in the low-density region on the northern side of the cluster at a distance of R_ proj =2.1^ from the centre. The stellar kinematics is characterised by low rotation velocity ( v_ max 20 km s ^ 1 at R 15 arcsec) and velocity dispersion ( 20 km s ^ 1 ) which is constant at all radii. The light distribution from the reconstructed image shows irregular isophotes in the central region ( R 10 arcsec). The H line-strength index measured in the central parts of the galaxy is 1 \ larger while the Mg b and Fe5015 indices are both 1 \ smaller compared to the outskirts.
Ionised-gas emission is detected only in the galaxy centre, where it appears to rotate in the same direction as the stars and is, probably, mainly powered by ongoing star formation.

sources.summary: The paper groups FCC 119 with galaxies showing young central populations, linking young NSC stars and, for some systems, ionised gas to ongoing or recent in-situ nuclear growth.

quotes.quote: Galaxies with young central populations FCC 119 and FCC 148 are the only two sample galaxies that show the presence of ionised gas in their NSC as traced by the presence of H , H , [ NII ] , [ SII ] and [ OIII ] lines in the MUSE spectra (see also the emission line maps in Iodice2019 ). Our analysis uncovered young stellar populations ( < 3 Gyr) in both NSCs, while their host stellar bodies are older. The same was found for VCC 2019 although without the clear presence of ionised gas.

This ongoing or recent star formation is a clear sign of in-situ star formation building up the NSCs. However, FCC 119 also shows an older, more metal-poor component in its NSC and is on average more metal-poor than the host galaxy. This drop in metallicity could indicate additional accretion of metal-poor GCs from the galaxy outskirts to the centre.

sources.summary: The paper newly fits FCC 119 deep imaging with multiple photometric components and describes it as a low-inclination S0 whose second component combines disk and stellar halo light.

quotes.quote: As FCC 119, FCC 249 and FCC 255 were not analysed by Spavone2020 , we performed the multi-component fit of their deep band VST images taken from the FDS first data release Peletier2020 , available via the ESO Science Portal https://archive.eso.org/scienceportal/home?publ_date=2020-08-26 . The azimuthally-averaged surface-brightness radial profiles obtained by following the procedure described by Iodice2019 and their corresponding best-fitting radial profiles are shown in Fig. fig:fit while the best-fitting values of the structural parameters, transition radii, and total accreted mass fraction are reported in Table tab:fit . Both FCC 119 and FCC 255 are S0 galaxies.

As explained in Spavone2020 , for these objects the second component of the fit represents a superposition of the disk and the stellar halo. However, differently from the three S0s FCC 153, FCC 170 and FCC 177, FCC 119 and FCC 255 are low-inclined galaxies and they do not have a prominent disk. Therefore, the low-accreted mass fraction estimated for these objects can reasonable take into account a fraction of light coming from the disk component.

gmw_00006125.name: FCC 249
gmw_00006125.overview: FCC 249 is a compact, low-luminosity elliptical galaxy [arXiv:1906.08187] located in the southern low-density region of the Fornax cluster [arXiv:1906.08187]. The galaxy features a nuclear rotating component [arXiv:1906.08187], a strong inner isophotal twist [arXiv:astro-ph/9806331], and an old, metal-rich stellar population [arXiv:0710.5529, arXiv:2104.06412].

sources.summary: FCC 249 is described as a compact, low-luminosity non-rotating elliptical intermediate infaller with a nuclear rotating component, central dispersion peak, and similar line-strength gradients.

quotes.quote: FCC 249 (NGC 1419) -- Fig. fig:FCC249map FCC 249 is a compact low-luminosity non-rotating elliptical galaxy at a distance of R_ proj =2.1^ from the centre, close to the virial radius of Fornax. It is an intermediate infaller in the southern low-density region of the cluster. The stellar kinematic maps reveal a nuclear ( R 5 arcsec) component with v_ max 50 km s ^ 1 . The stellar velocity dispersion peaks in the centre ( 110 km s ^ 1 ) and decreases outwards ( 80 km s ^ 1 at R 20 arcsec). Similar behaviour is observed in Mg b and Fe5015, which are larger in the centre ( 4 ) and decrease outwards ( 2 ).
H is nearly constant at all radii ( 2 ).

sources.summary: The paper presents NGC 1419 as an E0 Fornax galaxy with anomalously bright K-band surface-brightness fluctuations but central spectra consistent with old, metal-rich populations.

quotes.quote: NGC 1419 - this E0 galaxy has anomalously bright K-band surface brightness fluctuations (SBF) relative to its I-band surface brightness fluctuations, suggesting the presence of extended giant branches, perhaps due to the presence of an intermediate-age population mei01, liu02 .

However, two galaxies in common with our sample (NGC 1419 and 1427) were found to have brighter (larger) K-band SBF relative to the mean relationship (see also Mei et al. 2001). A larger K is thought theoretically to arise from the presence of an extended giant branch, i.e. more cool, bright, M giants. In the spectroscopic data discussed here, there are no indications of such extended giant branches in these galaxies -- their central line indices are consistent with an integrated stellar populations dominated by an old, metal-rich population.

sources.summary: The paper places FCC 249 among intermediate-mass galaxies with old, metal-rich NSCs, interpreting their stellar populations as evidence for repeated central star formation or young-cluster accretion.

quotes.quote: Galaxies with old, metal-rich NSCs FCC 190, FCC 310, FCC 277, and FCC 249 have similar stellar masses ( 3 - 5 10^9 M_ ). Their NSCs are more metal-rich than the hosts and any GCs. The NSCs and galaxies are characterised by old stellar populations ( > 10 Gyr) and FCC 190 and FCC 277 show a minor young, metal-rich population in its NSC. The high metallicities of the NSCs as well as the extended SFHs in FCC 190, FCC 310, and FCC 277 are in agreement with formation from multiple episodes of in-situ star formation or the repeated accretion of young star clusters.

sources.summary: NGC 1419 has NGC 1336-like rotation-curve wiggles, a flat then declining dispersion profile, strong inner isophotal twist, and possible bar-like structure.

quotes.quote: NGC 1419 This galaxy has a very similar RC to NGC 1336, with 15 km s ^ 1 wiggles observed out to 2 R_e . The VDP is largely flat (at 120 km s ^ 1 ) over the inner 9 (=1 R_e ), then it steadily decreases to 70 km s ^ 1 reaching the outer radius limit of the data. Like NGC 1336, this galaxy also has a very strong isophotal twist ( 100 ^ ) over the inner region, where the RC is observed to remain flat. Also in this case we thus find evidence for a possible bar-like structure.

sources.summary: The paper adds FCC 249 to the multi-component photometric analysis, deriving its surface-brightness profile, structural parameters, transition radii, and accreted mass fraction from FDS imaging.

quotes.quote: As FCC 119, FCC 249 and FCC 255 were not analysed by Spavone2020 , we performed the multi-component fit of their deep band VST images taken from the FDS first data release Peletier2020 , available via the ESO Science Portal https://archive.eso.org/scienceportal/home?publ_date=2020-08-26 . The azimuthally-averaged surface-brightness radial profiles obtained by following the procedure described by Iodice2019 and their corresponding best-fitting radial profiles are shown in Fig. fig:fit while the best-fitting values of the structural parameters, transition radii, and total accreted mass fraction are reported in Table tab:fit . Following the predictions of theoretical simulations Cooper2013, Cooper2015 and the procedure described in Spavone2017 , we modelled the azimuthally-averaged surface-brightness radial profiles by the superposition of i) a S e rsic law Sersic, Caon1993 , for the central galaxy regions, ii) a second S e rsic law for the intermediate regions, and iii) an outer S e rsic or exponential law for the outskirts.

gmw_00006126.name: FCC 255
gmw_00006126.overview: FCC 255 is a low-inclination lenticular galaxy located in the northern region of the Fornax cluster [arXiv:2205.15331, arXiv:1906.08187]. It features an inner thin stellar disk surrounded by a thicker envelope [arXiv:1906.08187], with a young, metal-rich nuclear star cluster shaped by in-situ star formation [arXiv:2104.06412].

sources.summary: The paper includes FCC 255 in its new multi-component fit, reaching its first transition radius and treating it as a low-inclination S0 with disk/stellar-halo light mixing.

quotes.quote: For most of them, we were able to follow the stellar kinematic and population properties well beyond the first transition radius, from the bounded in-situ component to the accreted (ex-situ) stellar halo. There only a few exceptions: for FCC 310 and FCC 255 we reached only 0.4 R_ tr ,1 and R_ tr ,1 , respectively, whereas for FCC 167, the F3D data extend out to the second transition radius reaching the regions of the stellar envelope (Figs. fig:metallicity_sb and fig:kinematics_populations ). Our combined photometric-spectroscopic analysis yielded two major results for for the brightest ETGs inside the virial radius of the Fornax cluster: the galaxies in the highest ( 10.8 (M_ / M _ ) 11.2 ) and intermediate ( 10.5 (M_ / M _ ) 10.8 ) mass bins have flatter metallicity radial profiles than those observed for the lowest ( 8.9 (M_ / M _ ) 10.5 ) mass bin (Fig. fig:mass_bin ); on average, the galaxies with highest accreted mass fraction (as derived from photometry) have milder gradients of both metallicity and surface-brightness radial profiles (Fig. fig:cook ); it seems that a segregation in metallicity exists between the cluster members belonging to the core-NS clump and the infalling galaxies.

Both FCC 119 and FCC 255 are S0 galaxies. As explained in Spavone2020 , for these objects the second component of the fit represents a superposition of the disk and the stellar halo. However, differently from the three S0s FCC 153, FCC 170 and FCC 177, FCC 119 and FCC 255 are low-inclined galaxies and they do not have a prominent disk.

sources.summary: FCC 255 is described as a lenticular intermediate infaller with an inner thin disc and thicker envelope, low rotation, minor-axis dispersion increase, and distinct disc line strengths.

quotes.quote: FCC 255 (ESO 358-G050) -- Fig. fig:FCC255map FCC 255 is a lenticular galaxy on the northern side of Fornax at R_ proj =1.8^ from FCC 213 (NGC 1399). It is one of the intermediate infallers in the low-density region, with an inner thin disc and outer thicker envelope, also evident in the stellar kinematic and line-strength maps. The maximum rotation is v_ max 50 km s ^ 1 at R 30 arcsec. The stellar velocity dispersion is small and constant ( 40 km s ^ 1 ) along the disc major axis and increases by 20 km s ^ 1 along the minor axis. The thin disc has larger values of Mg b and Fe5015 (by 2 ) and smaller values of H (by 1 ) compared to the thicker envelope.

sources.summary: The paper says FCC 255 belongs to a set with similar galaxy and NSC properties, where young, metal-rich NSCs indicate dominant in-situ nuclear star formation.

quotes.quote: FCC 301 and VCC 990 have similar galaxy ( M_ gal 10^ 9 M_ ) and NSC masses ( M_ NSC 7 10^6 M_ ), and their stellar population properties are also similar to that of FCC 255 which is slightly more massive. In these three galaxies, the NSCs are younger and more metal-rich than their hosts, their SFHs show a single peak at age 3 Gyr. The young ages and high metallicities indicate NSC formation dominated by in-situ star formation either in recently accreted young massive star clusters or directly in the NSC.

gmw_00006127.name: G1
aliases_external: MG0414+0534 main lens galaxy G1 / CASTLES component G
gmw_00006127.overview: The galaxy G1 is a gravitational lens system [arXiv:1212.2166]. Photometry from the Hubble Space Telescope indicates that the lens galaxy has relatively round isophotes [arXiv:1212.2166].

sources.summary: The paper models G1 as the main lens galaxy, using an isothermal ellipsoid plus external shear and weak priors informed by its relatively round HST isophotes.

quotes.quote: The main lens galaxy (G1) is modeled as a singular isothermal ellipsoid (SIE) combined with an external shear, as this is the simplest plausible model for a lens. There is considerable evidence in favor of the isothermal profile, and the properties of 4-image systems other than time delays are very insensitive to the radial mass distribution see mey06,koo09 . We include weak priors on the ellipticity of the main lens ( 1-b/a=0 1 ), and the external shear ( =0.05 0.05 ). The expectation value of 0 for the ellipticity is based on the relatively round isophotes derived from the HST photometry of the lens fal97,kee97 .

gmw_00006128.name: G2
aliases_external: MG0414+0534 object X / secondary lens G2
gmw_00006128.overview: The galaxy G2 is a nearby object that acts as a secondary lens in gravitational lensing models [arXiv:1212.2166]. Its presence in these models is essential for reproducing the observed image astrometry of the lensed system [arXiv:1212.2166].

sources.summary: The paper identifies G2 as the nearby object X secondary lens, tests SIS and pseudo-Jaffe models for it, and includes its mass scale and position in two-galaxy lens models.

quotes.quote: ros00 found that the presence of an observed nearby galaxy, object X sch93 , hereafter ``G2'', in their lens models was essential for reproducing the image astrometry. For G2, we tried two different models: a singular isothermal sphere (SIS) or a pseudo-Jaffe (pJaffe) profile with a scale length a' that is allowed to vary.

The models have 17 total constraints (the positions of 4 lensed images and both lens galaxies, the 3 flux ratios, plus the priors on the ellipticity e and shear ) and 12 free parameters (the mass scale b_1 , position, ellipticity e and position angle _e for G1, the amplitude and position angle _ of the external shear, the mass scale b_2 and position for G2, and the source position), plus one more free parameter for the scale length a'_2 when using a pseudo-Jaffe profile.
We find a slightly better fit when using an SIS profile for G2, with ^2=31.1 for dof=5 degrees of freedom and a best-fit flux ratio A2/A1=1.01 .

gmw_00006129.name: CTA 102
gmw_00006129.overview: CTA 102 is a flat-spectrum radio quasar located at a redshift of z=1.037 [arXiv:1807.02869]. During active flaring states in 2016 and 2017, it exhibited extreme gamma-ray luminosity and contemporaneous UV, optical, and X-ray activity [arXiv:1807.02869].

sources.summary: The paper studies CTA 102 during 2016-2017 flaring, reporting extreme gamma-ray luminosity, contemporaneous UV/optical/X-ray activity, compact emission-region constraints, and SED models involving dusty-torus radiation.

quotes.quote: We report on broadband observations of CTA 102 ( z=1.037 ) during the active states in 2016-2017. In the ray band, Fermi LAT observed several prominent flares which followed a harder-when-brighter behavior: the hardest photon index =1.61 0.10 being unusual for FSRQs. The peak ray flux above 100 MeV (3.55 0.55) 10^ 5 photon cm^ 2 s^ 1 observed on MJD 57738.47 within 4.31 minutes, corresponds to an isotropic ray luminosity of L_ =3.25 10^ 50 erg s^ 1 , comparable with the highest values observed from blazars so far.

The shortest e-folding time was 4.08 1.44 hours, suggesting a very compact emission region R 2.16 10^ 14 cm. We modeled the spectral energy distribution of CTA 102 in several periods (having different properties in UV/optical, X-ray and ray bands) assuming a compact blob inside and outside the BLR. We found that the high-energy data are better described when the infrared thermal radiation of the dusty torus is considered.

gmw_00006130.name: NGC 6269
gmw_00006130.overview: NGC 6269 is a cD galaxy located at the center of the poor galaxy cluster AWM 5 [arXiv:1103.1364]. It hosts a low-power, compact radio source within its optical envelope [arXiv:1103.1364] and exhibits velocity substructure in its central region [arXiv:0808.1521].

sources.summary: The paper describes NGC 6269 as the cD galaxy in AWM 5 hosting a compact low-power radio source aligned with possible small X-ray cavities.

quotes.quote: NGC 6269 The cD galaxy NGC 6269, at the center of the poor cluster of galaxies AWM 5, hosts a low-power (Table 1), small radio source (Burns, White Hough 1981; Giacintucci et al. 2007). Our GMRT images are shown in Fig. fig:ngc6269 . On the left, the 235 MHz contours are overlaid on the optical image, while the right panel shows the X-ray emission of the cluster with the 610 MHz contours overlaid. The radio emission is totally contained within the optical envelope of the host galaxy. In the 5 ^ resolution image at 610 MHz, the source is resolved in three components.

sources.summary: The paper reports published surface-brightness profiles for NGC6269, finds central velocity substructure, and notes the slit was 46 degrees from the major axis.

quotes.quote: NGC6269: Surface brightness profiles were published by Schombert (1986) and by Malumuth Kirshner (1985). The radial velocity profile shows some velocity substructure in the centre of this galaxy. This galaxy was observed at an angle of 46 degrees away from the major axis.

gmw_00006131.name: HLS J045518.0+070103
gmw_00006131.overview: HLS J045518.0+070103 is a high-redshift galaxy with a tentative redshift identification [arXiv:1506.04747]. Observations of a single CO-line detection favor a redshift of z=2.927 associated with the CO(3-2) transition, though lower-redshift alternatives remain possible [arXiv:1506.04747].

sources.summary: The paper reports a single CO-line detection for HLS J045518.0+070103, favors a z=2.927 CO(3-2) identification, and notes that lower-redshift alternatives remain possible.

quotes.quote: HLS J045518.0+070103 A line with an integrated S/N=7.3 with a well-fitted Gaussian profile (see Fig. lines_all ) has been detected in this spectrum. The detection of just one line at =88.051 GHz gives a tentative redshift for this galaxy and therefore we have to associate this line with the most probable transition based on other characteristics of the galaxy. If we associate this detection with CO(4--3) at z=4.236 , we expect to detect the CO(5--4) transition at 110.1 GHz still within our bandpass. The spectrum, however, does not show any line feature around this frequency and, therefore, this solution is discarded. Other possible solutions are CO(3--2) or CO(2--1), which correspond to redshifts of z=2.927 and z=1.618 , respectively.

gmw_00006132.name: HLS J102225.9+500536
gmw_00006132.overview: HLS J102225.9+500536 is a high-redshift galaxy [arXiv:1506.04747]. It exhibits a low flux density at 1.1 mm and a high ratio of 850 Ιm to 1.1 mm flux, which may indicate a higher dust temperature [arXiv:1506.04747].

sources.summary: The paper reports no secure line detection for HLS J102225.9+500536 and argues that faint 1.1 mm flux, redshift coverage, or temperature could explain the nondetection.

quotes.quote: HLS J102225.9+500536 For this source we have no detections at S/N 3.5 , either in the raw or in the binned spectrum. One possible explanation is that the galaxy lies in the `redshift desert' of the RSR. However, as we can see in Table fotometria , this galaxy has the lowest flux density at 1.1 mm, which is less than half of those of the rest of the sample. This would suggest that we need to integrate more in this fainter galaxy to detect a CO emission line. Furthermore the ratio S_ 850 m /S_ 1.1 mm is the largest of the sample, which could be due to a higher temperature, and make more difficult the detection of molecular lines than in the other sources.

gmw_00006133.name: HLS J173140.8+225040
gmw_00006133.overview: HLS J173140.8+225040 is a high-redshift galaxy located at a redshift of z=2.712 [arXiv:1506.04747]. Observations of the galaxy have detected CO(3-2) and CO(5-4) line transitions, with the CO(3-2) line shape showing some departure from a single Gaussian profile [arXiv:1506.04747].

sources.summary: The paper detects a single line in HLS J173140.8+225040, identifies it as CO(3-2) at z=2.712 using prior observations, and discusses a possible non-Gaussian line shape.

quotes.quote: HLS J173140.8+225040 This is another case of single-line detection at =93.163 GHz with a line integrated S/N=5.7. In this case we can associate this transition with CO(4--3) at z=3.949 or CO(3--2) at z=2.712 having rejected the CO(2--1) solution at z=1.475 due to the low probability of lensing at this lower redshift. Independently, earlier observations made by the HLS team had already identified the redshift of this galaxy through a CO redshift search with the IRAM 30-m/EMIR, yielding z=2.712 through the detections of CO(5--4) and CO(3--2) (Dessauges-Zavadsky et al., in preparation), and hence our single line detection unambiguously corresponds to CO(3--2). The line-shape shows some departure from a single Gaussian profile.

gmw_00006134.name: HLS J204314.2--214439
gmw_00006134.overview: HLS J204314.2--214439 is a spectroscopic blend containing at least two sources [arXiv:1506.04747]. One of these components is confirmed at a redshift of z=4.680, detected via carbon II and carbon monoxide emission lines [arXiv:1506.04747].

sources.summary: The paper finds HLS J204314.2--214439 is a spectroscopic blend, with multiple lines requiring at least two sources and a confirmed z=4.680 component detected in CII.

quotes.quote: HLS J204314.2--214439 In this case, we have detected four lines with S/N 3.5 in the spectrum at =75.827 , 81.178, 101.459, and 108.428 GHz. They are not compatible with a single redshift solution, which indicates that we have at least two sources inside our RSR beam. As a first step to associate these detections with CO transitions, we find that the 81.2 and 101.5 GHz lines agree very well with CO(4--3) and CO(5--4) at z=4.680 , however there is no explanation for the 76 and 108 GHz lines at this redshift. The z=4.680 solution has been confirmed after we reanalysed SMA observations, where we found the [ CII ] transition at =334.648 GHz (see Fig. SMA_CII ). The Gaussian fitting to the spectral line gives a FWHM of 432 68 km s ^ 1 , which is in good agreement with the line widths of the CO emission lines (see Table espectros ), and a redshift of z=4.679 is also in agreement with our redshift derived from the CO detections.

gmw_00006135.name: FCC204
gmw_00006135.overview: FCC204 is a dwarf galaxy classified as dS0 or dE(di) that exhibits a discy photometric structure [arXiv:1105.4809]. It features a spiral structure visible after model subtraction [arXiv:0903.4393, arXiv:1105.4809], stellar populations of old and intermediate ages [arXiv:0903.4393], and a central metallicity gradient that flattens outward [arXiv:0903.4393].

sources.summary: The paper describes FCC204 as a spiral-structure dwarf with old and intermediate populations and a metallicity gradient that flattens outward.

quotes.quote: FCC204 This is a galaxy with a spiral structure.
The SFH in the both 1 and 1 arcsec extractions are consistent with two subpopulations of old and intermediate ages.
For instance, FS29 and FCC204 have a metallicity gradient in the centre, but the profile flattens out beyond 0.5 .

sources.summary: FCC 204 is a dS0 galaxy with spiral arms visible after model subtraction, discy photometric structure, and dE(di)-type classification.

quotes.quote: FCC 204 A dS0(6) galaxy, with spiral arms, visible after subtracting an axis-symmetric model. The fit to the photometric profile shows positive C _4 , an indication of the disciness. In the terminology of lisker2007 it will be classified as dE(di).

gmw_00006136.name: FCC207
gmw_00006136.overview: FCC207 is a nucleated dwarf galaxy in the Fornax cluster that exhibits recent star formation in its inner region [arXiv:2005.01532, arXiv:0903.4393]. This central activity is marked by gas emission and a compact, offset disc-like component that may be partly obscured by dust [arXiv:2005.01532].

sources.summary: The paper highlights FCC 207 as a nucleated Fornax dwarf with central gas emission, a compact offset disc-like component, dust-obscured structure, and ambiguous evidence for in-situ NSC formation.

quotes.quote: The fit to FCC 207 is worth noting at this point. This galaxy required a double S ersic profile in addition to the PSF profile at the core, where the more compact S ersic profile resembles a flattened disc and is offset from the centre of the galaxy by 1.5 \ ( 147 pc at the distance of Fornax), as shown in Fig. fig:galfit_fit_FCC207 . An inspection of the spectra of each component showed that while all three components contain gas emission, the spectrum of the PSF component shows no clear stellar continuum or absorption features, and the features in the spectrum for the flattened disc have very low S/N. A study of this galaxy by DeRijcke_2003 found that the nucleus in FCC 207 is elongated and kidney-shaped due to a region of dust obscuration to the north, however with the mean seeing in the MUSE datacube of 1.22 \ the nucleus is unresolved in this data set.

No HST data have been obtained to our knowledge. Consequently, with the data available, it is unclear whether these two central components represent a faint NSC and a compact disc, or a gaseous disc, perhaps partly obscured by dust, that is fuelling star formation and creating the NSC in situ. Future high-resolution imaging is well encouraged.

sources.summary: The paper finds FCC207 has recent central star formation, Balmer and oxygen emission from young stars, and no detected young component at one effective radius.

quotes.quote: FCC207 The inner part of this galaxy displays constant SFH till recent epochs.
The presence of a young population is marked by Balmer and [ O iii ] emissions.
In the 1 region we failed to find a young component.

gmw_00006137.name: FCC245
gmw_00006137.overview: FCC245 is a nucleated dwarf elliptical galaxy [arXiv:1105.4809] with a low surface brightness [arXiv:astro-ph/0309311]. Its nuclear star cluster exhibits a central metallicity drop [arXiv:2104.06412, arXiv:1105.4809], and the galaxy hosts an un-imaged ring of intermediate-age stellar populations [arXiv:1105.4809].

sources.summary: The paper describes FCC 245 as having a central metallicity drop and mixed NSC populations, requiring both metal-poor cluster infall and younger metal-rich in-situ or cluster accretion.

quotes.quote: Galaxies with evidence of both NSC formation scenarios The radial metallicity profiles of FCC 188, FCC 202, FCC 222, and FCC 245 all clearly show a drop in metallicity in the galaxy centres and their NSCs are on average more metal-poor than the host galaxies. This drop in metallicity is indicating formation from the infall of metal-poor GCs, but the SFHs of these galaxies all show a secondary component with a higher metallicity and significantly younger age (also seen in FCC 119). This metal-rich, young population can be explained by additional in-situ star formation or the accretion of a young star cluster. The NSCs of these galaxies are therefore examples of NSCs were both formation channels are acting together.

sources.summary: The paper treats FCC245 as a peculiar dwarf with intermediate and old populations, a corrected-core decline, and unusual SSP profiles.

quotes.quote: FCC245 This galaxy is best fitted with a two-population (intermediate and old) scenario.
Its corrected core shows a decline in the SSP-equivalent metallicity and age.
The SSP profiles of FCC245 are peculiar.

sources.summary: FCC 245 is described as a nucleated dE0 with a central metallicity depression and an intermediate-population ring not apparent in images.

quotes.quote: FCC 245 This is a nucleated galaxy (dE0). As for FCC 136 we detected a central depression of the metallicity table 10 paperII . We find a ring of intermediate population, which is not seen in the images.

sources.summary: FCC245 is one of the low-CaT dwarf ellipticals; its indices match an old, low-metallicity population, and the paper notes low surface brightness and sky-line contamination of P14.

quotes.quote: FCC245 and NGC5898 dE2 (a dE in the NGC5898 group) have normal PaT values but very low CaT 5.5   .

In fact, FCC245 is the only galaxy in this subsample whose indices are in agreement with its low metallicity content ( [ Z/H ] 1.1 ) and old age ( t 10  Gyr). FCC245 has a low surface brightness ( _ e _B = 23.5  mag/arcsec ^2 ) and its P14 line is affected by bright sky emission lines (influencing PaT and CaT* but not CaT), hence the somewhat larger errorbars.

gmw_00006138.name: FCC288
gmw_00006138.overview: FCC288 is a dwarf S0 galaxy [arXiv:1105.4809] that contains an underlying spiral structure [arXiv:1105.4809, arXiv:0903.4393] and clear evidence of an embedded stellar disc [arXiv:0903.4393]. It exhibits a central metallicity depression [arXiv:1105.4809] and hosts a young stellar population in its outer region [arXiv:0903.4393].

sources.summary: FCC 288 is a dS0(7) with underlying spiral structure, positive disciness, central metallicity depression, and slight magnesium under-abundance.

quotes.quote: FCC 288 This is a dS0(7) galaxy with spiral underlying structure and with a positive C _4 . As for FCC 204, it wiould be called dE(di) in the terminology of lisker2007 . We detected a central depression of the metallicity profile table 10 paperII . It has a slight Mg _b under-abundance.

sources.summary: The paper reports a spiral structure in FCC288, a young outer population absent from the inner arcsecond, and embedded stellar-disc evidence.

quotes.quote: FCC288 A spiral structure is found in FCC288.
In the outer part we detect a young population which is not seen in the inner 1 arcsec (like in the case of FCC150).
FCC288 and FCC204 contain clear evidence for embedded stellar discs d03 .

gmw_00006139.name: FS373
gmw_00006139.overview: The dwarf elliptical galaxy FS373 contains three resolved stellar populations [arXiv:0903.4393]. Its central region is dominated by an intermediate-age starburst, and its isophote ellipticity profile suggests the presence of a small central stellar disk [arXiv:0903.4393].

sources.summary: The paper describes FS373 as having a small central stellar disk, three resolved populations, and a centrally dominant intermediate-age burst.

quotes.quote: In FS373, the ellipticity profile of the isophotes suggests the presence of a small central stellar disc d04 .
FS373 This galaxy was resolved in 3 populations.
While in the centre the intermediate age burst is dominating the mass, it has the smallest contribution in the outskirts.

gmw_00006140.name: J1048+7143
gmw_00006140.overview: J1048+7143 is a blazar classified as a flat-spectrum radio quasar [arXiv:2311.03924]. The active galactic nucleus exhibits recurrent gamma-ray flares [arXiv:2311.03924], which may be driven by a supermassive black hole binary system at its center [arXiv:2311.03924].

sources.summary: The paper models J1048+7143 as a blazar with recurrent gamma-ray flares and a possible supermassive black-hole binary at its heart.
sources.paper_coordinate_text: right ascension RA_ J2000 =162 1067 and declination DEC_ J2000 =71 7297

and Astronomy, ELTE E o tv o s Lor a nd University, P a zm a ny P e ter s e t a ny 1/A, H-1117 Budapest, Hungary HUN-REN--ELTE Extragalactic Astrophysics Research Group, ELTE E o tv o s Lor a nd Observatory, Chinese Academy of Sciences, 150 Science 1-Street, Urumqi 830011, China Analyzing single-dish and VLBI radio, as well as Fermi LAT ray observations, we explained the three major ray flares in the ray light curve of FSRQ J1048+7143 with the spin--orbit precession of the dominant mass black hole in a supermassive black hole binary system.
Here, we report on the detection of a fourth ray flare from J1048+7143, appearing in the time interval which was predicted in our previous work.

Using the updated analysis covering the time range between 2008 Aug 4 and 2023 Jun 4, we further constrain the parameters of the hypothetical supermassive binary black hole at the heart of J1048+7143 and we predict the occurrence of the fifth major ray flare that would appear only if the jet will still lay close to our line sight.

gmw_00006141.name: UGC 9753
gmw_00006141.overview: UGC 9753 is a SAbc spiral galaxy classified as a LINER/H II galaxy [arXiv:1804.07594]. Photometric analysis reveals a bright central spot, a disc, and a bulge [arXiv:1804.07594], while its dark matter halo is modeled as an oblate spheroidal configuration [arXiv:2011.01863].

sources.summary: The paper analyzes UGC 9753 rotation curves and dark matter halo geometries, classifies it as a LINER/H II SAbc galaxy, and models its disc, central structure, and bulge photometrically.

quotes.quote: As an illustration of the potentiality of the determined circular velocity expression we perform the rotation curves analysis of UGC 8490 and UGC 9753 and we estimate the total and dark matter mass of these two galaxies under the assumption that their respective dark matter halos have spherical, prolate and oblate spheroidal mass distributions. We employ stellar population synthesis models and the total HI density map to obtain the stellar and HI+He+metals rotation curves of both galaxies. The subtraction of the stellar plus gas rotation curves from the observed rotation curves of UGC 8490 and UGC 9753 generates the dark matter circular velocity curves of both galaxies. We fit the dark matter rotation curves of UGC 8490 and UGC 9753 through the newly established circular velocity formula specialised to the spherical, prolate and oblate spheroidal mass distributions, considering the Navarro, Frenk and White, Burkert, Di Cintio, Einasto and Stadel dark matter halos.

UGC 9753 (NGC 5879) is a LINER/H II galaxy Filho2000 , and is classified as SAbc in NED, Hyperleda and Simbad database following the study of Ann2015 . The GALFIT 2D photometric study reveals three main features: a bright central spot, a disc and a bulge. We fit a 2D exponential disc profile to the SDSS g band image of UGC 9753 Abazajian2009 , after removing the luminous nuclear structure through a stellar point spread function extracted from the same SDSS image.

sources.summary: The paper fits UGC 9753's dark-matter rotation curves with several halo models, finding cored or cuspy profiles fit the data and an oblate spheroidal dark matter configuration is supported by torque estimates.

quotes.quote: In the second part of this manuscript, we employ the tools developed in the first part, to analyse the mass density content and the inner and global structure of the dark matter haloes of UGC 8490 and UGC 9753, through the fits to the dark matter rotation curves of the two galaxies, assuming a triaxial spheroidal dark matter mass configuration. We employ the Navarro Frenk and White, Burkert, DiCintio, Einasto and Stadel dark matter models, and we obtain that both a cored Burkert and cuspy DiCintio and Navarro Frenk and White inward dark matter distributions could represent equally well the observed data, furthermore we determine an oblate spheroidal dark matter mass density configuration for UGC 8490 and UGC 9753. The latter outcome is confirmed by the estimation of the gravitational torques exerted by the dark matter halo of each analysed galaxy, on the corresponding baryonic components.

sources.summary: NGC5879 is marginally detected in BeppoSAX data after using a small aperture to avoid a nearby quasar, yielding a low 2-10 keV luminosity estimate.

quotes.quote: NGC5879 This object is marginally detected at the 3 level. We used a radius of 1 arcmin detection circle to avoid contamination from a nearby ( 4 arcmin) quasar. This translates to a flux of 8 10^ 14 or to a luminosity of 3 10^ 39 in the 2-10 keV band (assuming a spectrum with =1.9 ).

gmw_00006142.name: NGC 3269
gmw_00006142.overview: NGC 3269 is a grand-design Sa spiral [arXiv:astro-ph/0505541] or lenticular galaxy [arXiv:1510.04274, arXiv:2011.06988] that is strikingly devoid of gas [arXiv:astro-ph/0307200] and lacks active star formation [arXiv:astro-ph/0505541, arXiv:2011.06988]. It exhibits extended arc-like structures suggesting tidal interactions [arXiv:astro-ph/0505541] and features a compact, isolated dust patch [arXiv:astro-ph/0307200, arXiv:2011.06988].

sources.summary: The paper notes a compact absorption feature projected on the smooth, gas-poor spiral NGC 3269. Its expected size and reddening behavior make an in-galaxy dust cloud peculiar, favoring a foreground Galactic cloud.

quotes.quote: During the visual inspection of the frames we noted a compact absorption feature in NGC 3269, a spiral galaxy that has no noticeable GCS. The dust cloud can be seen in the south-western part as shown in a C image (Fig. fig:n3269C ). NGC 3269 is strikingly devoid of gas (Barnes et al. ) and exhibits a very smooth structure. The absorption feature has a diameter of 4 and can also been seen on previous images of NGC 3269, for example in the Sandage Betke (1994) atlas. At the distance of NGC 3269 it would have a diameter of approximately 500 pc.

Unless it is located well above the plane of NGC 3269 (which would be another peculiar feature) we expect light contribution from the foreground stellar population. Both facts would result in a grayer reddening law, i.e. a higher R value. Thus the observation of a rather lower R value is an indication that this cloud indeed is located within our galaxy.

sources.summary: The paper places NGC 3269 in a high-velocity trio of bright lenticulars projected near NGC 3268, arguing the undisturbed S0s likely lie behind Antlia.

quotes.quote: To the last group belong the three bright lenticulars NGC 3267 (FS90-168), NGC 3269 (FS90-184), and NGC 3271 (FS90-224), near to NGC 3268 in projected distance.

Strikingly, all three S0's around the central galaxy (FS90-224, FS90-168, and FS90-184) have positive velocity offsets in the range of 900-1000 km s ^ 1 . If these velocities were due to the potential of NGC 3268 (and its associated dark matter), very radial orbits would be needed for surpassing the circular velocity that is about 300 km s ^ 1 and probably constant cas14 . The high velocity offsets are, moreover, related to fairly large projected spatial offsets (which in the case of FS90-224 is about 65 kpc), whereas the highest velocities are found at the bottom of the potential well. Furthermore, the galaxies do not show any morphological indication of being tidally disturbed.
We therefore prefer the interpretation that the three S0s have to be placed in the background of NGC 3268.

sources.summary: The paper describes NGC 3269 as a grand-design Sa spiral with little star formation or HI, smooth light distorted by a dust patch, and arcs suggesting tidal interaction and gas removal.

quotes.quote: ISM: clouds --- ISM: dust, extinction --- In the course of studying the globular cluster systems of NGC 3258 and NGC 3268 in the Antlia cluster, we noted a tiny dust patch in the neighbouring galaxy NGC 3269 dirsch03 with a diameter of about 4 arcsec, barely visible on images found in the literature. This is striking since NGC 3269 is a grand design Sa spiral without signs of star formation or small scale substructure. Moreover, the galaxy seems to be devoid of HI barnes01 , who quote from their ATCA observations an upper limit of about 10^9 M_ .

The inset shows NGC 3269 on a larger scale in Washington C (see for details). Clearly visible are extended arc-like structures indicating previous or ongoing tidal interactions. These events might have been responsible also for a gas removal out of NGC 3269.

sources.summary: The paper describes NGC 3269 as an S0 galaxy with visible spiral structure, no recent star formation, little H I, and an atypical isolated dust complex possibly associated with the galaxy.

quotes.quote: NGC 3269 is a S0 galaxy with a grand design spiral pattern that is still visible (Fig. Magellan1 ), and with a heliocentric radial velocity of 3750 to 3797 . There are no signs of actual or recent star formation. The existence of an isolated dust complex far from the centre is also very atypical for the appearance of dust in early-type galaxies. Moreover, the galaxy seems to be devoid of H I barneswebster2001 , with an upper limit of about 10^9 . If associated with NGC 3269, the patch would be an extragalactic giant gas/dust cloud complex larger than 500 pc in diameter.

gmw_00006143.name: CXOECDFS J033115.0 275518
gmw_00006143.overview: CXOECDFS J033115.0 275518 is a radio-loud quasar located at a redshift of z=1.369 [arXiv:0901.2929]. It exhibits a double-peaked broad Mg II emission line and displays two radio lobes with a Fanaroff-Riley II morphology [arXiv:0901.2929].

sources.summary: The paper identifies CXOECDFS J033115.0 275518 as a z=1.369 radio-loud quasar with double-peaked Mg II emission, FR II lobes, typical quasar SED, and X-ray luminosity in the quasar regime.

quotes.quote: We report the discovery of the most-distant double-peaked emitter, CXOECDFS J033115.0 275518, at z=1.369 . A Keck/DEIMOS spectrum shows a clearly double-peaked broad Mg 2 2799 emission line, with FWHM 11 000  km s ^ 1 for the line complex. The line profile can be well fit by an elliptical relativistic Keplerian disk model. This is one of a handful of double-peaked emitters known to be a luminous quasar, with excellent multiwavelength coverage and a high-quality X-ray spectrum.

CXOECDFS J033115.0 275518 is a radio-loud quasar with two radio lobes (FR II morphology) and a radio loudness of f_ 5  GHz /f_ 4400  429 . The X-ray spectrum can be modeled by a power law with photon index 1.72 and no intrinsic absorption; the rest-frame 0.5--8.0 keV luminosity is 5.0 10^ 44   . The spectral energy distribution (SED) of CXOECDFS J033115.0 275518 has a shape typical for radio-loud quasars and double-peaked emitters at lower redshift.

gmw_00006144.name: dwarf galaxy at =12^h35^m21.335^s , =27^ 33^m46.68^s
gmw_00006144.overview: The dwarf galaxy at 12h35m21.335s, +27d33m46.68s is located at least 58 kpc away from the center of NGC 4559 [arXiv:1703.09345]. It is likely unbound from NGC 4559 and has two optical counterparts in the Sloan Digital Sky Survey [arXiv:1703.09345].

sources.summary: The paper reports a newly detected H I dwarf near NGC 4559, likely unbound, with two SDSS optical counterparts interpreted as merging blue compact dwarf galaxies.
sources.paper_coordinate_text: The center of this dwarf is located at =12^h35^m21.335^s , =27^ 33^m46.68^s

quotes.quote: A previously undetected dwarf galaxy was found in the widest field HALOGAS data cube of NGC 4559. The center of this dwarf is located at =12^h35^m21.335^s , =27^ 33^m46.68^s , which puts it at least 0.418^ ( 58 kpc) away from the center of NGC 4559, if the objects are spatially aligned in the same plane. The heliocentric velocity of the dwarf is 1200 km s ^ 1 and emission from the dwarf spans 9 channels from 1187-1224 km s ^ 1 , in total.

This yields an escape velocity of v_ esc 121 km s ^ 1 . This value is much lower than the 390 km s ^ 1 difference in systemic velocity of NGC 4559 and the central velocity of the dwarf. Though we cannot measure the exact distance where the rotation curve ceases to be flat, this would have to occur at a radial distance of 261 kpc for the escape velocity to match the velocity of the dwarf. Since it is unlikely that the rotation curve is flat to such an extreme distance, we conclude that the dwarf is unbound. Two optical counterparts for this object were found in the Sloan Digital Sky Survey (SDSS, eisensteinetal11 ) data release 12 database.

gmw_00006145.name: J1924--2914
gmw_00006145.overview: J1924--2914 is a radio-loud blazar and quasar located at a redshift of z=0.353 [arXiv:1208.4402, arXiv:2208.01662]. The source exhibits strong optical variability and high polarization [arXiv:1208.4402, arXiv:2208.01662], and it features a compact core with a bending inner jet [arXiv:1208.4402, arXiv:2208.01662].

sources.summary: The paper presents 1.3 mm VLBI imaging of quasar 1924-292, resolving its inner jet, measuring a compact core, and interpreting the low brightness temperature in jet-model terms.

quotes.quote: The model image of the quasar 1924-292 was obtained with four telescopes at three observatories: the James Clerk Maxwell Telescope (JCMT) on Mauna Kea in Hawaii, the Arizona Radio Observatory's Submillimeter Telescope (SMT) in Arizona, and two telescopes of the Combined Array for Research in Millimeterwave Astronomy (CARMA) in California in April 2009. With the greatly improved resolution compared with previous observations and robust closure phase measurement, the inner jet structure of 1924-292 was spatially resolved. The inner jet extends to the northwest along a position angle of 53^ at a distance of 0.38 mas from the tentatively identified core, in agreement with the inner jet structure inferred from lower frequencies, and making a position angle difference of 80^ with respect to the cm-jet. The size of the compact core is 0.15 pc with a brightness temperature of 1.2 10^ 11 K. Compared with those measured at lower frequencies, the low brightness temperature may argue in favor of the decelerating jet model or particle-cascade models.

The quasar 1924-292 (PKS 1921-293, OV-236) is one of the brightest and most compact flat-spectrum radio sources in the sky. It has been classified as an optically violent variable 1981Natur.289..384W,1988AJ.....96.1215P and highly polarized quasar 1990ApJ...360..396W . As a radio-loud blazar, it shows strong variability from radio to X-ray.

sources.summary: The paper presents EHT and multi-frequency VLBI imaging of J1924--2914, a compact radio-loud quasar/blazar whose inner jet bends with frequency and shows ordered toroidal magnetic fields.

quotes.quote: The blazar J1924--2914 is a primary Event Horizon Telescope (EHT) calibrator for the Galactic Center's black hole Sagittarius A ^* . Here we present the first total and linearly polarized intensity images of this source obtained with the unprecedented 20 as resolution of the EHT. J1924--2914 is a very compact flat-spectrum radio source with strong optical variability and polarization.

We combine the multi-frequency images of J1924--2914 to study the source morphology. We find that the jet exhibits a characteristic bending, with a gradual clockwise rotation of the jet projected position angle of about 90 degrees between 2.3 and 230 GHz. Linearly polarized intensity images of J1924--2914 with the extremely fine resolution of the EHT provide evidence for ordered toroidal magnetic fields in the blazar compact core. galaxies: active --- galaxies: jet --- galaxies: individual: J1924--2914 --- techniques: interferometric The radio source (PKS 1921--293, OV--236) is a radio-loud quasar at a redshift z=0.353 Wills_1981,Jones2009 . The source exhibits strong optical variability and is highly polarized Wills_1981,Pica_1988,Worrall_1990 .

sources.summary: The paper uses quasar calibrator 1924 292 to validate 1.3 mm VLBI calibration, finding all-baseline detections, repeatable correlated-flux behavior, and stable jet-associated structure.

quotes.quote: The flux density measured for the calibrator 1924 294, observed over the same time and elevation ranges as Sgr A*, was 9.95, 10.21, and 10.75 Jy on days 95, 96, and 97. We normalized CARMA gains to a constant flux density of 10.25 Jy on all three days.

The quasar 1924 292 was easily detected on all baselines (Table  table-detections ). On each scan, low-band and high-band fluxes after a-priori calibration track each other consistently (Figure  fig-1921-scans ). After gain correction assuming a total flux density of 10.25  Jy, the data from all three days are highly consistent with one another. The SC and SD baselines show consistent variation in the correlated flux density that is repeated each day. The long-baseline detections (SJ, JC, and JD) also show day-to-day repeatable behavior, indicating detection of stable source structure presumably associated with a jet shen1997 .

sources.summary: The paper identifies 1921-293 as OV-236 and reports optical/infrared polarization, a sharp H-band brightness decrease, and correlated infrared color indices.

quotes.quote: 1921-293, OV-236 , z=0.352 An optical variation of m=2.64 (Pica et al. 1988) and polarizations of P_ opt. =16.89 and P_ IR =13.94 (Mead et al. 1990) are reported for this object. The H light curve shows a sharp brightness decrease, which leads H variation to be greater than the optical variation. During the sharp decreasing period, there were no observations for J or K. (J-K) is found correlated with (J-H) and (H-K) (see Fig. 12): (J-K) = 0.63 + 1.30 (J-H) with = 0.80 and p = 9.1 10^ 4 , (J-K) = 0.17 + 1.80 (H-K) with = 0.50 and p = 4.7 .

gmw_00006146.name: Reticulum II
gmw_00006146.overview: Reticulum II is an ultra-faint dwarf galaxy that is strongly dominated by dark matter [arXiv:1504.02889]. Its member stars exhibit a wide metallicity spread, including several extremely metal-poor stars [arXiv:1504.02889].

sources.summary: The paper confirms Reticulum II as an ultra-faint dwarf galaxy, measuring member-star kinematics, high mass-to-light ratio, metallicity spread, and dark-matter annihilation expectations.

quotes.quote: We present Magellan/M2FS, VLT/GIRAFFE, and Gemini South/GMOS spectroscopy of the newly discovered Milky Way satellite Reticulum II. Based on the spectra of 25 member stars selected from Dark Energy Survey imaging, we measure a mean heliocentric velocity of and a velocity dispersion of . The mass-to-light ratio of within its half-light radius is , demonstrating that it is a strongly dark matter-dominated system.

The likely member stars of span 1.3 dex in metallicity, with a dispersion of 0.28 0.09 dex, and we identify several extremely metal-poor stars with [ Fe/H ] < -3 . In combination with its luminosity, size, and ellipticity, these results confirm that is an ultra-faint dwarf galaxy. With a mean metallicity of [ Fe/H ] = , matches Segue 1 as the most metal-poor galaxy known.

gmw_00006147.name: DDO 133
gmw_00006147.overview: DDO 133 is a dwarf irregular galaxy located 3.5 Mpc away in the Canes Venatici I Group [arXiv:2006.01735]. It features a stellar bar [arXiv:1905.06915], five Type 1 interstellar gas holes [arXiv:2006.01735], and a massive outer neutral hydrogen filament [arXiv:1905.06915].

sources.summary: The paper identifies DDO 133 as a barred dwarf irregular with weak noncircular gas near the bar and a massive outer H I filament offset from young star-forming regions.

quotes.quote: In optical images, DDO 133 appears to have a stellar bar. This is visible as a rotation in the position angle of isophotes between the inner galaxy and the outer. In the V band image shown in Figure fig-d133ubv one can see the bright, boxy inner structure that is the stellar bar and the more diffuse oval structure that is the stellar disk. The bar is outlined on other images in Figure fig-d133bar . Here we have included a velocity map of the weak noncircular gas map (wnonc1) where we have identified two wnonc regions that are coincident with the northern end of the bar (wnonc region 1) and upper part of the body of the bar (wnonc region 2).

The filament in DDO 133 is region 18 in Figure fig-maps7 , and region 17 just above region 18, although somewhat detached, could also be part of this filament. There are 2.7 10^6 M \ of gas in this filament, which is 3 of the total \ mass of the galaxy. The width of the filament is about 590 pc, the average column density of the \ is 1.8 10^ 20 , and the distance of the filament from the center of the galaxy in the face-on plane of the galaxy is about 3 kpc.

sources.summary: The paper describes DDO 133 as a Canes Venatici I Group galaxy with five H I holes, all classified as Type 1.

quotes.quote: DDO 133 DDO 133 (PGC 41636, UGC 76980) is situated at a distance of 3.5 Mpc in the Canes Venatici I Group. Its V-band magnitude is 14.8 . DDO 133 has five holes in the ISM, and all are Type 1 holes.

gmw_00006148.name: DDO 167
gmw_00006148.overview: DDO 167 is an asymmetric, low surface brightness irregular galaxy located approximately 4.2 Mpc away in the Canes Venatici I group [arXiv:astro-ph/0210414, arXiv:2006.01735]. Its neutral hydrogen disk extends to 1 kpc and contains two Type 1 holes and one Type 3 hole [arXiv:2006.01735].

sources.summary: The paper discusses UGC 8308 as a CVn I galaxy observed with WFPC2, describing its stellar population or morphology and deriving a TRGB-based distance.

quotes.quote: This is an asymmetric irregular galaxy of low surface brightness, becoming redder from its core to the periphery. It has been resolved into stars by Tikhonov Karachentsev (1998), who derived a distance of 3.7 0.7 Mpc from the brightest star photometry. The CMD (Fig. 2) shows blue and red stellar populations with the TRGB position yielding a distance of 4.19 0.47 , which is in close agreement with the previous estimate.

sources.summary: The paper describes DDO 167 as a Canes Venatici I Group galaxy with two Type 1 holes and one Type 3 hole in its H I disk.

quotes.quote: DDO 167 DDO 167, also known as PGC 45939 or UGC 8308 lies in the Canes Venatici I group about 4.2 Mpc away. The galaxy has two Type 1 holes and a Type 3 hole in the \ disk which extends to 1 kpc. The estimated \ scale height of the galaxy is about 240 pc.

gmw_00006149.name: Haro 36
gmw_00006149.overview: Haro 36 is a blue compact dwarf galaxy located approximately 9.3 Mpc away [arXiv:1502.01281, arXiv:2006.01735]. It contains a kiloparsec-sized Type 3 hole that is larger than the galaxy's scale height [arXiv:2006.01735].

sources.summary: The paper identifies Haro 36 as a blue compact dwarf whose rotation curve can fit either core or cusp halo models, with a relatively steep slope that may be influenced by beam smearing.

quotes.quote: However, as already discussed in Section DM_PROFILE , the rotation curves of some LITTLE THINGS sample galaxies (e.g., DDO 70, DDO 101, DDO 154, DDO 210 and Haro 36) are equally well fitted by CDM NFW and pseudo-isothermal halo models in terms of ^ 2 _ red values. Moreover some of them, such as DDO 101, DDO 210 and Haro 36, appear to have relatively steep inner density slopes (although Haro 36 is defined as a blue compact dwarf galaxy which usually shows a steep increase in rotation velocity in the inner region mainly due to young starburst components formed during galaxy interaction or mergers; e.g., Bekki_2008 Bekki_2008 ).

Notwithstanding the dominant trend of core-like DM distribution in the LITTLE THINGS sample galaxies, some of the sample galaxies, such as DDO 210 and Haro 36 are equally well fitted by core- and cusp-like halo models in describing their DM rotation curves. They have relatively steeper inner density slopes with 0.70 and 0.50 for DDO 210 and Haro 36, respectively, compared to the mean value (DM only) of the rest of the sample ( 0.29 ). It is possible, however, despite the high angular resolution of the LITTLE THINGS H i data, the inner density slopes of these two galaxies are affected by beam smearing (the H i disk of DDO 210 being intrinsically small and Haro 36 being one of the more distant dwarfs).

sources.summary: The paper describes Haro 36 as the second most distant sample galaxy, containing a kiloparsec-sized Type 3 hole larger than its scale height.

quotes.quote: Haro 36 Haro 36 (PGC 43124, UGC 7950) is located about 9.3 Mpc away and is the second most distant galaxy in the sample. It is a galaxy with a kiloparsec-sized Type 3 hole with expansion velocity 18 . Interestingly, the size of this intact hole is larger than the galaxy's scale height of 220 pc, which suggests that the hole might not be completely spherical in nature. The rotational velocity of the galaxy is 76 .

gmw_00006150.name: M81dwA
gmw_00006150.overview: M81dwA is an extremely faint dwarf galaxy located in the M81 group at a distance of approximately 3.5 Mpc [arXiv:2006.01735]. It is a close companion to Holmberg II, and a past interaction between the two galaxies may have triggered elevated star formation in M81dwA [arXiv:1208.4808].

sources.summary: The paper describes M81dwA as a faint M81 Group dwarf whose inner H I disk is nearly covered by a giant Type 1 hole.

quotes.quote: M81dwA M81dwA (PGC 23521) is one of the extremely faint (V-band magnitude 11.7 ) dwarfs in the M81 group, and is about a distance of 3.5 Mpc. The inner part of the \ disk of the galaxy is almost covered by a giant kiloparsec-sized Type 1 hole. The galaxy also contains one Type 2 and eight Type 3 holes in the outer part of the disk. Mrk 178 Mrk 178 (UGC 6541, PGC 35684) is a BCD located 3.9 Mpc away from the Milky Way in the Canes Venatici I Group, and has a V-band magnitude of 14.1 . The structure of the galaxy was studied by but they didn't report on any notable \ structures.

sources.summary: The paper discusses M81dwA as HoII's nearby dwarf companion, close enough for a past interaction about 300 Myr ago and showing elevated recent star formation like UGC4483.

quotes.quote: Indeed, as shown in Figure fig9 , HoII has two close companion dwarf galaxies: M81dwA (=Kar52) located at a projected distance of 30 kpc to the North East, and UGC4483, 110 kpc to the South East). The former, in particular, is sufficiently close that the timescale of a past interaction is roughly compatible with the enhanced SFR found by dal12 . Assuming a relative velocity of 100 km s ^ 1 the difference of radial velocities is 45 km s ^ 1 ; kar02 , means that M81dwA could have passed close to HoII about 300 Myr ago, in good agreement with the beginning of the SFR enhancement. Both companion dwarfs also appear to have been experiencing higher star formation in the past few hundred million years than at older epochs mcq10,war11 .

gmw_00006151.name: S0
gmw_00006151.overview: S0 is a magnified disk galaxy [arXiv:1512.04527] with a central bright core [arXiv:1512.04527] and an optical spectrum containing emission lines [arXiv:1512.04527]. It is classified as an Sbc-type spiral galaxy [arXiv:1512.04527] and hosts an active galactic nucleus [arXiv:1512.04527].

sources.summary: The paper identifies S0 as a magnified disk galaxy with a central bright core, emission-line optical spectrum, Sbc template, and X-ray luminosity typical of an AGN.

quotes.quote: The source S0 has a spectroscopically measured redshift of 1.6852 0.001 2014ApJS..211...21E and an amplification factor of 3.6 1.0 The source is associated with a disk galaxy that has a central bright core in the HST image. The source is also detected with \ ( 171 25 net counts) with an unabsorbed 2-10  keV flux of 1.71_ 0.20 ^ +0.16 10^ 14  erg s ^ 1  cm ^ 2 . The photon index of the power-law of was determined at 0.89_ 0.42 ^ +0.33 . Together with the amplification factor and associated uncertainty this translates into a rest-frame luminosity of 0.29_ 0.07 ^ +0.11 10^ 44 erg s ^ 1 , typical of an AGN.
The optical spectrum of the galaxy contains emission lines and the best-fitting BPZ template is that of an Sbc type spiral galaxy.

sources.summary: The paper uses galaxy 399 as a slitless-spectroscopy test case, fitting emission-line kinematics and reporting rotation, redshift, or detection significance.

quotes.quote: We apply this simultaneous fit on the [ O III ] emission line of galaxy 399 from the cluster MACS0717 of the GLASS survey, measured with a disc scale length r_ d = ;;0.44 . Each roll-angle spectrogram is first adjusted on its own, leading to a 14.7 and 21.6 internal kinematic detection respectively; the joint fit on the two spectrograms provides a kinematic z score of 25.5 (see Fig. fig:399_residus ). The fit gives the following results for the common adjusted kinematic parameters: v_ 0 i= 287 15 km.s^ 1 , w_ 0 i= 605 32 km.s^ 1 .arcsec^ 1 which corresponds to a RC turnover radius r_ 0 = ;;0.47 r_ d . We find a final redshift z = 1.69175 0.00006 , accounting for the wavelength offset x = 12.37 0.32 ; this corresponds to a shift of about 0.5 px between the wavelength solutions.

gmw_00006152.name: S6
gmw_00006152.overview: S6 is a compact, star-like gravitationally lensed source with an associated X-ray counterpart [arXiv:1512.04527]. Although its best-fitting template is a spiral galaxy, the poor fit and stellar appearance suggest the object may instead be a quasar [arXiv:1512.04527].

sources.summary: The paper describes S6 as a compact star-like lensed source with an X-ray counterpart, spiral-galaxy template but poor fit, and a possible quasar interpretation.

quotes.quote: Source S6 seems to be associated with a compact star-like object. We also find an X-ray counterpart to the source ( 150 12 net counts). The object has a z_ phot = 1.89_ 0.09 ^ +0.08 and an amplification factor of 2.3 0.7 . With this amplification factor and redshift, the unabsorbed 2-10  keV flux of 8.48_ 1.08 ^ +1.24 10^ 15  erg s ^ 1  cm ^ 2 translates to a rest-frame luminosity of 0.88_ 0.25 ^ +0.41 10^ 44  erg s ^ 1 .

The best-fitting BPZ template was that of a spiral galaxy, but the ^2 value of 29.3 indicated a very poor fit. Given the star-like nature of the object and poor fit, this source could be a quasar Note that the BPZ fitting did not include AGN or quasar galaxy templates .

gmw_00006153.name: S8
gmw_00006153.overview: S8 is a highly magnified starburst galaxy located at a photometric redshift of approximately 1.15 [arXiv:1512.04527]. It has an estimated stellar mass of 2.4 billion solar masses and a radio-derived star formation rate of 15 solar masses per year [arXiv:1512.04527].

sources.summary: The paper identifies S8 as a highly magnified starburst galaxy, estimates a radio-derived star formation rate, and derives stellar mass and specific star formation rate.

quotes.quote: S8 is located at z_ phot = 1.15_ 0.03 ^ +0.04 and has a high amplification factor of about 6.4 1.8 . The best-fitting BPZ template is that of a starburst galaxy. Based on the S-band radio flux, we compute a SFR of 15^ +12 _ 7  M _  yr ^ 1 . With M _ * = 2.4^ +1.4 _ 1.0 10^ 10  M _ we compute a sSFR of 0.6 ^ +0.4 _ 0.3  Gyr ^ 1 .

gmw_00006154.name: S9
gmw_00006154.overview: S9 is a faint red lensed galaxy with a photometric redshift of approximately 1.69 [arXiv:1512.04527]. Best-fitting templates correspond to an Sbc or ES0 galaxy, which exhibits a high star formation rate of 49 solar masses per year [arXiv:1512.04527].

sources.summary: The paper associates S9 with a faint red lensed galaxy, gives its redshift, template, relatively flat radio spectral index, and high radio-derived star formation rate.

quotes.quote: S9 is associated with a faint red galaxy with z_ phot = 1.69_ 0.06 ^ +0.22 and is amplified with a factor of 3.4 1.0 . The best-fitting BPZ template corresponds to a Sbc/ES0 galaxy. The source has a relatively flat spectral index of =-0.4 0.2 between 1.5 and 5.5 GHz. Based on the integrated flux density, we compute a high SFR of 49^ +46 _ 21  M _  yr ^ 1 , scaling with =-0.4 . A blue galaxy is located about 1  to the east of this galaxy at z_ photo = 1.70_ 0.14 ^ +0.12 .
With M _ * = 4.5^ +3.3 _ 1.9 10^ 10  M _ we compute a sSFR of 1.1^ +0.8 _ 0.5  Gyr ^ 1 .

gmw_00006155.name: S45
gmw_00006155.overview: S45 is a faint red galaxy with a photometric redshift of approximately 1.41 [arXiv:1512.04527]. The galaxy is gravitationally lensed with a high amplification factor of 8.7, fits a spiral spectral template, and has a radio-derived star formation rate of 17 solar masses per year [arXiv:1512.04527].

sources.summary: The paper describes S45 as a faint red lensed galaxy with a spiral template, high amplification, and a radio-derived star formation rate.

quotes.quote: S45 is associated with a faint red galaxy with z_ phot = 1.41_ 0.19 ^ +0.06 . It has a high amplification factor of 8.7 4.1 with a best-fitting spiral galaxy spectral template. We compute a SFR of 17^ +29 _ 11  M _  yr ^ 1 .

gmw_00006156.name: S61
gmw_00006156.overview: S61 is a blue irregular starburst galaxy with a complex morphology [arXiv:1512.04527]. It features a bright northern knot embedded within its overall emission, which is listed as a separate component in some catalogs [arXiv:1512.04527].

sources.summary: The paper identifies S61 as a lensed blue irregular/starburst galaxy with complex morphology, split catalog components, and photometric-redshift consistency.

quotes.quote: S61 is associated with a blue irregular galaxy. Its amplification factor is 3.4 2.5 and the best-fitting spectral template is that of a starburst galaxy. It is listed as two separate objects in the CLASH photometric catalog, with component 2 (Table  tab:compactsources ) being a bright ``knot'' to the north, embedded within the overall emission from the galaxy (component 1). Thus, the HST images suggest that both components belong the same galaxy with a complex morphology. This is also consistent with the two photometric redshifts that indicate z_ photo 1.6 .

gmw_00006157.overview: The active galaxy 1642+690 is a radio-loud quasar [arXiv:1510.08153] classified as a flat-spectrum radio quasar [arXiv:astro-ph/0302265] and a blazar [arXiv:1804.03703]. It hosts a fast parsec-scale jet [arXiv:1101.5822] with a core-dominated morphology [arXiv:1510.08153], alongside a curved kiloparsec-scale jet [arXiv:1101.5822] and possible Fe K emission [arXiv:astro-ph/0302265].

sources.summary: The paper reports a low-significance gamma-ray signature for 5BZQJ 1642+6856 in source confusion with a nearby HSP blazar, showing residual positional consistency.

quotes.quote: Even though no \ source is reported within 1 ^ of 5BZQJ 1642+6856 in any of the FGL catalogs, our likelihood analysis finds a \ signature matching the position of this source, which is located only 10 arcmin from the HSP blazar 2WHSPJ 164014.8+685233. Indeed, the high-energy E > 1.0 GeV TS map (Fig. 2whsp1640 in top left panel) reveals a dominant \ signature coincident with the 2WHSP source, however extending toward the position of 5BZQJ 1642+6856. We then consider photons in the 0.1-500 GeV band and a likelihood function including two nearby point-like emitters, which gives us an estimate of the \ spectral properties for each source as shown in Table tableFermi2 . Despite the low statistical significance associated with BZQJ 1642+6856 \ signature (TS < 10), we note that the photon spectral index estimated for the pair 5BZQ 2WHSP is consistent with the hypothesis of confusion between a steep and a hard component as expected for nearby LSP and HSP blazars. As a consistency test, we built an additional high-energy TS map (E > 1 GeV) by adding a point-like source at the 2WHSP position modeled as a power-law with the same parameters as from Table solved1 , such that the 2WHSP \ signature is now part of the background model.

As a result (Fig. 2whsp1640 , top right panel) we reveal a residual point-like signature consistent with 5BZQJ 1642+6856 within the 68 confinement radius. All together, it suggests this might be a case of source confusion, which is hard to resolve with currently available data.

sources.summary: The paper reports possible Fe K emission in 1642+690 and links its equivalent width to the same weaker-beaming interpretation discussed for the detected lines.

quotes.quote: Interestingly, we find evidence for possible Fe K line emission in two sources of our sample, 0723+679 and 1150+497, and possibly in a third object, 1642+690. Inspection of the residuals of the power law models shows excess emission around 3.5 keV, 4.8 keV, and 3.7 keV for 0723+679, 1150+497, and 1642+690, respectively. These energies are consistent with the redshifted Fe K line energy, 6.4 keV. To model the line, a Gaussian component was added to the power law models reported in Table 4 for each of the three sources. The significance of the ^2 improvement is 97 for 0723+679 and 1150+497, and 94 for 1642+690. In Figure 3 we show the best-fit model and data for 0723+679 and 1150+497, for which the line detections are more significant.

We detect an Fe K emission line in two sources (0723+679 and 1150+497) and possibly in a third (1642+690) source. The detection of such a line is very interesting di per se , because the presence of Fe K-shell emission from high-luminosity AGNs in general, and from radio-loud sources in particular, is still not established (see Sect. 1). While it is important to confirm these lines and study their profile with higher-quality X-ray spectra, we note that their measured rest-frame EWs (Table 6a) are consistent with the EWs of the other radio-sources with a double-lobe radio morphology and similar intrinsic X-ray luminosity recently studied by ASCA (Hasenkopf et al. 2002). Usually, dilution of the X-ray continuum by a beamed jet component would weaken the strength of the Fe K lines in radio-loud quasars. This could suggest weaker beaming in 0723+679 (indeed a SSRQ) and in 1150+497 and 1642+690 (both FSRQs).

sources.summary: The paper identifies 1642+690 as a radio-loud quasar with only core-dominated KaVA morphology, a faint southern jet component, four model components, and the faintest target flux.

quotes.quote: 1642+690 (4C+69.21) 1642+690 (4C+69.21), shown in Figure fig:fig3 , is a radio loud quasar with a redshift of 0.751 Lawrence1986 . The corresponding angular scale is 7.35 pc/mas. Several works reported that the source shows apparent superluminal jet with a speed of 9c Venturi1997, Lister2013 .

In our data, we only found a core-dominated morphology with a very faint jet component south of the core, approximately 12 mas from the core. Even though the detailed source structure is not resolved, we found four model components that are expanding along the jet. 1642+690 has a total flux of 0.87 Jy with a peak intensity of 0.61 Jy/beam, making it the faintest among our targets.

sources.summary: The paper describes a fast pc-scale jet, a differently oriented kpc-scale jet curving east, and an X-ray excess at the brightest extended-jet region.

quotes.quote: 1642 + 690 (4C +69.21) This source was in the sample and is included in our analysis for completeness. The pc-scale jet is oriented along a position angle of -162 \ and has a maximum apparent speed of 16 vlba while the kpc-scale jet points due south and curves to the east odea88 . There appears to be an X-ray excess associated with the brightest part of the extended jet, 3 \ from the core.

gmw_00006158.name: NGC 5678
gmw_00006158.overview: NGC 5678 is classified as a spiral galaxy [arXiv:1504.06282]. It is considered a possible barred galaxy, exhibiting unusually large non-circular motions within its structure [arXiv:1504.06282].

sources.summary: The paper treats NGC 5678 as a possible barred galaxy, citing high-signal data and unusually large non-circular motions as supporting evidence.

quotes.quote: For completeness, we have added NGC 5678, a galaxy which may have a bar but which has not been classified as either SAB or SB (see Sect. spiralncm ). To quantify the non-circular motions, we have studied the cumulative distribution function (CDF) of the pixel values in the residual images. We have adopted the value of the 95 of the distribution of the residual velocities (in absolute value) as a representative value of the overall non-circular motion within that region. With this method, we avoid introducing possible caveats: if we had used a histogram, the bin size would have changed the adopted value for the residual velocity; and if we had defined regions of certain physical scale, the size of the region would matter. Another advantage of the method is that the correlations and trends would not change if we choose 50, 70 or 95 , as we represent the distribution of all the values. The uncertainties were estimated via a Monte Carlo simulation of the effect of adding random noise to the velocity residuals, the former following a Gaussian distribution with sigma equal to the spectral sampling . In Fig. cdf we present the statistical distribution of the residual velocities of the arm region in NGC 2748. The adopted value for the residual velocities there is 23.2 1.8 km s ^ 1 ; which corresponds to the representative value in the residual map of NGC 2748 (panel e in Fig. ngc2748plots ). The computed values for the non-circular motions in the bar, SAR and spiral arms are presented in Table ncmtable . For completeness, we have normalised the residual velocities. To do so, we have computed the corresponding circular velocity at a radius equal to the bar length, to the extent of SAR, and to the last measured point (assuming that the spiral arms end there), following the universal rotation curve by Persic1991 . This method includes several uncertainties, such as assuming that the circular velocity across the complete region is equal to that at the last radial point, or that the region is circular with a radius the extent of the structure (bar, SAR or arms). Note that the residual velocities have been deprojected. Structural parameters To understand the nature of the non-circular motions within the regions of the galaxies, we study them in conjunction with some of the structural parameters that have been derived for the galaxies of our sample. The bar strength is quantified by the torque parameter Q_ b , which describes the maximum amplitude of tangential forcing normalised by axisymmetric radial force ( Combes1981 ; Buta2001 ). Q_ b values have been measured from the torque maps derived from the 3.6 m S ^ 4 G images (for a description of the method see Salo2010 and Laurikainen2002 ), and will be published in a compilation of bar strengths for the S ^ 4 G sample (S. D iaz-Garc ia et al., in prep). The parameter Q_ b is defined so that Q_ b decreases with the axisymmetric radial force, hence the presence of the bulge is implicitly present in Q_ b . Due to the bulge influence, Q_ b is not the best parameter to show the strength of the bar on the gas kinematics. However, we will use those Q_ b values as they are the only ones that measure the bar strength that have been derived from the S ^ 4 G images. We have also studied the bulge-to-total ratio ( B/T ). The values from B/T have been derived from 2D decompositions of the 3.6 m images Salo2015 . We list the Q_ b and B/T values for the galaxies of our sample in Table ncmtable . The bar lengths have been measured from the torque maps (D iaz-Garc ia et al., in prep.). The bar lengths are deprojected units, measured using the method explained in Salo2010 , except when the fits do not have trustworthy quality, in which the bar lengths are measured visually (Herrera-Endoqui et al., in prep.). We have also studied the spiral arm class, as a parameter that is related to the spiral arm strength. We have obtained the arm class classifications carried out by D. Elmegreen and presented in the CVRHS, where F means flocculent , M denotes multi-armed and G grand design . Note that NGC 4324 has not been presented in the table as it does not have an arm class classification. The values for the arm classification are in Table ncmtable . Star formation rates The H line traces the emission from massive young stars. The advantages of this recombination line are that it traces very recent SF (timescales 6-8 Myr), and that it has lower sensitivity to dust attenuation than the UV (although not negligible, Cardelli1989 ). Thus, we can exploit the H imaging and relate the star formation to the galaxy's kinematics.
