We derive the star formation rates (SFRs) derived from the H luminosity (L _ H ) following Kennicutt2009 : SFR (M_ yr^ 1 ) = 5.5 10^ 42 L( H ), where L( H ) is the luminosity, calculated as L( H ) [ erg/s ] =4 D^ 2 (3.086 10^ 24 )^ 2 F_ H ^ * , with D the distance to the galaxy in Mpc (Table 1) and F_ H ^ * the flux corrected for Galactic absorption, as taken from NED and obtained from the Schlafly2011 recalibration of the Schlegel1998 dust map. Equation sfrha assumes a Kroupa2001 stellar IMF with a mass range of 0.1-100 M_ , an electron temperature of T_ e =10^ 4 K and electron density n_ e =100 cm ^ 3 , whereas variations in T_ e from 5000 to 20000 K would result in a variation of the calibration constant ( 5.5 10^ 42 ) of 15 . Variations of n_ e = 100-10^ 6 cm ^ 3 would result in variations in the calibration constant below 1 Osterbrock2006 . This calibration also assumes that over timescales > 6 Myr, star formation needs to remain constant, and no information about the previous star formation history is given (see Kennicutt2012 ). The uncertainties in the measurements of the SFR come from: mainly from the distance, with a typical 20 uncertainty estimated for the distance value, basic reduction processes (i.e. flat-fielding correction, around 2 ), the zero-point calibration (3 , in agreement with the value of 2 typical for photometric nights), uncertainty in the flux measurement (1 ) and continuum-subtraction process (around 11 ). Altogether, taking into account the uncertainties related to the quality of the image (without considering the distance) we estimate an uncertainty of 17 . To consider only star formation, we have to take into account that some of the galaxies of our sample have nuclear activity: NGC 918 (no specific classification; Palumbo1983 ); NGC 4151 (Seyfert type 1.5; Khachikian1974 ) and NGC 4639 (Seyfert 1.0; Ho1997 ), where all nuclear activity classifications have been collected in VeronCetty2006 . The H emission in these cases is not all coming from star formation, so the derived SFRs in these galaxies should be understood as upper limits. We have masked the nuclear emissions in these three cases and excluded them from the integration (very small in the cases of NGC 918 and the low-luminosity AGN of NGC 4639), so that the global H luminosities are not biased due to AGNs. NGC 3504 on the other hand is a starburst galaxy Balzano1983 with a large amount of star formation in its centre. In Fig. sfr , we present the SFR as a function of the morphological T type (from the CVRHS ). Excluding the starburst galaxy (which stands out in the plot as a filled green diamond), we find the same trend as widely found in the literature (e.g., Kennicutt1998 ; James2004 ; and references therein): early-type galaxies tend to have low SFRs, intermediate-type spirals have the highest SFRs and very late-type spirals and irregulars have lower SFRs than the intermediate-type ones, although higher than those of galaxies of the earliest types. Although our sample is small, we confirm that the presence of a bar does not affect the global SFR (e.g., Phillips1993 ; Kennicutt1994 ; Kennicutt1998 and references therein; James2004 ; Fisher2006 ). A Kolmogorov-Smirnov (K-S) test on both the barred and the non-barred distributions with respect to the SFR results in a P value of 0.094. This P value indicates the relationship between both distributions, and in this case, it means that both the barred and the non-barred distributions with respect to the SFR are very similar. From our ACAM images, we have measured the total SFRs for the galaxies of our sample, and also the SFRs for each of the defined regions. In Table sfrtable we present the resulting SFRs (total, bar, SAR and arms). All the data discussed here are released publicly with the publication of this paper: raw but reduced cubes (de-rotated, phase corrected and wavelength calibrated cubes) , continuum-subtracted cubes, 0th, 1st and 2nd order moment maps and continuum-subtracted H images. The data are available in FITS format through the NED and the Centre de Donn ees Stellaires (CDS). In this Section we will discuss critically the data and data quality, and the results from our analysis in terms of kinematical parameters and non-circular motions. We will not analyse here the rotation curves as they will be studied in depth in the forthcoming Paper III (Erroz-Ferrer et al., in prep.). Data quality We first discuss how our observing, reduction and analysis procedures have led to caveats and constraints on the data quality. Effects from the nature of the H data One of the scientific goals of this kinematical survey is the study of the inner parts of the rotation curves, exploiting the high angular resolution provided by GH FaS data. To do so, we have tried not to change the angular resolution when reducing the data, as would have been the case when using large spatial smoothing kernels or adaptive binning methods. As the data are made public, the user can always choose to adopt another smoothing kernel depending on scientific interest. Normally, H data have a typical patchy appearance with many blank spaces in between the H ii regions. As we have hardly smoothed the data spatially, this effect is rather obvious in most of our data. The data set contains galaxies with high signal and less patchy appearance (such as NGC 2748 or NGC 5678), and also galaxies with low signal and patchier appearance (e.g., NGC 691 or NGC 918). Consequently, the process of deriving rotation curves is difficult and leads to unsampled radial regions or bad radial sampling. For example, the central regions of the galaxies NGC 428, NGC 4151, NGC 4324, NGC 4639, NGC 5334, NGC 5740 and NGC 5921 are not sampled and can not be studied. Kinematical parameters versus photometric parameters Another caveat (though related to the first point) is the determination of the kinematical parameters i , PA or v_ sys . We explained in Sect. section5 how we have derived our rotation curves using rotcur in GIPSY. Due to the nature of our data, it is not easy to determine the kinematical parameters, and it is more often reliable to use other types of data, with full spatial coverage and full spatial sampling albeit at lower angular resolution. Thus, in our fits we have used H i derived parameters, whenever possible, as initial conditions. We also use the photometric information provided by the ellipse fitting analysis on the S ^ 4 G 3.6 m images by Mu noz-Mateos et al. (in prep), although our results do not always match the photometric results (see Table rotcuroutput for a comparison). Regarding the PA, the PV diagrams and the position of the minor axis in our velocity maps are good tracers of the kinematical PA, so the results from rotcur describe the gas motion better than the photometric results. There is one case with a high discrepancy: NGC 4151 with PA _ 3.6 =150.6 and PA _ H =20 3 (the real difference is the same as with PA _ H =180 +20 =200 , that is 49.4 ). After an inspection of the continuum-subtracted cube, and also by looking at the velocity map (panel b of Fig. A8 bottom) we have found that the PA is closer to 0 , very different from the 150 derived from the S ^ 4 G image. Bosma1977 discussed the first H i synthesis maps of this galaxy, along with a deep photograph by Arp showing the outer spiral. They found that the PA _ kin =19 4 , much closer to our PA _ H than to PA _ phot . We confirm Bosma's conclusion: the photometric image detects only the oval distribution of the galaxy. In the same way, there are other galaxies in the sample where the mid-IR morphology results in PA _ phot different from PA _ kin , but our PA _ kin agree with previous values of PA _ kin found in the literature. These are presented in Table pacomparison . All the galaxies in Table pacomparison are barred (NGC 2805 is classified as SA in the CVRHS, but is claimed to host a bar in the 2D structural decomposition of the S ^ 4 G images, Salo2015 ). Therefore, the differences between the photometric and kinematic PA may well be explained with the presence of the bar which, depending on its orientation, influences the kinematics in the bar region and causes deviations from pure circular motions (see Sect. ncm ). Also, irregular outer spiral arms (such in NGC 6412) can influence on the measurements of PA _ phot , and we conclude that PA _ kin trace the real PA better than PA _ phot . As for the inclination, the largest discrepancies occur in NGC 4151 (already discussed), and in NGC 2748 and NGC 3504, where the inclinations as derived from the kinematic data are substantially higher than those derived from the surface photometry. This can be due to the non-uniform distribution of the H ii regions as compared to the distribution of the stars and dust as seen in the S ^ 4 G 3.6 m images. NGC 2748 shows a bulge at faint light levels, hence the discrepancy in inclination. NGC 3504 has a low inclination, seen in the S 4 G image . Also, NGC 2748 and NGC 3504 host an outer ring (R' and R' _ 1 , respectively), not seen in H . Comparison with literature data In the Introduction, we mentioned the H FP kinematic surveys of Garrido2002 (GHASP), Daigle2006a (SINGS sample) and Chemin2006 (VIRGO sample). These surveys have used instrumentation similar to GH FaS: Cigale and FaNTOmM (Fabry–Perot de Nouvelle Technologie de l'Observatoire du Mont M egantic http://www.astro.umontreal.ca/fantomm ). These were mounted at different telescopes: the 3.6 m Canada-France-Hawaii Telescope (VIRGO and SINGS), the 3.6 m European Southern Observatory telescope (VIRGO) , the 1.93 m Observatoire de Haute-Provence telescope (VIRGO, SINGS and GHASP), and the 1.60 m Observatoire du Mont M egantic telescope (VIRGO and SINGS). All these data are seeing-limited, but taken under observing conditions worse than ours (less than 2" seeing in the very best cases, but with seeing values of 8"-12" in the worst cases). In Table tablecomparison , we show the different quality characteristics of these surveys as compared to our GH FaS data. The reduction techniques used in these surveys are based on the University of Montr eal Improved 3D Fabry-Perot Data Reduction Techniques http://www.astro.umontreal.ca/ odaigle/reduction/ and are summarised in Daigle2006 . The most important reduction steps that differ from ours are spectral and spatial smoothing. The three surveys smooth the data spectrally (that is, in the wavelength direction), with three-channel or Hanning-filtering smoothing. Also, they use the Voronoi adaptive binning method Cappellari2003 , which consists of gathering as many pixels as necessary in order to achieve a threshold value of the signal-to-noise ratio for the considered bin. Hence, the Voronoi method allows maximum spatial resolution for regions with strong emission and good signal-to-noise values for regions with low emission. On the contrary and with the goal of keeping the highest spectral and angular resolution possible throughout, we have not smoothed the data spectrally and applied a median spatial smoothing of 3 3 pixels. These reduction procedures have been chosen following our scientific goals of studying the inner parts of the rotation curves and studying streaming motions within the different galaxy substructures. In Fig. comparisoghasp , we compare the velocity fields of three galaxies in the overlap of our sample with that of GHASP (NGC 864, NGC 2500 and NGC 2543) and two galaxies in the overlap with the VIRGO sample (NGC 4498 and NGC 4639). We compare the velocity fields as obtained from the GH FaS, GHASP and VIRGO data by different reduction methods: ours, as explained in Sect. section4 , and following the reduction techniques used for the GHASP and VIRGO surveys (three-channel spectral smoothing and Voronoi adaptive binning, see Chemin2006 and Epinat2008 ) . In these figures, it is possible to compare directly the advantages and disadvantages of each reduction procedure. On the one hand, we see that one drawback of following the reduction steps of Sect. section4 is that the spatial coverage is limited to the location of the H ii regions, resulting in patchy maps. But on the other hand, Voronoi smoothing reduces the angular resolution often to that typical of H i data (6"-10"). With the results from the adaptive binning, it is very difficult to perform studies inside the structures of the galaxies (such as bars or spiral arms), as they cannot be resolved any more. This includes the study of the streaming motions (this paper), and of the inner parts of the rotation curves (Paper III). As explained in Sect. section8 , we publish our raw and basic reduced data, and any worker interested in smoothed versions of our data sets, either spectrally or Voronoi, can simply download the data and perform their own favourite recipes. In Fig. comparisoghasp2 we present the velocity field of the central part of NGC 2543 to show the differences between the GH FaS and GHASP data, both reduced following the techniques explained in the current paper. The differences arising from the different pixel scale (0 2 of GH FaS against 0 68 of GHASP) and different seeing conditions ( 1 3 for our observations against 3 7 for GHASP) can clearly be seen. To see the possible impact of the different reduction processes, we also compare the rotation curves of the overlapping galaxies. In Fig. rotcurcomparison we superimpose the rotation curves from GHASP and VIRGO papers onto our rotation curves for the galaxies in Fig. comparisoghasp . Erroz-Ferrer2012 give a direct comparison between the rotation curve of NGC 864 from our data and that of GHASP, so this galaxy has not been included in Fig. rotcurcomparison . For completeness, we compare the rotation curves of the remaining overlapping galaxies in Figs. B1-B4. There are many similarities and the rotation curves agree reasonably. The possible discrepancies mainly come from the different parameters ( v_ sys , i and PA) used for the fits to the velocity fields. Also, the spatial coverage resulting from the different reduction techniques is reflected in the rotation curves: smoothing kernels such as Voronoi help to highlight the emission in the low-signal regions, but compromise the spatial resolution. Non-circular motions One of the aims of this kinematical study is to analyse the influence of the galaxies' main structural features on their kinematics. In other words, we want to study the kinematical footprints of the components of the galaxies, traced for instance by the non-circular motions. Deviations from pure circular motion have been widely studied previously in the literature. The first studies of streaming motions induced by the spiral density waves were presented by Bosma1978 , Visser1980 and Rots1990 using H i data; by Ichikawa1985 , Clemens1985 and Cepa1992 using CO data, and by vanderKruit1976 and Marcelin1985 using H data. They confirm what Roberts1969b had predicted theoretically: variations of 10-30 km s ^ 1 in the velocity are found when the gas crosses the density wave. Bar-induced non-circular motions have also been analysed in the literature, and the first studies were performed by Peterson1978 , Duval1983 and Pence1988 using optical data; and by Sancisi1979 and Gottesman1984 using H i data. They found that the isovelocity contours tend to go parallel to the bar rather than parallel to the minor axis. Also, they found some cases where these deviations from the circular motion are more prominent when the PA _ BAR is at 45 from the kinematic major axis (in agreement with the models by, e.g., vanAlbada1981 ). The nature and characterisation of these non-circular motions is a topic still under debate, and many studies of the streaming motions have been performed (e.g., Knapen2000 ; Fresneau2005 ; Spekkens2007 ; Castillo-Morales2007 ; Shetty2007 ; Tamburro2008 ; Garcia-Burillo2009 ; Sellwood2010 ; Meidt2013 ; Font2014b ). In Sect. section6 , we explained how we computed a non-circular motions map, the residual velocity field. These residual velocity fields depend significantly on the derived rotation, that is, on the rotation curve derived from the observed velocity field. Consequently, a rigorous analysis of the output of the rotcur task in gipsy has been carried out. We want to study both non-circular motions caused by the spiral density waves (hereafter spiral-induced non-circular motions) and those caused by the gravitational potential of the bar (bar-induced non-circular motions). The galaxies in our sample span a wide variety of morphological types and features, so a statistical approach to the non-circular motions is possible, though not straightforward. Bar-induced non-circular motions We are interested in analysing kinematically the effects of secular evolution by investigating the effects of the presence of a bar on the rotation of the galaxies. Our kinematic data allow us to study the kinematics and the star formation at the same time, with the caveat that we are only observing the star-forming regions. Our first goal is to understand the connection between the presence of a bar and the kinematics within the bar region. Our second goal is to study the link to star formation within the bar. Bars are usually dominated by old population stars, and in some cases do not show H emission (e.g., in the SB galaxies NGC 4639, NGC 5334 or NGC 5921). From the 12 SAB and 7 SB galaxies in our sample, the bar region is clearly defined in H in 11 of them. In most of these (NGC 864, NGC 1073, NGC 2500, NGC 2748, NGC 2805, NGC 4151, NGC 4389, NGC 4498, NGC 5112, NGC 6207 and NGC 6412) the bar shows H emission, and there are non-circular motions along the bar (see Table ncmtable ). Although the bar is not obvious in H , using the method shown in Fig. cdf we can in fact measure non-circular motions in three further galaxies: NGC 428, NGC 918 and NGC 3504 (Table ncmtable ). In the other galaxies (NGC 2543, NGC 2712, NGC 5740 and NGC 5921) the bar itself does not appear in H , but the surrounding regions (mostly the SAR) show large non-circular motions. We have also measured the non-circular motions in the SAR in all other barred galaxies, and along the spiral arms of all galaxies. NGC 4324 is a lenticular galaxy, so there we have measured the residual velocities along the ring instead of the arms, finding values of 16.2 3.2 km s ^ 1 . The PV diagrams along the minor axis presented in Appendix C are the first clue to see that there are deviations from circular motion. As illustrated for NGC 864 in Paper I, in the absence of non-circular motions, the velocity profile along the minor axis should be completely flat. For most of the barred galaxies in our sample, there are deviations from the systemic velocity, presented as peaks or troughs in the velocity profile along the minor axis. These indicate that some gas along the minor axis is not following the rotational pattern of the galaxy. As we see in NGC 864 (Fig. C5), NGC 3504 (Fig. C6) or NGC 5678 (Fig. C7), the deviations from the systemic velocity are found beyond the extent of the bar. This implies that the non-circular motions outside the bar region (i.e., in the SAR) can be a result of the influence of both potentials (bar and spiral arms). In Fig. deltapaplots , we represent the residual velocities in the bar region (and normalised) as a function of the difference between the major axis of the bar and that of the kinematic major axis, PA. We see that the amplitude of the non-circular motions here does not increase as PA approaches 45 , contrary to the expectation. Fig. ncm1 (left) shows the residual velocities in the three regions -bar (Fig. ncm1 a) , SAR (Fig. ncm1 d) and spiral arms (Fig. ncm1 g) as a function of the bar strength, quantified by the torque parameter Q_ b . Q_ b is strongly reacting to the bulge: a stronger bulge dilutes the tangential force from the bar and lowers Q_ b . It is not necessarily true that the bar is weaker, just that motions are more controlled by the spherical potential of the bulge. Therefore, to distinguish the influence of the bulge in the bar strength, we have also studied the non-circular motions as a function of the bulge-to-total ratio ( B/T ) in the middle plots of Fig. ncm1 : bar (Fig. ncm1 b), SAR (Fig. ncm1 e) and spiral arms (Fig. ncm1 h) . Also, in all the plots, we have presented the galaxies with B/T < 0.1 with blue asterisks, galaxies with 0.1 <B/T< 0.2 with red triangles, and galaxies with B/T> 0.2 (bulge dominated) with squares. We list the Q_ b and B/T values for the galaxies of our sample in Table ncmtable . In plots Fig. ncm1 (a,b,,d,e,f,g) we only represent those cases where a galaxy has a bar (i.e., Q_ b 0). The same plots as in Fig. ncm1 have been created for the normalised residual velocities, and are presented in Fig. ncm1bis . Analysing the left hand plots of Figs. ncm1 and ncm1bis (a,d,g) , we conclude that Q_ b does not correlate with the amplitude of the non-circular motions. Also, the middle plots of Figs. ncm1 and ncm1bis (b,e,f) show no correlation between the amplitude of the non-circular motions and the presence of a bulge. Although the few cases with a significant bulge ( B/T>0.1 ), show that the residual velocities in the bar decrease as B/T increases, more data would be needed to prove whether the bulge is constraining the velocities to remain circular. We have measured the luminosities from the three regions identified previously in the galaxies of the sample (bar, SAR and spiral arms), and derived the SFRs in those regions using the equations presented in Sect. section7 . In panels Fig. ncm1 and Fig. ncm1bis , we see a slight tendency that the higher non-circular motions correspond to star forming bars, or to bars that show more H emission (note that the scale in SFR is logarithmic). This is surprising as in the literature, the high shear and shocks in the bar region prevent star formation in the models (e.g., Athanassoula1992b ) and observations (e.g., Zurita2004 and Castillo-Morales2007 ), where star formation is inhibited in the bar region and the non-circular motions anti-correlate with the H luminosity there. This would cause a bias in our study, as we might not see in H those bars with higher non-circular motions. Analysing also panels f and i of Figs. Fig. ncm1 and ncm1bis , we see that the SFR and the amplitude of the non-circular motions do not correlate. We previously stated that some galaxies show H emission along their bar, and others do not. Why does that happen? In the literature, many studies regarding the star formation along bars have been carried out, but with no clear answer. (i) As we stated before, the strong shear and parallel shocks limit the formation of stars (e.g., Athanassoula1992b ; Reynaud1998 ; Zurita2004 ), because the giant molecular clouds can be pulled apart ( Downes1996 ; Schinnerer2002 ). On the other hand, H ii regions have been found under conditions of high shear stress and shocks (e.g., Martin1997 ; Sheth2002 ; Zurita2008 ). Martinet1997 found that the highest SFRs along the bar correspond to the stronger and longer bars in their sample of isolated late-type barred galaxies. In Fig. sfrvsqb , we present the resulting SFR values within the bar and SAR of our sample galaxies as a function of Q_ b , a parameter which quantifies the strength of the bar and consequently that of shocks/shear. From Fig. sfrvsqb we conclude that the bar strength does not determine the presence or quantity of H emission along the bar. In our sample, we see that the strongest bars ( Q_ b,NGC1073 =0.63 and Q_ b,NGC5112 =0.62) show H emission, but further study is needed to see if this emission is located in the regions of high shocks/shear. (ii) Garcia-Barreto1996 found that the barred galaxies with Hubble types SBa or earlier in their sample did not show H emission, probably due to the low gas content available for inflow (which is not the case, for example, for NGC 4151) so the morphological type is not in principle the only reason. (iii) Martin1997 also found that the two highest SFRs correspond to the galaxies which present the strongest signs of recent interaction or merging (NGC 4731 and NGC 7479). (iv) Sheth2000 came to the conclusion that the stars in NGC 5383 may have been formed in the spurs before the gas encounters the dust lane, and travel ballistically through the shock at the dust lane, ionizing the regions located at the leading side of the dust lane. (v) Verley2007 suggested that the presence of star formation in the central region and along bars is a consequence of an evolutive sequence , that goes from galaxies with H in the bar to galaxies with H emission in the circumnuclear region but not within the bar, ending in galaxies without any emission either along the bar or in a central knot. We conclude that there is no simple answer to the question why some bars have current star formation and others don't . The answer is probably a combination of the previous features. With our sample of galaxies, we find that neither the Hubble type nor the bar strength determine the presence of H emission within the bar region, but larger samples are needed to establish well-based statistical results on the SF within bars. Furthermore, deeper and higher-resolution kinematic data (e.g., from the Atacama Large Millimeter/submillimeter Array, ALMA) would enable the study of the physical conditions in the bar related to the formation of stars at the scales of the molecular clouds. Then, it may be possible to understand if a recent interaction or merger is causing the star formation of the bar, if the galaxy has already used up the available gas to be transformed into stars, or if the location of the shocks and shear correspond to regions of diminished star formation. Spiral-induced non-circular motions In the bar region, the mechanisms triggering star formation may be different from those in spiral arms, due to the different dynamics and shock conditions. In Fig. ncm2 , we show star formation within the spiral arms and the amplitude of the non-circular motions there as a function of the arm class, a parameter which might plausibly be related to the ``strength" of the spiral arms. We do not find any trend or correlation. Elmegreen1986 found that the SFR per unit area does not depend on the arm class, and we confirm this. Therefore, we agree with other studies which confirm that the SFR does not depend on the strength of the arms ( Dobbs2009 ; Foyle2010 ), and disagree with those that confirm the opposite (e.g., Seigar2002 ; Clarke2006 ). The presence and magnitude of streaming motions in the arms seem to be a local phenomenon, unrelated to the SFR or arm class. The high amplitude of the non-circular motions found in NGC 5678 ( with a representative value of 70 km s ^ 1 above the average 20-30 km s ^ 1 for spiral-induced non-circular motions) leads us to consider that a spiral cannot be the single cause, and that this galaxy could be barred.

The high amplitude of the non-circular motions found in NGC 5678 ( with a representative value of 70 km s ^ 1 above the average 20-30 km s ^ 1 for spiral-induced non-circular motions) leads us to consider that a spiral cannot be the single cause, and that this galaxy could be barred. This galaxy was classified as SAB in RC3 and argued to be barred by Ganda2007 , but the CVRHS reclassified it as SA from an S ^ 4 G mid-IR image and therefore does not have a value for Q_ b . The PV diagram along the minor axis (Fig. C7) shows the deviations from the circular motions, very similar to those created by the potential of the bar in NGC 864 (Paper 1). We conclude that the non-circular motions in NGC 5678 are an evidence supporting the claim that NGC 5678 is a barred galaxy, and the high amplitude of those non-circular motions could be bar-induced rather than only spiral-induced. Another interpretation could be that there was a recent minor merger.

gmw_00006159.name: XLS-9
gmw_00006159.overview: XLS-9 is a high-redshift Lyman-alpha emission line galaxy [arXiv:2102.07779]. Its spectrum lacks detected non-resonant emission lines, requiring its systemic redshift to be estimated from the red peak of the Lyman-alpha line [arXiv:2102.07779].

sources.summary: The paper says XLS-9 lacks detected non-resonant lines, has a Ly-alpha-based aperture and red-peak redshift, and is excluded from uniform analyses.

quotes.quote: For three objects (XLS-9, 14 and 22) we use a Ly based aperture as the continuum is not detected with sufficient S/N.

For XLS-9 and XLS-13 we were not able to detect any emission-line within the full spatial area covered by the slit besides Ly in rest-frame wavelengths =1000-7000 . We note that a faint blue Ly peak may be seen in XLS-13 at 300 km s ^ 1 from the red Ly line, suggesting the systemic redshift is at 150 km s ^ 1 from the peak of the red Ly line (Fig. fig:Lyaspec ). In order to understand why no rest-frame optical lines are detected, we determine upper limits of the strongest lines H and [ O iii ] assuming a single gaussian with line-width FWHM of 150 km s ^ 1 and velocity shifts of -300 to -100 km s ^ 1 with respect to Ly . The limiting flux of [ O iii ] strongly depends on this shift while the expected wavelength for H is less affected by skylines. The limiting H fluxes can be translated into a lower limit of the Ly escape fraction (assuming zero attenuation). For XLS-9 and XLS-13 we find 2 limiting escape fractions of >59 and >131 .
* For XLS-9 and XLS-13 we list the redshifts of the red peak of the Ly line as we do not detect a non-resonant emission-line in their spectra.

gmw_00006160.name: XLS-13
gmw_00006160.overview: XLS-13 is a high-redshift emission-line galaxy that exhibits Lyman-alpha emission [arXiv:2102.07779]. The galaxy displays a possible faint blue Lyman-alpha peak, though its rest-frame optical lines, including H-alpha and [O III], remain undetected [arXiv:2102.07779].

sources.summary: The paper reports only Ly-alpha for XLS-13, a possible faint blue Ly-alpha peak, skyline-limited [O III], and an unexpectedly high H-alpha non-detection limit.

quotes.quote: For XLS-9 and XLS-13 we were not able to detect any emission-line within the full spatial area covered by the slit besides Ly in rest-frame wavelengths =1000-7000 . We note that a faint blue Ly peak may be seen in XLS-13 at 300 km s ^ 1 from the red Ly line, suggesting the systemic redshift is at 150 km s ^ 1 from the peak of the red Ly line (Fig. fig:Lyaspec ). In order to understand why no rest-frame optical lines are detected, we determine upper limits of the strongest lines H and [ O iii ] assuming a single gaussian with line-width FWHM of 150 km s ^ 1 and velocity shifts of -300 to -100 km s ^ 1 with respect to Ly . The limiting flux of [ O iii ] strongly depends on this shift while the expected wavelength for H is less affected by skylines. The limiting H fluxes can be translated into a lower limit of the Ly escape fraction (assuming zero attenuation). For XLS-9 and XLS-13 we find 2 limiting escape fractions of >59 and >131 .

This implies that the non-detection of H is not particularly unexpected for XLS-9, but we would expect an H detection for XLS-13 with a S/N >2.7 . This slight discrepancy can be a statistical noise effect or indicate that the H line-width of XLS-13 is broader (resulting in a higher flux limit). Regarding the (typically) brighter [ O iii ] lines we note that for the typical velocity shift of 150 km s ^ 1 with respect to Ly , the location of the redshifted [ O iii ] lines of XLS-9 and XLS-13 are both heavily affected by skylines, plausibly explaining their non-detection.

gmw_00006161.name: XLS-25
gmw_00006161.overview: The high-redshift emission line galaxy XLS-25 has high-resolution imaging available from the Hubble Space Telescope ACS [arXiv:2102.07779]. Its [O III] line profile is characterized by multiple components, visually showing two narrow components in addition to a broad component [arXiv:2102.07779].

sources.summary: The paper notes XLS-25 has HST imaging and a multi-component line profile, using it as an example of simultaneous [O III] fitting under skyline contamination.

quotes.quote: XLS-25 and 28 have high-resolution HST /ACS imaging from the GEMS survey Rix2004 . We compile size measurements from and we use the multi-wavelength ground-based photometry in >30 filters from the MUSYC survey Cardamone2010 .
Two galaxies (XLS-25 and 35) visually show two narrow-components in addition to a broad component, see Fig. fig:O3_examples .
We fit three components for XLS-25 and 35 (two narrow components and a broad one).

gmw_00006162.name: J1406507
gmw_00006162.overview: J1406507 is a candidate changing-look narrow-line Seyfert 1 galaxy [arXiv:2202.04851]. It has exhibited spectral changes, including variations in its hydrogen emission line width and the appearance of an iron emission line complex [arXiv:2202.04851].

sources.summary: The paper presents J1406507 as a changing-look NLS1 candidate whose line width, Fe emission complex, and WISE/ZTF variability distinguish it from other CLAGN.

quotes.quote: J1406507 - A CLNLS1 J1406507 is potentially the second reported CLNLS1 with an actual variation to the emission line width. J1406507 is a type-2 in 6dFGS with a H line width of FWHM 500 km / s. The WiFeS spectrum shows a NLS1 with a H FWHM 2400 km / s.

In addition, there is also the appearance of the Fe emission line complex redward and blueward of the H NLS11 region, which is a prominent feature of NLS1 and a change that we do not observe in other CLAGN. Compared to CLAGN, there are not many cases of CLNLS1. A few of the current reported potential cases do not include the appearance of the broad lines, but rather a strong increase in flux. These include PS16dtm blanchard17 , CSS100217 drake11 and J1236511 HonCL . The only other case with an appearance of broad lines is SDSS J123359.12+084211.5 macleodb . Variations observed in PS16dtm and CSS100217 are not believed to be caused by AGN activity and there was limited information about the other two. When we observed the object with WiFeS, the WISE W1 magnitude had dimmed by almost 1 magnitude since MJD= 55000 (see figure res:clnls1:lc ). The ZTF light curve instead shows that the source has brighten by over 1 magnitude just after our WiFeS observation.

gmw_00006163.name: J121345.22+363805.8
gmw_00006163.overview: J121345.22+363805.8 is a dwarf galaxy hosting a hard X-ray source with a luminosity of 10^39.5 erg/s [arXiv:2310.02999]. This source has been interpreted as either a low-luminosity active galactic nucleus or a ultraluminous X-ray source powered by a stellar-mass black hole [arXiv:2310.02999].

sources.summary: The paper gives a spectral case study of J121345.22+363805.8, fitting its X-ray spectrum and weighing a low-luminosity AGN interpretation against a ULX origin.

quotes.quote: Of those, only one (J121345.22+363805.8, hereafter J1213+3638) had sufficient signal-to-noise to perform robust spectral fitting. This source had 3500 counts in each of the MOS1 and MOS2 images. The pn data were heavily affected by background flares and therefore are not taken into account for this analysis.

The best-fit power law photon index was determined to be =1.92 0.06 , with an associated normalization of (8.3 0.4) 10^ 4 photons keV ^ 1  cm ^ 2  s ^ 1 . While the target's hard X-ray luminosity of 10^ 39.5  erg s ^ 1 could be suggestive of a low luminosity AGN, this source was previously reported as a ULX powered by a stellar mass black hole e.g., ghosh2021 . The best-fit model returned an intrinsic N_ H column density of (2.26 0.26) 10^ 21  cm ^ 2 .

gmw_00006164.name: HAQ J0015 + 1129
gmw_00006164.overview: HAQ J0015 + 1129 is a reddened quasar located at a redshift of approximately 0.87 [arXiv:1510.01708]. Its redshift was determined using the magnesium II emission line, and its spectral energy distribution is well fit by a steep extinction curve [arXiv:1510.01708].

sources.summary: The paper identifies HAQ J0015 + 1129 as a reddened QSO, gives its redshift and spectroscopic line basis, and fits its SED with A_V=0.58 0.13 and R_V=2.66 0.31.

quotes.quote: HAQ J0015 + 1129 This is a reddened QSO from the HAQ survey of paper II at redshift z=0.87 . The spectrum was obtained with the NOT/ALFOSC. The redshift was based on the detection of the Mg ii emission line. The data are well fit with a steeper extinction curve with A_V=0.58 0.13 and R_V=2.66 0.31 .

gmw_00006165.name: HAQ J0129 0059
gmw_00006165.overview: HAQ J0129 0059 is a reddened quasar located at a redshift of approximately 0.71 [arXiv:1510.01708]. Its redshift was determined from the Mg II emission line, and its spectral energy distribution is well fit with an extinction value of 0.74 [arXiv:1510.01708].

sources.summary: The paper identifies HAQ J0129 0059 as a reddened QSO, gives its redshift and spectroscopic line basis, and fits its SED with A_V=0.74 0.17 and R_V=2.41 0.28.

quotes.quote: HAQ J0129 0059 This is a reddened QSO from the HAQ survey of paper I at redshift z=0.71 . The spectrum was obtained with the NTT/EFOSC2. The redshift was estimated from the Mg ii emission line. We modelled the QSO SED, and the data are well fit with A_V=0.74 0.17 and R_V=2.41 0.28 .

gmw_00006166.name: HAQ J0130 + 1439
gmw_00006166.overview: HAQ J0130 + 1439 is a reddened quasar located at a redshift of z = 0.93 [arXiv:1510.01708]. Its redshift was determined from its magnesium II emission line, and its spectral energy distribution is well fit by a dust extinction of A_V = 0.46 [arXiv:1510.01708].

sources.summary: The paper identifies HAQ J0130 + 1439 as a reddened QSO, gives its redshift and spectroscopic line basis, and fits its SED with A_V=0.46 0.12 and R_V=2.23 0.35.

quotes.quote: HAQ J0130 + 1439 This is a reddened QSO from the HAQ survey of paper II at redshift z=0.93 . The spectrum was obtained with the NOT/ALFOSC, and the redshift was derived from the Mg ii emission line. We modelled the QSO SED, and the data are well fit with A_V=0.46 0.12 and R_V=2.23 0.35 . Paper II reported A_V=0.4 from their SED analysis.

gmw_00006167.name: HAQ J0151 + 0618
gmw_00006167.overview: HAQ J0151 + 0618 is a reddened quasar located at a redshift of z=0.95 [arXiv:1510.01708]. Its redshift was determined through the detection of the Mg II emission line, and its spectral energy distribution indicates a steep extinction curve [arXiv:1510.01708].

sources.summary: The paper identifies HAQ J0151 + 0618 as a reddened QSO, gives its redshift and spectroscopic line basis, and fits its SED with A_V=0.51 0.14 and R_V=2.29 0.23.

quotes.quote: HAQ J0151 + 0618 This is a reddened QSO from the HAQ survey of paper II at redshift z=0.95 . The spectrum was obtained with the NOT/ALFOSC. The redshift was based on the detection of the Mg ii emission line. The SED is nicely reproduced with a steep extinction curve with A_V=0.51 0.14 and R_V=2.29 0.23 .

gmw_00006168.name: HAQ J0247 0052
gmw_00006168.overview: HAQ J0247 0052 is a reddened quasar located at a redshift of z=0.825 [arXiv:1510.01708]. Its spectral redshift was determined using magnesium II and oxygen II emission lines, and its spectral energy distribution exhibits a high extinction value [arXiv:1510.01708].

sources.summary: The paper identifies HAQ J0247 0052 as a reddened QSO, gives its redshift and spectroscopic line basis, and fits its SED with A_V=1.00 0.11 and R_V=2.52 0.19.

quotes.quote: HAQ J0247 0052 This is a reddened QSO from the HAQ survey of paper I at redshift z=0.825 . The spectrum was obtained with the NTT/EFOSC2. The redshift was estimated from the Mg ii and O ii emission lines. The SED can be modelled with R_V=2.52 0.19 and a very high extinction value of A_V=1.00 0.11 .

gmw_00006169.name: HAQ J0312 + 0035
gmw_00006169.overview: HAQ J0312 + 0035 is a reddened quasar located at a redshift of z=1.28 [arXiv:1510.01708]. Its spectral energy distribution can be modeled with a steep extinction curve [arXiv:1510.01708].

sources.summary: The paper identifies HAQ J0312 + 0035 as a reddened QSO, gives its redshift and spectroscopic line basis, and fits its SED with A_V=0.50 0.12 and R_V=2.29 0.23.

quotes.quote: HAQ J0312 + 0035 This is a reddened QSO from the HAQ survey of paper I at redshift z=1.28 . The spectrum was obtained with the NTT/EFOSC2. The redshift was derived from the Mg ii and C iii emission lines. The SED can be modelled with a steep extinction curve with A_V=0.50 0.12 and R_V=2.29 0.23 .

gmw_00006170.name: HAQ J0319 + 0623

sources.summary: The paper identifies HAQ J0319 + 0623 as a reddened QSO, gives its redshift and spectroscopic line basis, and fits its SED with A_V=0.20 0.06 and R_V=2.38 0.365.

quotes.quote: HAQ J0319 + 0623 This is a reddened QSO from the HAQ survey of paper II at redshift z=2.10 . The spectrum was obtained with the NOT/ALFOSC, and the redshift was derived from the Si iv + O iv , C iv , and C iii emission lines. The SED can be modelled with a steep extinction curve with A_V=0.20 0.06 and R_V=2.38 0.365 . Paper II reported A_V=0.13 from the SED analysis.

gmw_00006171.name: HAQ J0347 + 0115
gmw_00006171.overview: HAQ J0347 + 0115 is a reddened quasar located at a redshift of approximately 0.98 [arXiv:1510.01708]. Its spectrum was obtained using the NOT/ALFOSC instrument, and its spectral energy distribution is modeled with a steep extinction curve [arXiv:1510.01708].

sources.summary: The paper identifies HAQ J0347 + 0115 as a reddened QSO, gives its redshift and spectroscopic line basis, and fits its SED with A_V=0.29 0.12 and R_V=2.23 0.39.

quotes.quote: HAQ J0347 + 0115 This is a reddened QSO from the HAQ survey of paper II at redshift z=0.98 . The spectrum was obtained with the NOT/ALFOSC. The redshift was estimated from the Mg ii emission line. The SED is modelled well with a steep extinction curve and A_V=0.29 0.12 together with R_V=2.23 0.39 .

gmw_00006172.name: HAQ J1400 + 0219
gmw_00006172.overview: HAQ J1400 + 0219 is a reddened quasar located at a redshift of z=0.86 [arXiv:1510.01708]. Its redshift was determined through the detection of a Mg II emission line, and its spectral energy distribution fits a steep extinction curve [arXiv:1510.01708].

sources.summary: The paper identifies HAQ J1400 + 0219 as a reddened QSO, gives its redshift and spectroscopic line basis, and fits its SED with A_V=0.56 0.09 and R_V=2.16 0.36.

quotes.quote: HAQ J1400 + 0219 This is a reddened QSO from the HAQ survey of paper II at redshift z=0.86 . The spectrum was obtained with the NOT/ALFOSC. The redshift was based on the detection of Mg ii emission line. We found the observed data fit very well with a steep extinction curve and relatively high A_V with A_V=0.56 0.09 and R_V=2.16 0.36 .

sources.summary: The paper identifies HAQ J1409 + 0940 as a reddened QSO, gives its redshift and spectroscopic line basis, and fits its SED with A_V=0.54 0.10 and R_V=2.27 0.26.

quotes.quote: HAQ J1409 + 0940 This is a reddened QSO from the HAQ survey of paper II at redshift z=0.92 . The spectrum was obtained with the NOT/ALFOSC, and the redshift was derived from the relatively weak Mg ii emission line. gmw_00006174.name: HAQ J1527 + 0250
gmw_00006174.overview: HAQ J1527 + 0250 is a reddened broad-absorption line quasar located at a redshift of z=2.13 [arXiv:1510.01708]. Its redshift was determined using carbon emission lines, and its spectral energy distribution fits a steep extinction curve [arXiv:1510.01708].

sources.summary: The paper identifies HAQ J1527 + 0250 as a reddened BAL QSO, gives its redshift and spectroscopic line basis, and fits its SED with A_V=0.21 0.07 and R_V=2.33 0.36.

quotes.quote: HAQ J1527 + 0250 This is a reddened BAL QSO from the HAQ survey of paper II at redshift z=2.13 . The spectrum was obtained with the NOT/ALFOSC. The redshift was estimated from the C iv and C iii emission lines. We found that the observed data fit very well with a steep extinction curve with A_V=0.21 0.07 and R_V=2.33 0.36 .

gmw_00006175.name: HAQ J1606 + 2902
gmw_00006175.overview: HAQ J1606 + 2902 is a reddened broad-absorption line quasar located at a redshift of z=1.82 [arXiv:1510.01708]. Its spectrum displays emission lines of C IV, Fe II, and Mg II, and its spectral energy distribution is modeled with a low extinction value [arXiv:1510.01708].

sources.summary: The paper identifies HAQ J1606 + 2902 as a reddened BAL QSO, gives its redshift and spectroscopic line basis, and fits its SED with A_V=0.26 0.08 and R_V=2.44 0.24.

quotes.quote: HAQ J1606 + 2902 This is a reddened BAL QSO from the HAQ survey of paper II at redshift z=1.82 . The spectrum was obtained with the NOT/ALFOSC, and the redshift was based on the detection of C iv , Fe ii , and Mg ii emission lines. The data are nicely reproduced with a low extinction value of A_V=0.26 0.08 and R_V=2.44 0.24 . Paper II inferred A_V=0.25 from their SED analysis.

gmw_00006176.name: HAQ J1639 + 3157
gmw_00006176.overview: HAQ J1639 + 3157 is a reddened quasar located at a redshift of z=0.82 [arXiv:1510.01708]. Its spectrum, which exhibits a magnesium II emission line, is characterized by a steep extinction curve [arXiv:1510.01708].

sources.summary: The paper identifies HAQ J1639 + 3157 as a reddened QSO, gives its redshift and spectroscopic line basis, and fits its SED with A_V=0.73 0.11 and R_V=2.48 0.18.

quotes.quote: HAQ J1639 + 3157 This is a reddened QSO from the HAQ survey of paper II at redshift z=0.82 . The spectrum was obtained with the NOT/ALFOSC, and the redshift was based on the detection of Mg ii emission line. The data are well described with a steep extinction curve and relatively high extinction value of A_V=0.73 0.11 and R_V=2.48 0.18 . Previously, paper II derived A_V=0.79 .

gmw_00006177.name: HAQ J1643 + 2944
gmw_00006177.overview: HAQ J1643 + 2944 is a reddened quasar located at a redshift of z=1.08 [arXiv:1510.01708]. Its redshift was determined using a relatively weak Mg II emission line, and its spectral energy distribution fits a dust extinction of A_V=0.39 [arXiv:1510.01708].

sources.summary: The paper identifies HAQ J1643 + 2944 as a reddened QSO, gives its redshift and spectroscopic line basis, and fits its SED with A_V=0.39 0.13 and R_V=2.56 0.27.

quotes.quote: HAQ J1643 + 2944 This is a reddened QSO from the HAQ survey of paper II at redshift z=1.08 . The spectrum was obtained with the NOT/ALFOSC. The redshift was derived from the relatively weak Mg ii emission line. The SED of the QSO fits well with A_V=0.39 0.13 and R_V=2.56 0.27 .

gmw_00006178.name: HAQ J2310 + 1117
gmw_00006178.overview: HAQ J2310 + 1117 is a reddened quasar located at a redshift of z = 0.82 [arXiv:1510.01708]. Its spectrum exhibits a weak Mg II emission line, and its observed data fits a steep extinction curve [arXiv:1510.01708].

sources.summary: The paper identifies HAQ J2310 + 1117 as a reddened QSO, gives its redshift and spectroscopic line basis, and fits its SED with A_V=0.57 0.13 and R_V=2.44 0.28.

quotes.quote: HAQ J2310 + 1117 This is a reddened QSO from the HAQ survey of paper II at redshift z=0.82 . gmw_00006179.name: HAQ J2355 0041

sources.summary: The paper identifies HAQ J2355 0041 as a reddened QSO, gives its redshift and spectroscopic line basis, and fits its SED with A_V=0.65 0.13 and R_V=2.54 0.24.

gmw_00006180.name: host galaxy of GRB 011121
gmw_00006180.overview: The host galaxy of GRB 011121 is a subluminous disk-like system located at a redshift of z = 0.36 [arXiv:astro-ph/0611555]. It features young stellar populations with low metallicity and exhibits a high specific star formation rate [arXiv:astro-ph/0611555].

sources.summary: The paper models the host galaxy of GRB 011121 as a subluminous, large disk-like system with young stellar populations, low metallicity, substantial opacity, and high specific star formation.

quotes.quote: We present a detailed study of the host galaxy of GRB 011121 (at z = 0.36 ) based on high-resolution imaging in 5 broad-band, optical and near-infrared filters with HST and VLT/ISAAC. The surface brightness profile of this galaxy is best fitted by a Sersic law with index n 2 - 2.5 and a rather large effective radius ( 7.5 kpc). Both the morphological analysis and the F450W - F702W colour image suggest that the host galaxy of GRB 011121 is either a disk-system with a rather small bulge, or one hosting a central, dust-enshrouded starburst. Hence, we modeled the integrated spectral energy distribution of this galaxy by combining stellar population and radiative transfer models, assuming properties representative of nearby starburst or normal star-forming, Sbc-like galaxies.

A range of plausible fitting solutions indicates that the host galaxy of GRB 011121 has a stellar mass of 3.1 -- 6.9 10^9 M_ , stellar populations with a maximum age ranging from 0.4 to 2 Gyr, and a metallicity ranging from 1 to 29 per cent of the solar value, as a function of the time elapsed since star formation started. As for the opacity, starburst models suggest this galaxy to be nearly as opaque as local starbursts (with an A_V = 0.27 - 0.76 mag). On the other hand, normal star-forming Sbc-like models suggest a central opacity larger than that of local disks by up to a factor of 8, whereas the attenuation along the line of sight is only A_V = 0.12 - 0.57 mag owing to the galaxy's low inclination. For this subluminous galaxy (with L_B/L^ _B = 0.26 ), we determine a model-dependent star formation rate (SFR) of 2.4 -- 9.4 M_  yr^ 1 , which gives a SFR per unit luminosity of 9.2 -- 36.1 M_  yr^ 1  (L_B/L^ _B)^ 1 and a SFR per unit stellar mass of 0.4 -- 2.9 10^ 9  yr^ 1 .

gmw_00006181.name: J0836
gmw_00006181.overview: J0836 is a highly disturbed host galaxy of a high-excitation radio AGN that is involved in major interactions [arXiv:1905.01315]. It is classified as a compact symmetric object with a C-shaped radio structure spanning 1.7 kiloparsecs [arXiv:1411.0361].

sources.summary: The paper classifies B3 0833+442 as a CORALZ CSO with C-shaped VLBI structure, a 1.7 kpc size, and a new narrow redshifted H I absorption profile.

quotes.quote: B3 0833+442 In the CORALZ sample this source is classified as a CSO. However, the 1.6 GHz VLBI image Vries2009 shows a C-shaped radio structure. The LLS of the source is 1.7 kpc. did not find \ in this source. In our observations, we detect a narrow \ profile, slightly redshifted from the systemic velocity.

sources.summary: The paper identifies J0836 as one of eight highly disturbed HERG host galaxies, associating the group with major interactions, high radio powers, and mixed [OIII] luminosities.

quotes.quote: Following cursory visual inspection, however, eight of the galaxies in the sample show clear, highly disturbed morphologies or are involved in interactions with large-scale tidal tails, suggestive of major interactions: J0757, J0836, J0902, J1243, J1257, J1351, J1358 and J1412 (large red stars in Figure  fig:RPvsOIII ). All of these galaxies lie in the half of the sample with the highest radio powers, and hence none lie in the half with the lowest radio powers. Using a two-proportion Z-test, it is found that the null hypothesis that the proportion of highly disturbed galaxies in these two halves of the sample are the same can be rejected at a confidence level of 99.9 per cent (3.3 ). We note that given that there were only eight of such galaxies in the entire sample, a more significant result could not have been observed. Only 5 out of these 8 galaxies lie in the half with the highest [ OIII ] 5007 luminosities, however, with the remaining 3 out of 8 seen to be in the half with the lowest [ OIII ] 5007 luminosities.

gmw_00006188.name: IC 4767
gmw_00006188.overview: IC 4767 is an edge-on disk galaxy [arXiv:astro-ph/0209024]. It features an X-shaped boxy structure with surface density enhancements along its major axis [arXiv:astro-ph/0209024].

sources.summary: The paper uses IC 4767 as an edge-on galaxy with an X-shaped boxy structure whose observed morphology, enhancements, and branch angle resemble its barred-orbit model.

quotes.quote: Studies of individual galaxies give for this angle values ranging from 22 for IC 4767 to 45 for NGC 128 . Pfenniger Friedli (1991), taking as an example the case of IC 4767, claim that this feature is an optical illusion obtained when one uses a particular look-up-table for viewing the image.

Combining the profiles of x1v1 and x2v1 with those of x1v3 and x1v4, we obtain an edge-on profile of model A2 which has a striking similarity with the edge-on galaxy IC 4767 (Fig. ic_Sc2yz a). We point with arrows at the enhancements of the surface density along the major axis in Fig. ic_Sc2yz b. They clearly have their counterparts in the image of the galaxy (Fig. ic_Sc2yz a) and are clearly revealed in the processed image in Whitmore Bell (1988, their Fig. 1c).
In the slow bar model A2 the `X' is made mainly of the x1v1 orbits and this angle is about 27 (Fig. A2yzall b), close to the value Whitmore Bell (1988) give for IC 4767.

gmw_00006189.name: SPRC-40
gmw_00006189.overview: SPRC-40 is a lenticular galaxy featuring a small, blue, tilted ring that is inclined at about 45 degrees to the host stellar disc [arXiv:1904.02513, arXiv:1107.1966]. The system exhibits counter-rotating gas with central star formation, a tidal tail, and shocks generated by collisions between the ring and the disc [arXiv:1904.02513, arXiv:1107.1966].

sources.summary: The paper treats SPRC-40, NGC 5014, as an atypical polar-ring galaxy where a tilted ring, settled counter-rotating gas, and dust-lane shocks alter the ionization pattern.

quotes.quote: The bottom panels in Fig. fig:bpt_14_40 show an exception to the discussed relation: the emission line ratios in the central region of SPRC-40 correspond to the regions, whereas at larger radii, the points lie in the `composite' or `shock' areas on the BPT diagrams. This radial changes of the ionization state is due to the specific morphology of SPRC-40 (NGC 5014) differing from other `classical' polar rings in our sample (Fig. fig:sdss ): the low-contrast ring has a significantly smaller size than the stellar disc. Moreover, the ring is not orthogonal, but tilted on about 45 to the host galaxy. Along the disc major axis ( PA=102 ) in the central region we observed star formation in the accreted gas already settled to the main plain and counter-rotated to the stars (Sec. sec:kin ). Whereas at distances of 8-12'' , the slit crosses the dust lane, that is obviously related to shocks generated by collision between the tilted ring and the disc.

The angular scale in NGC 5014 (the closest galaxy in the sample) is 8--20 times better than that in other studied SPRC galaxies; it gives us a `zooming up view' on the process of the polar/inclined ring interaction with the disc in the circumnuclear region that is not resolved in other cases. Indeed, the SCORPIO-2 long-slit located along the ring major axis ( PA=56 ) does not cross the disc-ring regions of intersection. The corresponded spectra demonstrate the like ionization up to 1.5-2r_e (the blue and green points in Fig. fig:bpt_14_40 , bottom).

sources.summary: The paper describes SPRC-40 as a lenticular galaxy with a small blue inclined ring, extended H I rotation, a tidal tail, and a polar structure likely formed by accretion or capture.

quotes.quote: SPRC-40 (NGC 5014). On the background of a bright lenticular body of the galaxy a small blue ring is visible with a diameter of approximately 45 arcsec ( 3.9 kpc), strongly inclined to the plane of the galaxy, elongated along PA 45 . According to the radio observations of Noordermeer2005 with the WSRT, the HI rotation occurs in the disc, the kinematic axis of which coincides with this blue ring, and its diameter reaches about 15 kpc. The HI distribution also reveals a tidal tail, extending over at least 20 kpc. It is noted that the galaxy belongs to a group, rich in HI clouds, the accretion of which (or a capture of a companion) has formed a polar structure.

gmw_00006190.name: SPRC-14
gmw_00006190.overview: SPRC-14 is a well-resolved polar-ring galaxy [arXiv:1904.02513] that hosts a weak, LINER-type active galactic nucleus [arXiv:1904.02513]. The system exhibits complex kinematics with two distinct spin components [arXiv:1904.02513, arXiv:1107.1966], showing evidence of collisions and shock ionization between gas clouds in the polar ring and the host galaxy disk [arXiv:1904.02513].

sources.summary: The paper presents SPRC-14 as a well-resolved polar-ring galaxy with LINER-like nuclear activity, shock ionization, and evidence for gas-cloud interactions in its multi-spin components.

quotes.quote: A vivid example of the considered relation is the galaxy SPRC-14, its BPT diagrams are shown separately on the top panels in Fig. fig:bpt_14_40 . The polar structure in this galaxy is well resolved; it has one of the largest angular sizes in the sample. We are able to study the gas excitation in the polar ring even along the slit with PA=130 (the host galaxy major axis), because the ring is moderately inclined to the line-of-sight. SPRC-14 contains a weak AGN (of the LINER-type) according to the BPT-diagrams.

However, the shock ionization appears also at the intermediate radial range (0.5--2.5 r_e ) between the nucleus and outward regions in the star-forming polar ring. The observed gas kinematics provides evidence of the interaction between gaseous clouds on the polar orbits and in the host galaxy disc (see notes in Sec. sec:kin ). It is possible that the ionization in the intermediate radii in SPRC-14 is caused not only by an intersection of polar gas clouds with a stellar disc as suggested by Wakamatsu1993 but also by a direct collision of clouds in both multi-spin components.

sources.summary: The paper reports CO and ionized-gas kinematics for SPRC-14, placing its polar ring on the Tully-Fisher diagram where it falls in the range expected for a spherical potential.

quotes.quote: For SPRC-14 the ionized gas data in projection on the sky has a symmetrized maximal line-of-sight rotation velocity of 160 km/s, in good agreement with the CO(1-0) value of 165 24 km/s. The polar ring is, however, asymmetric, and with an extension on the NE side, where the velocity reaches up to Vmax= 262 11 km/s (H ) and 268 5 (NII).

In Fig. fig:TF , one of the PRGs falls in the expected range for a spherical potential (SPRC-14), two are mildly offset (SPRC-69 and SPRC-42), and two are very far from the usual relation, with very broad velocities (SPRC-48 and SPRC-47). This large scatter might indicate different formation mechanisms for the systems, and at least four out of the five should have their dark matter halo aligned with the polar plane. The position of the five PRGs in the TF diagram does not appear related to the mass ratio between the polar disk/ring and the host. The galaxies SPRC-42 and SPRC-69 have polar structures brighter than their hosts, while SPRC-14 and SPRC-48 are comparable, and SPRC-47 is weaker than its host.

sources.summary: SPRC-14 also shows two kinematic components, with gas velocity scatter along the ring major axis and outer stellar velocities that nearly coincide with the ring gas.

quotes.quote: SPRC-14 This galaxy also reveals two kinematic components, but the observed pattern is more complex than in the previous case. The scatter of line-of-sight velocities of gas is maximal along the major axis of the ring PA(1)=35 . Moreover, in the NE half, the ionized gas emission extends almost twice farther than in the opposite direction away from the centre. This slit position almost exactly coincides with the minor axis of the central disc, but line-of-sight velocities of stars are constant only at <4 arcsec, while at large distances they almost coincide with the velocities of gas in the ring, except for the outermost region in the SW part of the section. It means that just like in SPRC-10, we observe the motion of stars, belonging to the ring at a distance from the centre.

gmw_00006191.name: SPRC-60
gmw_00006191.overview: SPRC-60 is a ring galaxy with a distinct central structure [arXiv:1107.1966]. In its central region, the line-of-sight velocity gradient of the stars is nearly zero, while the gas in the surrounding ring exhibits a symmetrical rotation curve [arXiv:1107.1966].

sources.summary: SPRC-60 has a velocity pattern similar to SPRC-39, with nearly zero central stellar velocity gradient, ring stars visible outward, and symmetric gas rotation in the ring.

quotes.quote: SPRC-60 There is only one section here as well, revealing a picture close to that, described above for SPRC-39. In the central part, the gradient of line-of-sight velocities of stars is nearly zero. We can see the stars, belonging to the ring at increasing distances from the nucleus. Unlike in SPRC-39, the rotation curve of gas in the ring is symmetrical here on both sides from the centre.

gmw_00006192.name: J1145-0033
gmw_00006192.overview: J1145-0033 is a lobe-dominated giant radio quasar located at a redshift of z=2.055 [arXiv:1104.0488]. It exhibits a Fanaroff-Riley II radio morphology spanning a projected linear size of 1.34 Mpc, alongside broad absorption lines in its optical spectrum [arXiv:1104.0488].

sources.summary: The paper presents J1145-0033 as a z=2.055 lobe-dominated giant radio quasar with 1.34 Mpc FR II radio structure, broad absorption lines, high black-hole mass, and low accretion rate.

quotes.quote: We present J1145-0033, a candidate for the most distant (z=2.055) lobe-dominated giant radio quasar, with a projected linear size of 1.34 Mpc. This quasar has both FR II -- type radio morphology and broad absorption lines in its optical spectrum. Some physical characteristics (e.g. black hole mass, accretion rate, equipartition magnetic field, energy density and particle density of ambient medium) based on the optical and radio data are provided. We have also found that the quasar has a relatively large central black hole mass and a very small accretion rate in comparison with similar objects.

gmw_00006193.name: Crater
gmw_00006193.overview: Crater is a dwarf galaxy [arXiv:1506.03615] that acts as a satellite of the Milky Way [arXiv:1407.2612]. It is spatially and kinematically associated with the Leo IV and Leo V galaxies [arXiv:1506.03615], and it is situated close to the Vast Polar Structure [arXiv:1407.2612].

sources.summary: The paper argues Crater is best interpreted as a dwarf galaxy, based on member-star velocities, metallicity, age structure, possible younger stars, and spatial-kinematic association with Leo IV and Leo V.

quotes.quote: The radial velocities imply that both stars are members of the Crater stellar system. The difference in velocity between the two taken at face value implies a velocity dispersion > 3.7 at a 95 confidence level. Our spectroscopic metallicities agree excellently well with those determined by previous investigations using photometry.

Our deep photometry and the spectroscopic metallicity imply an age of 7 Gyr for the main population of the system. The stars to the blue of the turn-off can be interpreted as a younger population that is of the same metallicity and an age of 2.2 Gyr. Finally, spatial and kinematical parameters support the idea that this system is associated with the galaxies Leo IV and Leo V. All the observations favour the interpretation of Crater as a dwarf galaxy.

sources.summary: The paper treats Crater as a Milky Way satellite galaxy/globular-cluster transition object near the VPOS, predicts its proper motion, and shows it improves VPOS-plane alignment.

quotes.quote: We test whether recently discovered objects in the MW halo, the satellite galaxy/globular cluster transition object named PSO J174.0675-10.8774 or Crater and three stellar streams, are part of the VPOS. Crater is situated close to the VPOS. Incorporating the new object in the VPOS-plane fit slightly improves the alignment of the plane with other features such as the Magellanic stream and the average orbital plane of the satellites co-orbiting in the VPOS. We predict Crater's proper motion by assuming that it, too, orbits in the VPOS.

Crater is offset by 39.1 1.3 kpc from this VPOS-3 plane. If Crater is added as a satellite to that plane fit, the offset reduces to 31.4 0.8 kpc, approximately 1.5 times the best-fit plane's root-mean-square height If Crater is at a distance of 145 kpc, these offsets are reduced to 57.4 2.1 kpc from the VPOSall, 34.7 1.3 kpc from VPOS-3, and 29.1 0.8 kpc from VPOS-3 if Crater is included in the plane fit. .

sources.summary: The paper prioritizes Voggel probabilities for Crater I, keeps ten members, and notes that high surface brightness and deep spectroscopy limit contaminant and missing-candidate concerns.

quotes.quote: Crater I For this system we give higher priority to the continuous probabilities by Voggel_16 . If that is not available we use the binary classification of Kirby_15 . Ten members stars remain after the membership selection. Due to Crater I relative high surface brightness, pollution by contaminant stars in not on issue. Because the available spectroscopy is deep and rather complete there are no additional member candidates with Gaia DR2 information only.

gmw_00006194.name: Pisces A
gmw_00006194.overview: Pisces A is a faint, gas-rich dwarf irregular galaxy that exhibits slow rotation and is primarily supported by random motions [arXiv:1511.01484, arXiv:1601.00494]. Its neutral hydrogen gas displays a disturbed morphology, which likely results from interactions with the intergalactic medium [arXiv:2009.09145].

sources.summary: The paper maps Pisces A's H I with the JVLA, finding ordered rotation plus disturbed gas aligned with Local Void filamentary structure, favoring interaction with the IGM over a past companion encounter.

quotes.quote: We present synthesis imaging of one such galaxy, Pisces A, a low-mass dwarf originally confirmed via optical imaging and spectroscopy of neutral hydrogen ( ) sources in the Galactic Arecibo L-band Feed Array \ (GALFA- ) survey. Using \ observations taken with the Karl G. Jansky Very Large Array (JVLA), we characterize the kinematic structure of the gas and connect it to the galaxy's environment and evolutionary history. While the galaxy shows overall ordered rotation, a number of kinematic features indicate a disturbed gas morphology. These features are suggestive of a tumultuous recent history, and represent of the total baryonic mass. We find a total baryon fraction f_ bary = if we include these features.

We also quantify the cosmic environment of Pisces A, finding an apparent alignment of the disturbed gas with nearby, large scale filamentary structure at the edge of the Local Void. We consider several scenarios for the origin of the disturbed gas, including gas stripping via ram pressure or galaxy-galaxy interactions, as well as accretion and ram pressure compression. Though we cannot rule out a past interaction with a companion, our observations best support the suggestion that the neutral gas morphology and recent star formation in Pisces A is a direct result of its interactions with the IGM.

sources.summary: The paper treats Pisces A as a faint, gas-rich isolated dwarf whose low luminosity, rotation velocity, continuing star formation, and BTFR placement challenge standard dwarf-formation models.

quotes.quote: Leo P giovanelli13 and Pisces A tollerud15 are the latest additions to a growing list of faint, gas-rich, isolated dwarf galaxies; a list that dates back at least to the discovery of Leo T irwin07 . These galaxies have luminosities of only a few 10^5 L_ ryanweber08,mcquinn13,tollerud15 and masses 3-4 times higher than their stellar mass. Rotation velocities, derived from radio observations of the , are estimated at v_ rot 12.5 km s^ 1 Leo T: ryanweber08 , v_ rot 17 km s^ 1 Pisces A: tollerud15 and v_ rot 18 km s^ 1 Leo P: giovanelli13,bernsteincooper14 . The available data suggest that all three dwarfs formed stars continuously, although at a very low and highly variable rate clementini12, weisz12, mcquinn13, mcquinn15 .
At a circular velocity of 15 km s^ 1 , comparable to Leo T, Leo P, and Pisces A, over 90 of all halos is predicted to be dark sawala14 .

In short, our simulations including feedback succeed in creating faint, gas-dominated dwarf galaxies lying on the BTFR, such as Leo T, Leo P, and Pisces A, while the control simulations without feedback do not.

sources.summary: KAT-7 observations show Pisces A is a gas-rich dwarf irregular with slow rotation, random-motion support, a 233 velocity midpoint, and kinematics suggesting it is not yet in equilibrium.

quotes.quote: The small rotation velocities detected of 5 \ and 10 \ in Pisces A B respectively, and their \ content show that they are really dwarf irregular galaxies (dIrr). Despite that small rotation component, it is more the random motions 9-11 \ that provide most of the gravitational support, especially in the outer parts. The study of their kinematics, especially the strong gradients of random motions, suggest that those two dwarf galaxies are not yet in equilibrium.

Mid-point velocities (50 level) of 233 3 \ and 617 3 \ are found for Pisces A and Pisces B, respectively. These can be compared to 236 0.5 \ and 615 1 \ found by TOL15. The profile widths at the 50 levels are V _ A = 28 3 \ and V _ B = 41 3 , compared to V _ A = 22.5 1.3 \ and V _ B = 43 3 \ for TOL15.

gmw_00006195.name: DDO 161
gmw_00006195.overview: DDO 161 is a gas-rich dwarf galaxy located at a distance of approximately 6.03 Mpc [arXiv:1704.07648]. It forms an isolated physical pair with the dwarf galaxy UGCA 319 [arXiv:1704.07648], and its neutral hydrogen extent is nearly five times larger than its optical size [arXiv:astro-ph/0301492].

sources.summary: The paper reports HST/ACS TRGB distances for the gas-rich dwarf DDO 161, finding it forms an isolated physical pair with UGCA 319 and has positive peculiar velocity toward Virgo.

quotes.quote: firstpage - lastpage 2016 We report HST /ACS observations of two nearby gas-rich dwarf galaxies: DDO 161 and UGCA 319. Their distances determined via the Tip of the Red Giant Branch are 6.03_ 0.21 ^ +0.29 Mpc and 5.75 0.18 Mpc, respectively. The galaxies form an isolated pair dynamically well separated from the nearest neighbors: KK 176 ( 7.28 0.29 Mpc) and NGC 5068 ( 5.16 0.21 Mpc). All four galaxies have a bulk spatial peculiar velocity towards the Virgo cluster of 158 17 in the Local Group rest frame and 330 with respect to the cluster center.

In this Note, we report on measurements of accurate distances to dwarf galaxies DDO 161 = UGCA 320 and UGCA 319 with radial velocities 543 and 555 respectively, which form a tight pair well separated from other neighboring galaxies. The galaxies DDO 161 and UGCA 319 were observed with the Hubble Space Telescope ( HST ) using Advanced Camera for Surveys (ACS) on February 11 and 14, 2017 (cycle 24 proposal GO-14636, PI Karachentsev).

sources.summary: The paper presents what it believes is DDO 161 first published oxygen abundance, with H I extent, slit placement, and adopted nitrogen-to-oxygen ratio.

quotes.quote: DDO 161 This Cen A group dwarf galaxy was studied in some detail by , but the oxygen abundance here is believed to be the first ever published for this galaxy. The H I extent is almost five times larger than the optical extent cote00 . The spectrum presented here was taken with the east-west slit placed so that the most northern star-forming clump was observed. The adopted oxygen abundance is 12 + log(O/H) = 8.06 and the nitrogen-to-oxygen ratio is log(N/O) = 1.91 .

gmw_00006196.name: UGCA 319
gmw_00006196.overview: UGCA 319 is a gas-rich dwarf galaxy located at a distance of 5.75 Mpc [arXiv:1704.07648]. It forms an isolated galaxy pair with its close neighbor DDO 161 [arXiv:1704.07648].

sources.summary: The paper measures UGCA 319's TRGB distance with HST/ACS, resolves a Tully-Fisher discrepancy as HI confusion, and identifies it as a gas-rich dwarf paired with DDO 161.

quotes.quote: firstpage - lastpage 2016 We report HST /ACS observations of two nearby gas-rich dwarf galaxies: DDO 161 and UGCA 319. Their distances determined via the Tip of the Red Giant Branch are 6.03_ 0.21 ^ +0.29 Mpc and 5.75 0.18 Mpc, respectively. The galaxies form an isolated pair dynamically well separated from the nearest neighbors: KK 176 ( 7.28 0.29 Mpc) and NGC 5068 ( 5.16 0.21 Mpc). All four galaxies have a bulk spatial peculiar velocity towards the Virgo cluster of 158 17 in the Local Group rest frame and 330 with respect to the cluster center.

In contrast to DDO 161, NED presents for UGCA 319 only a single distance estimate, 19.7 Mpc, via the infra-red Tully-Fisher relation sorce2014 that is in sharp contradiction to our present TRGB measurement. The nature of this discrepancy is caused apparently by confusion of the HI-line width of UGCA 319 with the strong HI-flux from the close neighbor DDO 161. This region of sky has been imaged in HI with Australian Telescope Compact Array (ATCA) by pisano2011 . Answering our request, Daniel Pisano kindly re-estimated HI parameters for UGCA 319 from ATCA observations.

gmw_00006197.name: KK 176
gmw_00006197.overview: KK 176 is a nearby dwarf galaxy [arXiv:1704.07648] located at a distance of approximately 7.28 Mpc [arXiv:1704.07648]. Although once considered part of a loose group, its distance indicates it is not physically connected to the DDO 161/UGCA 319 galaxy pair [arXiv:1704.07648].

sources.summary: The paper measures KK 176 with HST/ACS as a nearby dwarf, gives its TRGB distance, and concludes it is not physically connected to the DDO 161/UGCA 319 pair.

quotes.quote: Apart from UGCA 319, in the wide surroundings of DDO 161 up to the angular separation of 5^o there are only two galaxies with radial velocities within 200 around the velocity of DDO 161: the dwarf galaxy KK 176 at a separation of 128 arcmin with the velocity of V_ LG = 618 , and the spiral galaxy NGC 5068 at a separation of 310 arcmin with the velocity of V_ LG = 469 . pisano2011 assumed that DDO 161 together with KK 176, NGC 5068 and another dwarf BCD galaxy MCG-03-34-002 ( V_ LG = 765 ) form a loose "HIPASS"-group having the linear diameter of 600 kpc, radial velocity dispersion of 124 and the virial mass of 5.1 2.5 10^ 11 . We have observed the galaxies KK 176 and NGC 5068 with ACS on HST during February 1 and February 11, 2017 in the same program with DDO 161 and UGCA 319. The CM diagrams for both the galaxies are presented in Fig. 3. The estimated values of the TRGB for KK 176 and NGC 5068 as well as basic properties of them are given in Table 3.

The distance estimates: 7.28 0.29 Mpc for KK 176 and 5.16 0.21 Mpc for NGC 5068 do not give grounds for considering these neighbors to be physically connected to the tight pair DDO 161/UGCA 319. The larger galaxy NGC 5068 is 540 kpc removed in projection and 730 310 kpc removed in the line of sight. It is interesting to note that all 4 galaxies with accurately measured distances have positive peculiar velocities, V_ pec = V_ LG H_0 D assuming the Hubble parameter H_0 = 73 , i.e.: +103 (DDO 161), +135 (UGCA 319), +87 (KK 176), and +92 (NGC 5068).

gmw_00006198.name: SHiZELS-14
gmw_00006198.overview: SHiZELS-14 is a massive, dusty star-forming galaxy at a redshift of approximately 2.24 [arXiv:2102.07791]. It is likely undergoing a merger [arXiv:2102.07791], exhibiting velocity-dispersion-dominated kinematics [arXiv:2011.00686], extended far-infrared emission [arXiv:2102.07791, arXiv:2011.00686], and a compact, dusty central starburst [arXiv:2102.07791].

sources.summary: The paper presents resolved multi-wavelength imaging of SHiZELS-14, identifying it as a massive dusty z=2.24 star-forming galaxy with extended FIR structure and likely merger activity.

quotes.quote: firstpage - lastpage We present 0.15'' spatial resolution imaging of SHiZELS-14, a massive ( M_ * 10^ 11 M_ ), dusty, star-forming galaxy at z=2.24 . Our rest-frame 1 kpc scale, matched-resolution data comprise four different widely used tracers of star formation: the H emission line (from SINFONI/VLT), rest-frame UV continuum (from HST F606W imaging), the rest-frame far-infrared (from ALMA), and the radio continuum (from JVLA). Although originally identified by its modest H emission line flux, SHiZELS-14 appears to be a vigorously star-forming ( SFR 1000 M_ yr^ 1 ) example of a submillimeter galaxy, probably undergoing a merger. SHiZELS-14 displays a compact, dusty central starburst, as well as extended emission in H and the rest-frame optical and FIR.

SHiZELS-14 shows striking, extended emission in both H and the FIR, with H derived effective radius 4.6 0.4 kpc and a FIR-derived effective radius along the semi-major axis 4.6 0.2 kpc (axial ratio q=0.47 ). Unlike many SMGs studied at similar redshifts which display compact 1-2 kpc cores, SHiZELS-14 displays an extended disk structure in the rest-frame FIR. Our deep imaging enables us to recover directly the fainter emission across extended regions of star formation, which are also traced by H . The irregular, extended structures and disordered H kinematics, together with the intense burst of dusty star formation observed, likely reflects ongoing (at =2.24 ) merger activity.

sources.summary: The paper describes SHiZELS-14 as a merger with separated H clumps, velocity-dispersion-dominated kinematics, high metallicity, and centrally concentrated but extended ALMA/FIR emission.

quotes.quote: SHiZELS-14 : This galaxy has been identified as a merger with three H clumps separated by 5 kpc Swinbank2012b . The H morphology in Gillman2019 is less clumpy, but still extends to a half-light radius of about 7 kpc (Sersic model fitting results). The H kinematics show a velocity-dispersion-dominated system. The galaxy has the highest metallicity in the SHiZELS sample of Swinbank2012a . The full extent of the H emission is not traced by the rest-frame UV imaging.

The ALMA continuum emission shows one dominant compact component at the centre, and more extended emission following a similar orientation as the F140W morphology. The FIR emission spreads up to 2'' , i.e. \ 16 kpc at z=2.23 .

sources.summary: The paper describes COSLA-16N as a robust PdBI-position identification at about z = 2.25 that would be securely recovered by all four LABOCA-position association methods.

quotes.quote: COSLA-16N. A robust single identification 0.70 arcsec from the PdBI position. z_ phot and z_ LW are in good agreement that the source has a redshift z 2.25 . This source would have been securely identified using all four types of statistical association on the basis of the original LABOCA position.

gmw_00006199.name: SDSS J020536.84-081424.7
gmw_00006199.overview: SDSS J020536.84-081424.7 is a gas-rich, metal-poor dwarf irregular galaxy [arXiv:2106.14925]. It is actively forming stars in several bright clumpy knots and shows signs of interaction with the nearby galaxy Mrk 1172 [arXiv:2106.14925].

sources.summary: The paper characterizes SDSS J020536.84-081424.7 as a gas-rich, low-metallicity dwarf irregular galaxy near Mrk 1172, with young populations, clumpy star formation, rotating gas, and interaction signatures.

quotes.quote: In this work we characterise the properties of the object SDSS J020536.84-081424.7, an extended nebular region with projected extension of 14 14 kpc ^2 in the line of sight of the ETG Mrk 1172, using unprecedented spectroscopic data from MUSE. We perform a spatially resolved stellar population synthesis and estimate the stellar mass for both Mrk 1172 (1 10^ 11 M _ ) and our object of study ( 3 10^ 9 M_ ).

Using these emission lines we find that it is metal-poor (with Z 1/3 Z_ , comparable to the LMC) and is actively forming stars ( 0.70 \ M_ yr ^ 1 ), especially in a few bright clumpy knots that are readily visible in H .
