We suggest that the most recent jet direction is that from C to W2 structures in this source, while the other lobes originated from the past activity episodes.

gmw_00006242.name: UM038
gmw_00006242.overview: UM038 is a dwarf galaxy that exhibits disklike rotation [arXiv:1403.6294]. Its star formation follows a twisted, spiral-like structure that is likely produced by differential rotation [arXiv:1403.6294].

sources.summary: The paper finds UM038 has twisted, spiral-like star formation, disklike rotation, a low stellar rotation-to-dispersion ratio, and a likely stellar-gas rotation-axis misalignment.

quotes.quote: For UM038, it seems that the star-formation follows a twisted structure, almost like spiral arms. These structures are most probably produced by differential rotation, which is also noticeable on the \ maps (see Fig. fig:gas_stars ), but only outside 10-20 arcsec (inside this radius the gas and stars rotate like a solid body, see Section subsec:star_gas ). Spiral-like structures are observed in rotating simulated dwarf galaxies, but they are not stable schroyen2013 .

The major axis of UM038 (Fig. fig:UM038_prop ) displays, again, an off-centered peak of luminosity associated with disklike rotation and a drop in velocity dispersion. The same structure has slightly higher metallicities ( [ Fe/H ] =-1.0 dex) and younger ages (below 1 Gyr), but this change is not significant. The images in the three bands show the star-formation structure, which seems to become smoother with increasing wavelength.

gmw_00006243.name: UM323
gmw_00006243.overview: UM323 is a blue compact dwarf galaxy with a regular body and a minor-axis luminosity peak shifted from its center [arXiv:1403.6294]. Its star-forming regions form an off-center, crown-like structure, and its most luminous region displays a turbulent character [arXiv:1403.6294].

sources.summary: The paper describes UM323 as a regular-bodied BCD with crown-like off-center star formation, turbulent luminous regions, shifted minor-axis light peak, and possible distant interacting neighbors.

quotes.quote: The star-formation pockets (hottest parts) of UM323 are forming a crown-like structure which is shifted to the NW with respect to the center of the outer isophotes of the galaxy. The red object at the north, north-west end of UM323, is a galaxy at a redshift of 0.484 http://skyserver.sdss3.org/dr9/en/tools/explore/obj.asp?id=1237663783133118504 . The body of UM323 is very regular with a (B-I) colour of 1.5.

The most luminous region along the UM323 major axis (Fig. fig:UM323_prop ) displays a more turbulent character with a slight increase in velocity dispersion. The ages and metallicities are spread around 1.1 Gyr and 1.6 dex. Its minor axis (Fig. fig:UM323_prop ) peak of luminosity seems to be shifted from the center with about 3-4 arcsec.

gmw_00006244.name: B2 0902+34
gmw_00006244.overview: B2 0902+34 is a high-redshift radio galaxy at z = 3.396 [arXiv:2401.03577] that contains a heavily obscured active galactic nucleus [arXiv:2401.03577]. It features a complex radio structure [arXiv:2401.03577, arXiv:2306.00071] and is surrounded by a rich circumgalactic medium [arXiv:2401.03577] within a protocluster environment [arXiv:2306.00071].

sources.summary: The paper presents B2 0902+34 as a z=3.396 high-redshift radio galaxy/protogalaxy with an obscured AGN, complex radio structure, rich CGM, and associated molecular absorption.

quotes.quote: Here we present the detection of molecular absorption associated with the high-redshift radio galaxy B2 0902+34 at z = 3.396. B2 0902+34 was one of the first galaxies discovered in the Early Universe, thanks to its bright radio continuum, which served as a beacon for tracing the faint host galaxy lil88,eis92 . It was suggested that this is a proto-galaxy undergoing its first episode of star formation eal93,pen99 . X-ray observations revealed that it contains a heavily obscured AGN fab02 . car95 described the radio/optical structure of this galaxy as ``bizarre'', with a bright northern radio lobe that shows a sharp ( 90 ^ ) bend and a flat-spectrum radio core located in a ``valley'' between two optical peaks.

Recent 144 MHz imaging of the radio source with the Low Frequency Array (LOFAR) suggests that the complex radio structure is either the result of different episodes of AGN activity, or, more likely, due to interactions between the radio source and the surrounding halo gas cor23 . B2 0902+34 is surrounded by a rich circumgalactic medium (CGM) in the form of a giant Ly nebula reu03 , and is thought to be a collapsing protogiant elliptical ada09 .

sources.summary: The paper compares its Fermi-acceleration Ly-alpha spectra with B2 0902+34, noting broad blue wings, fiber-to-fiber spectral variation, and a possible reinterpretation of the main peak.

quotes.quote: We now consider the observed Ly spectra of z 3.4 radio galaxy B2 0902+34 Adams . There is broad agreement in the shape of the observed and predicted spectra, with the broad wing extending well beyond 1000 km s ^ 1 , and some spectra displaying prominent peaks blueshifted by 1000 km s ^ 1 . The spectra from B2 0902+34 vary widely depending on where exactly on the galaxy the fiber is placed. It is however true to say that for the majority of fibers the most prominent peak has been observed to lie either at the systemic velocity, which was determined from the observed 21-cm absorption signature, or slightly blueward of it. This would appear to be in tension with our model here which, following NM88, predicts the most prominent peak to appear at v_b=0 (not shown in Figure spectrumSingleSource ) as a result of direct `blister' sources on the edge of the clump.
We reached the same conclusions for the radio galaxy B2 0902+34 Adams .

sources.summary: The paper describes B2 0902+34 as a z=3.4 high-redshift radio galaxy/protogalaxy with a bizarre radio-optical structure and associated narrow HI absorption.

quotes.quote: B2 0902+34 : This HzRG has a redshift of z=3.4 , a relatively flat optical spectral energy distribution, and was suggested to be a protogalaxy undergoing its first episode of star formation lilly1988discovery,eales1993evidence,pentericci1999hst . The radio/optical structure of this galaxy has been described as bizarre , with a flat-spectrum radio core located in a valley between 2 optical peaks carilli1995bizarre . This HzRG is also exceptional in that it is one of the only z>2 radio sources that has been observed to have an associated narrow HI absorption line 1991PhRvL..67.3328U, chandra2004associated .

B2 0902+34 is located in a protocluster environment eales1993evidence, pentericci1999hst , surrounded by several galaxies. As pointed out by carilli1995bizarre , B2 0902+34 has an unusual radio structure, with a relatively flat-spectrum core, located in a valley between 2 optical peaks. The bending of the northern structure eastwards and the southern structure westwards indicates that the jet ejected from the nucleus in the A1 - B2 direction is thereafter bent because of its interaction with a rotating gas cloud connected with the Ly halo visible on the Keck infrared map Ly map of Reuland_2003 in Figure fig:overlay_B20902 .

gmw_00006245.name: 4C 34.34
gmw_00006245.overview: 4C 34.34 is a high-redshift Fanaroff-Riley II radio galaxy located at a redshift of z=2.4 [arXiv:2306.00071]. It resides in a highly dense environment and exhibits an ultra-steep spectrum radio bridge alongside normal hot-spot spectra [arXiv:2306.00071].

sources.summary: The paper presents 4C 34.34 as a z=2.4 high-luminosity FR-II radio galaxy with normal hot-spot spectra and an ultra-steep radio bridge.

quotes.quote: 4C 34.34 : This HzRG has a redshift of z=2.4 . It has been mapped with the VLA at higher frequencies law1995vla and is, therefore, a suitable target for low-frequency spectral index mapping with the ILT observation. However, besides its optical spectrum and a Spitzer observation which places it in a highly dense environment wylezalek2013galaxy , there are no other optical, infrared, or X-ray data available for this source.

The radio structure of 4C 34.34 is typical of high-luminosity FR-II edge-brightened double radio sources, with hot spots at its northern and southern extremities and diffuse emission on both sides extending towards the galaxy nucleus that is not detected by the ILT. The spectral indices of the hot spots are normal for extended radio sources, both with = -0.70 0.04 . The radio bridge has an ultra-steep spectrum with = -1.10 0.05 .

gmw_00006246.name: Malin 2
gmw_00006246.overview: Malin 2 is a giant low-surface-brightness disk galaxy [arXiv:1310.3957] that hosts a bulge and an active galactic nucleus [arXiv:1108.0763]. Although it contains a massive, extended molecular gas disk [arXiv:1006.1973], its star formation activity is low compared to normal spiral galaxies [arXiv:1006.1973].

sources.summary: The paper reports CO(2-1) detections across Malin 2, showing molecular gas spread through the inner disk and connecting that gas distribution to the galaxy’s weak star formation.
sources.paper_coordinate_text: position of the nucleus , (J2000): 10^ h 39^ m 52^ s .5, +20^ 50^ 49^

quotes.quote: Our goal is to see if there is molecular gas extending throughout the optical low surface brightness disk of the galaxy Malin 2. We used the heterodyne receiver array (HERA) mounted on the IRAM 30m telecope to make deep observations at the frequency of the CO(2--1) line at nine different positions of Malin 2. With a total observing time of 11 hours at a velocity resolution of 11 km/s we achieve a sensitivity level of 1 mK. We detect CO(2-1) line emission from Malin 2. The line is detected in four of the nine HERA beams; a fifth beam shows a marginal detection.

These results not only confirm that there is molecular gas in the disk of Malin 2, but they also show that it is spread throughout the inner 34 kpc radius as sampled by the observations of the galaxy disk.

Star Formation threshold : The molecular gas disk in Malin 2 is extended and relatively massive with a significant amount of molecular gas but its star formation activity is low compared to normal spirals (Wyder et al. ).

sources.summary: The paper presents Malin 2 as a giant low-surface-brightness disc galaxy with an enormous mass, rarefied dark halo, extended spiral disc, unusual gas balance, and no need for a catastrophic merger origin.

quotes.quote: The subject of our study is 2 which is a member of the giant LSB galaxy family and possesses a number of peculiar properties. Despite its enormous total mass 2 10^ 12  M _ and the size of 100  kpc, 2 has an extended disc with a clear spiral structure (the plateau of rotation curve is about 350 km s ^ 1 ).

Evolutionary models 2 challenges standard evolutionary models of disc galaxies in which one can hardly form such a huge mass 2 10^ 12  M _ without recent major merger events. The dynamic and structural properties of galaxies are believed to be related closely to their host dark halos whose properties cannot change significantly by minor mergers. 2 must have had acquired some of its specific properties at the early epoch yet before the disc subsystem was formed. Therefore we have to understand whether there is a need for an exotic evolutionary scenario (as fine-tuned interaction with companions at later evolutionary stages) to explain the low brightness and the large scalelength of the 2 disc or their cause is in the initial cosmological conditions too.

sources.summary: The paper describes F568-6 (Malin 2) as a giant LSB galaxy with a prominent bulge, AGN, molecular gas, near-solar metallicity, and low black-hole mass.

quotes.quote: F568-6 (Malin 2): This is also a relatively well studied giant LSB galaxy. It has an LSB disk (Schombert Bothun 1987), prominent bulge and an AGN schombert98 . Like UGC 1922, it is one of the rare LSB galaxies that have a significant mass of molecular gas das.etal.2010 .
The oxygen abundances and hence metallicities are close to solar in value, particularly for F568-6 and UGC 6614 (see also for UGC 6614).
The masses are 0.3 10^6 \ M_ for the galaxies UGC 1922, UGC 6968 and F568-6, and lie on the lower mass tail of the low-mass blackhole sample of which has a median mass of 1.3 10^6 M _ .

sources.summary: The paper includes F568-VI among five bulgeless LSB galaxies, describing shared low surface brightness, disc size, dynamical mass, central H I density, and unusually stable discs.

quotes.quote: Our LSB sample consist of F563-1, F568-1, F568-3, F568-VI and F579-VI. They have a B-band central surface surface brightness _B (0) ranging between 23-24 mg arcsec ^ 2 , spanning exponential disc scalelength R_D between 2.8 - 5.3 kpc and comparable dynamical masses as given by the asymptotic rotational velocity V _ max 105 - 118 kms ^ 1 , except for F568-1 which has a slightly higher V _ max 142 kms ^ 1 . All our galaxies are bulgeless. Our sample LSBs have a central HI surface density a few M _ pc ^ 2 .

We find that the median value of Q _ RW ^ min , the minimum of Q _ RW over R , lies between 2.6 and 3.1 for our sample LSBs, which is higher than the median value of 1.8 0.3 for Q _ RW ^ min for a sample of high surface brightness galaxies (HSBs) as obtained in earlier studies.

sources.summary: The paper says Malin 2 could have an extended disc linked to a large dark-halo scale, while a major merger is less likely and gas accretion from a filament remains possible.

quotes.quote: Malin 2 . We can not exclude the possibility that extended disc of Malin 2 is due to the high radial scale of the dark halo in agreement with our finding in Kasparova2014 . The major merger scenario is less likely since the stellar velocity dispersion is not very high at one disc scalelength and we observe high gradient of gas metallicity in the disc Kasparova2014 . The formation of the giant disc from the gas accreted from the filament could not be fully excluded.

gmw_00006247.name: 2XMM J123103.2+110648
gmw_00006247.overview: 2XMM J123103.2+110648 is a highly variable, soft X-ray source [arXiv:1205.2774, arXiv:1210.0440] associated with a type 2 active galactic nucleus at a redshift of approximately 0.12 [arXiv:1210.0440, arXiv:2009.03061]. Its host is a small, low-mass disk galaxy containing an exceptionally low-mass central black hole [arXiv:1210.0440].

sources.summary: The paper reports 2XMM J123103.2+110648 as a candidate AGN at z = 0.13 with a purely soft, highly variable X-ray spectrum and possible galaxy counterpart.

quotes.quote: We report the discovery of a candidate active galactic nucleus (AGN), 2XMM J123103.2+110648 at z = 0.13 , with an X-ray spectrum represented purely by soft thermal emission reminiscent of Galactic black hole (BH) binaries in the disk-dominated state. This object was found in the second serendipitous source catalogue as a highly variable X-ray source. In three separate observations, its X-ray spectrum can be represented either by a multicolor disk blackbody model with an inner temperature of kT_ in 0.16-0.21 keV or a Wien spectrum Comptonized by an optically thick plasma with kT 0.14-0.18 keV.

The error circle of the X-ray position contains only one optical source SDSS J123103.24 +110648.5 classified as a galaxy and one possible interpretation is that 2XMM J1231 is an AGN. We examine the possibility that the detected X-ray source is not an AGN. The apparent size of this optical source ( 2 arcsec) is much smaller than the error circle and the source could be an off-nuclear source in this galaxy.

sources.summary: The paper treats 2XMM J123103.2+110648 as a Type-2 AGN with an LFQPO; GRPM places the emission region near the black hole across 6-20 radii.

quotes.quote: Another significant QPO at 3.8 hour timescale was reported in an Ultrasoft Active Galactic Nucleus Candidate 2XMM J123103.2+110648 .

There is no such known case in AGN where three QPOs are detected simultaneously; however, the X-ray QPO detected in Type 2 AGN 2XMM J123103.2+110648 (see Table AGNXrayQPO ) was suggested as a LFQPO because of its large rms value (25--50 ) , which is the typical characterstic of LFQPOs observed in BHXRB . Therefore, for AGN having a single QPO detection, we associate the QPO frequency with _ , except for 2XMM J123103.2+110648 where we also analyze the _ np frequency.

We show the contours of the LFQPO component of the GRPM, _ np , a , in the , a plane for the QPO frequency of 2XMM J123103.2+110648 in Figure plotra1QPO , where we fixed M_ =10^ 5 M_ . We see that the emission region for this LFQPO is 6-20 , for the whole range of a . Hence, the detected QPO of 2XMM J123103.2+110648 is an LFQPO that originated very close to the black hole.

sources.summary: The paper confirms J1231+1106 as a type 2 active nucleus in a small low-mass disk galaxy, with an unusually low black-hole mass and high Eddington ratio.

quotes.quote: Optical spectra and images taken with the Baade 6.5 meter Magellan telescope confirm that 2XMM J123103.2+110648, a highly variable X-ray source with an unusually soft spectrum, is indeed associated with a type 2 (narrow-line) active nucleus at a redshift of z=0.11871 . The absence of broad \ or \ emission in an otherwise X-ray unabsorbed source suggests that it intrinsically lacks a broad-line region. If, as in other active galaxies, the ionized gas and stars in J1231+1106 are in approximate virial equilibrium, and the M_ BH _* relation holds, the exceptionally small velocity dispersion of = 33.5 \ for \ 5007 implies that \ 10^5 , among the lowest ever detected. Such a low black hole mass is consistent with the general characteristics of the host, a small, low-luminosity, low-mass disk galaxy. We estimate the Eddington ratio of the black hole to be 0.5, in good agreement with expectations based on the X-ray properties of the source.

gmw_00006248.name: J0232
gmw_00006248.overview: J0232 is a red early-type galaxy that hosts a type-2 quasar [arXiv:2111.13578]. The galaxy is characterized by low star formation rates, weak radio emission, and slow ionized outflows [arXiv:2111.13578].

sources.summary: The paper classifies J0232 as a red early-type QSO2 host with low star formation, weak radio/FIR emission, compact continuum, no CO detection, and slow ionized outflow.

quotes.quote: The two seemingly undisturbed ETGs in the sample, J0232 and J1152, show rest-frame colors M _u M _g 1.4. These are much redder and closer to typical red-sequence galaxies (M _u M _g> 1.5; 2006ApJ...648..268B ) than the other five QSO2s, which show (M _u M _g ) 0.95--1.18. In the following, we will refer to J0232 and J1152 as red ETGs.

The difference between both J0232 and J1152 and the other ETGs in our sample are their redder optical colors, lower SFRs (3 and 3.7 M _ yr ^ 1 ), lower radio luminosities (log L _ 1.4 GHz 23 W Hz ^ 1 ) and slower ionized outflows (see Table tab3 ).

gmw_00006249.name: SDSS J101043.36+061201.4
gmw_00006249.overview: SDSS J101043.36+061201.4 is an early-type galaxy hosting a type-2 quasar [arXiv:2311.10132, arXiv:2111.13578]. It is in an early-stage merger with a smaller companion galaxy located 13 to 16 kiloparsecs away [arXiv:2311.10132, arXiv:2403.01258], which likely drives its high star formation rate [arXiv:2111.13578].

sources.summary: The paper describes J1010 as an early-type QSO2 interacting with a companion, with peculiar CO morphology, rotating ionized gas, and an unresolved ionized outflow in the seeing-limited data.

quotes.quote: J1010 This QSO2 is hosted in an early-type galaxy which is interacting with a smaller companion located at 13 kpc to the south-west. The CO(2-1) morphology of the QSO2, studied in Ramos22 at 0.8  resolution, shows two peaks that are 1.25 kpc apart. This peculiar CO morphology is most likely produced by gas removal/excitation in the north-south direction.

We show the kinematic maps of J1010 in Fig.  fig:J1010_extended . Its [ O III ] emission is round and centrally peaked, having a diameter of 4  (7.2 kpc), directly estimated from the kinematic maps. The vel _p map shows gas rotation, with negative velocities in the south-east and positive velocities to the north-west. This rotation pattern was first reported by Harrison14 , who used Gemini/GMOS observations of the central 5 3.5 arcsec ^2 of the QSO2.

sources.summary: The paper describes J1010+0612 as the nearest quasar in the sample, with central OIII emission, a nearby companion, disturbed central gas, early-stage merging, and a blueshifted outflow.

quotes.quote: J1010+0612 is the nearest quasar of our sample with z 0.098(1 '' 2.3 kpc). The [ OIII ] line emission is detected in a region limited to the central 7-8 kpc from the nucleus, beyond which the [ OIII ] flux becomes very faint. The H flux is more extended and shows a companion galaxy located approximately 16 kpc away (Fig. MOM012_J1010-0612 , the left panels in first two rows). In contrast to CO observations with ALMA, no double peak is observed in the moment 0 map of H and [ OIII ] ( Ramos22 ).

Unlike J1000+1242 and J1010+1413, it is not possible to clearly resolve a precise region with a higher-velocity dispersion, but the motion of the gas is highly disturbed overall throughout the central region (up to 10kpc) of J1010+0612, showing a velocity dispersion of up to 500 km/s. In contrast, the moment 2 of the companion galaxy decreases, suggesting that the merging is still in an early stage.
The velocity map of the broad emission instead exhibits a blueshifted emission ( V_ [ OIII ] ,BR 100 km/s) that we identify as the outflow.

sources.summary: The paper describes J1010 as an interacting early-type QSO2 host with high SFR, radio excess, compact continuum, double-peaked CO, and possible inflow or outflow motions.

quotes.quote: SDSS J101043.36+061201.4 (J1010) The SDSS optical image of this QSO2 resembles an ETG morphology (S0a; 2013MNRAS.435.2835W ) clearly interacting with a small galaxy at 7 (13 kpc) SW that it is also detected in CO (see Appendix appendixc ). The SFR estimated from the IR luminosity, of 30 M _ yr ^ 1 , is comparable to those of the spiral galaxies J1100 and J1509, placing J1010 1.15 dex above the MS (see Section sample ). This is likely a result of the interaction with the small companion.

The CO(2 1) moment maps of J1010 are shown in Figure fig2a . The maximum radius of the CO emission is R _ CO =1.3 (2.4 kpc; see Table tab4 ). The moment 0 map reveals a double-peaked structure, with a separation of 0.7 (1.25 kpc) between peaks in the E-W direction.

sources.summary: J1010+0612 has tentative high-excitation CO, a double-peaked CO(2-1) profile in higher-resolution ALMA data, and broad MUSE [O III] without matching narrow CO components.

quotes.quote: J1010+0612 shows a very tentative signs of a line with a S/N of 1.1 at a higher excitation than starburst/MS galaxies, but still less than the high- z QSO CO SLED.
Another target within our sample ( J1010+0612 ) also shows a double peak CO(2-1) profile identified in higher resolution ALMA data by Almeida21 .
However, like J1100+0846, J1010+0612 shows a broad line in the MUSE [ O III ] data, without signs of two narrower components as identified in CO.

gmw_00006250.name: SDSS J110012.39+084616.3
gmw_00006250.overview: SDSS J110012.39+084616.3 is a moderately inclined barred spiral galaxy [arXiv:2311.10132, arXiv:2111.13578] hosting a type 2 active galactic nucleus [arXiv:2403.01258]. Its molecular gas traces the spiral arms [arXiv:2403.01258, arXiv:2111.13578], while highly turbulent central gas indicates a resolved ionized outflow [arXiv:2311.10132, arXiv:2403.01258].

sources.summary: The paper characterizes J1100 as a moderately inclined barred spiral QSO2 with rounded [O III] emission, disturbed rotation, and a resolved blueshifted ionized outflow to the northeast.

quotes.quote: J1100 J1100 is a barred spiral galaxy, moderately inclined (i=38 ), the south being the near side Ramos22 . Fischer18 presented HST/ACS imaging observations of J1100 that show rounded [ O III ] emission, of 1.1 ( 2 kpc) in radius, with a small tail towards the south-east. Our GTC/MEGARA kinematic maps, shown in Fig.  fig:J1100_extended , also show a round [ O III ] morphology, with a radius of 3  ( 5.5 kpc). The velocity peak map shows some deviations from regular rotation, also reported from GMOS/IFU data by Harrison14 , which are most likely due to the stellar bar present in this galaxy. Despite that, we can distinguish positive velocities to be dominant in the north-east region and negative in the south-weast.

According to our analysis, presented in Section PSF , the [ O III ] emission is spatially resolved, and thus we can clearly identify high blueshifted velocities ( 1200 ) on the north-east side of the galaxy that correspond to broad line profiles (W _ 80 1600 ). Using HST/STIS data of J1100 and a slit orientation of 19^ , Fischer18 reported the presence of blueshifted velocities to the south-east, with FWHM of 1780 . This is in agreement with our results if we look at the values of the kinematic maps at PA= 19^ , but thanks to the IFU capabilities of MEGARA, we can see from the maps that the preferential orientation of the outflow (i.e., the maximum velocities and FWHMs) is to the north-east, with PA = 63^ and R _ out =2.8  ( 5.1 kpc).

sources.summary: The paper describes J1100+0846 as a barred-spiral type 2 AGN whose H emission traces the disk and arms, while central turbulent OIII/H gas is interpreted as an outflow.

quotes.quote: J1100+0846 is a type 2 AGN that is hosted in a barred spiral galaxy. The high-resolution HST /WFC3 image (Fig. HST ) reveals nuclear elongated emission ( 0.4'', i.e. 0.8 kpc) in a direction consistent with the radio structure identified by e-MERLIN. Different from the other galaxies, the [ OIII ] and H emissions show a different morphology (Fig. MOM012_J1100-0846 , top left and middle left panels). The H traces the disk and spiral arms of the galaxy, while the [ OIII ] emission shows a broad circularly symmetric emission around the nucleus that is only partially resolved.

The integrated H velocity map mainly traces the rotation of the disk, while the [ OIII ] velocity is low and close to the systemic velocity of the galaxy. The moment 2 maps reveal that in correspondence with the spiral arms, the H velocity dispersion is low ( 120 km/s), while in the central region, the gas shows high turbulence with velocity dispersions of up to 500 km/s in both [ OIII ] and H (Fig. MOM012_J1100-0846 , top right and middle right panels).
We identify this emission as an outflow with a direction roughly in the plane of sky, given its low velocities in the line of sight (Fig. MOM012_J1100-0846 , middle center panel).

sources.summary: The paper presents J1100 as a barred spiral QSO2 host with radio excess, compact continuum, extended CO following the bar and spiral arms, and perturbed central gas.

quotes.quote: SDSS J110012.39+084616.3 (J1100) J1100 has a SFR of 34 M _ yr ^ 1 and it shows a clear radio-excess in Figure fig2 , with only 4 of the 1.4 GHz luminosity being due to star formation as estimated by 2019MNRAS.485.2710J . VLA 6 GHz images of this QSO2 at the same angular resolution as our ALMA data ( 0.2 ) reveal a compact and rounded morphology, very similar to J1010, with a linear size of 800 pc and PA 170 2019MNRAS.485.2710J . Lower resolution VLA data do not show evidence for extended emission either.

The QSO2 host is a large and moderately inclined (i=38 ) barred galaxy (SBb; 2013MNRAS.435.2835W ). The optical continuum SDSS and HST/ACS images available for this QSO2 show strong and wound spiral arms, and an undisturbed morphology 2018ApJ...856..102F . The ALMA CO(2 1) moment maps shown in Figure fig3aa reveal a large disc of molecular gas with R _ CO =2.7 ( 5 kpc; see Table tab4 ).

sources.summary: 2MASX J110012 has a compact, nearly circular [O III] morphology with high-FWHM nuclear emission, a 450 pc outflow radius, and disturbed gas to 1 kpc.

quotes.quote: 2MASX J110012 This target has a compact [ O III ] morphology, which is nearly circular with diffraction spikes and a slight extension to the southeast. High FWHM emission ( > 1000 km s ^ 1 ) is measured across nucleus that is blueshifted to the southeast. We measure R _ out to the maximum extent of these high FWHM measurements at 450 pc northwest of the nucleus. Disturbed kinematics extend to the furthest observations northwest of the nucleus, R _ dist = 1 kpc, where we measure single-component emission-lines at systemic with widths of 500 km s ^ 1 , which may be a product of blending multiple components as observed over the nucleus. This QSO2 resides in a well-resolved barred spiral galaxy that is moderately inclined with no evidence of recent interactions or ongoing mergers.

sources.summary: J1100+0846 is highlighted for a tentative high-J CO signal, a clear double-peaked CO profile, and possible jet-gas interactions.

quotes.quote: J1100+0846 shows signs of a detection, with a S/N of 2.2, but the 3 upper limit is at a similar excitation to our detected J1430+1339.

This is limited by S/N in our CO data but interestingly, in the 2 cases where we find multiple components in the CO (J1100+0846 J1108+0659) we identify the following: J1100+0846 shows a clear double peaked line profile in the first three CO transitions.
The presence of double-peaked CO lines (e.g. in the case of J1100+0846 and J1010+0612) can be indicative of jet-gas interactions whereby jets are pushing the gas in opposite directions Kharb21 .

gmw_00006251.name: J1152
gmw_00006251.overview: J1152 is an elliptical galaxy and a type-2 quasar host that has undergone a past merger [arXiv:2111.13578]. Deep optical imaging reveals shells, a tidal tail, and a dust lane [arXiv:2111.13578], while its extended emission-line gas exhibits rotational kinematics and a compact outflow [arXiv:1802.06184, arXiv:2010.08050].

sources.summary: The paper describes J1152 as a red early-type, post-merger QSO2 host with shells, a tidal tail, compact continuum, radio excess, and little detected CO.

quotes.quote: J1152 has been revealed as a post-merger system from deep optical imaging (see Pierce et al. (2021, subm.). The host galaxy of this QSO2 is classified as an elliptical galaxy in the Galaxy Zoo project 2013MNRAS.435.2835W , based on the SDSS images (see Figure fig1 ). 2018ApJ...856..102F reported no signs of galaxy interactions from the analysis of the HST/ACS continuum image.

However, deeper optical imaging observations obtained in January 2020 with the Wide Field Camera on the 2.5 m Isaac Newton Telescope (La Palma, Spain), reveal spectacular shells and a tidal tail of several kpc (Pierce et al. 2021, subm.). These features are the result of a past merger/interaction with another galaxy, and they coincide with the faint structures towards the NE (shell) and SW (fan and tail) shown in the SDSS image (see Figure fig1 ). Finally, from inspection of the HST/ACS image of J1152 we detect a dust lane crossing the galaxy W of the nucleus.

sources.summary: SDSS J115245 has biconical [O III] arcs consistent with illuminated spiral arms; its gas mostly rotates, with a small outflow radius and disturbed gas to 1.1 kpc.

quotes.quote: SDSS J115245 This target has a clear biconical [ O III ] morphology, with arcs of emission that suggest that a majority of the gas exists in illuminated spiral arms. The observed kinematics appear to be largely rotational, with redshifted rotation to the north and blueshifted rotation to the south. High peak rotation velocities ( 200 km s ^ 1 ) are consistent with the alignment between the modeled host major axis and STIS long-slit position angles (PA _ diff = 33.3 ^ ). Narrow emission line components are measured going in the opposite direction of rotation immediately northeast of the nucleus, which represent the extent of outflows with R _ out = 130 pc. 400 km s ^ 1 FWHM gas is observed in rotation at radii outside the first northern arc of emission, for an R _ dist of 1.1 kpc.

sources.summary: The paper separates SDSS J115245.66+101623.8 from most QSO2s because it has extended [O III] emission but weak, compact outflow signatures.

quotes.quote: When comparing the two plots, we find three AGNs, SDSS J115245.66+101623.8, 2MASX J13003807+5454367 and B2 1435+30, are separate from the remainder of the QSO2 sample. Their shift in positions from the left panel to the right panel on Figure fig:Fig_11 is due to their very extended [ O III ] emission, based on the ACS images, despite having very weak outflow rates.
Our calculations show that for SDSS J115245.66+101623.8, 2MASX J13003807+5454367 and B2 1435+30 these values are 230, 270 and 310 years ago, respectively.

gmw_00006252.name: SDSS J150904.22+043441.8
gmw_00006252.overview: SDSS J150904.22+043441.8 is a barred spiral galaxy [arXiv:2111.13578] classified as a type-2 quasar [arXiv:2311.10132, arXiv:2111.13578]. The galaxy hosts a multi-phase outflow of blueshifted ionized and molecular gas [arXiv:2311.10132], alongside a rotating gas disc and a peculiar millimeter continuum structure [arXiv:2311.10132, arXiv:2111.13578].

sources.summary: The paper reports J1509 as a barred QSO2 with compact [O III] morphology, rotating gas, and a confirmed northwestern blueshifted ionized outflow matching earlier near-infrared evidence.

quotes.quote: J1509 The [ O III ] morphology of J1509, a barred galaxy whose near side is the north, shows a compact disc of 1.5  (3 kpc) in radius, as directly measured from the MEGARA maps shown in Fig. fig:J1509_extended , and a fish tail-like feature to the south-east. This is the only QSO2 in the sample not included in the kinematic study of Harrison14 and lacking VLA radio observations. Indeed, to the best of our knowledge, these are the first optical IFU observations of this QSO2.

The kinematic maps shown in Fig. fig:J1509_extended reveal gas rotation, with negative velocities to the east and positive to the west. This coincides with the kinematics of the molecular gas studied with ALMA by Ramos22 . A multi-phase outflow was reported in this QSO2 by Ramos19 using near-infrared GTC/EMIR long-slit observations. They detected blueshifted ionized and warm molecular gas, with FWHM 1500-1700 .

sources.summary: The paper describes J1509 as a barred spiral QSO2 host with peculiar millimeter continuum, a likely bent synchrotron jet, extended CO arms, and molecular outflow evidence.

quotes.quote: SDSS J150904.22+043441.8 (J1509) This galaxy is classified as a barred spiral (SBa) in Galaxy Zoo 2013MNRAS.435.2835W , with PA=94 and inclination i=44 (see Table tab2 ). From the -band SDSS image we measure a bar orientation of 30 and R _ bar 2.25 (5.6 kpc). Unlike the majority of QSO2s in our sample, there are no high angular resolution optical and radio data of J1509 to be compared with the ALMA continuum and molecular gas distribution.

The 1.3 mm continuum emission of J1509 is quite peculiar (see pink contours in Figure fig7a ). It follows the inner oblong structure of molecular gas, but from this central region an arc-like structure directed to the SE that does not follow the CO distribution is exhibited. By fitting a 2D Gaussian to the continuum emission at 3 we measure a deconvolved size of 0.74 0.55 (1.5 1.1 kpc ^2 ) with PA=135 22 .

gmw_00006253.name: ESO 558 G009
gmw_00006253.overview: ESO 558 G009 is a highly inclined Sb spiral galaxy [arXiv:1705.09762] that hosts an active galactic nucleus [arXiv:2207.06594] and a confirmed water megamaser disk [arXiv:2302.02572, arXiv:2207.06594]. The galaxy features two counterclockwise-wound spiral arms, an obscuring dust lane, and a 200 pc nuclear region [arXiv:1705.09762].

sources.summary: The paper describes ESO 558 G009 as a highly inclined Sb megamaser host with visible spiral arms, an obscuring dust lane, and a 200 pc nuclear region without a clear morphology.

quotes.quote: ESO 558 G009 is a highly inclined Sb galaxy 1982Lauberts 115 Mpc from Earth (luminosity distance, 2000Mould ). Two counterclockwise-wound spiral arms are visible in the F110W image. The central regions of ESO 558 G009 are obscured by a pronounced dust lane.

We identify a nuclear region with radius 0.4 arcsec ( 200 pc). Its main identifying features in the ellipse profiles are a plateau in eccentricity in F110W between 0.3 and 0.5 arcsec, a break in surface brightness at its inner edge ( 0.3 arcsec) and a maximum in F814W PA profile at 0.4 arcsec. The structure map reveals a dust structure associated with these features in ellipse fits, which supports the distinct character of this region. However, no clear morphological type can be assigned in this case.

sources.summary: ESO 558-G009 is described as a confirmed disk maser whose 183 GHz emission shows only systemic features, with possible high-velocity counterparts below significance.

quotes.quote: ESO 558-G009 The 22 GHz maser system in ESO 558-G009 was discovered by the MCP, and VLBI observations presented in Gao_2017 confirm that it is a disk maser system. Our 183 GHz spectrum of ESO 558-G009 is shown in the upper righthand panel of fig:hex1 , along with a 22 GHz spectrum reproduced from Pesce_2015 (see Fig. 1 in that paper). The 22 GHz spectrum is an average of multiple epochs of spectral monitoring observations taken with the GBT.

The only significant component seen in the 183 GHz spectrum of ESO 558-G009 is the central set of systemic maser features, which span a velocity range of 40 around a central velocity of 7585 . The peak flux density of the systemic features is 11.8 mJy, which is a factor of 6 fainter than the typical peak flux density of the 22 GHz systemic masers. With the overlaid 22 GHz spectrum in fig:hex1 , apparent features in the 183 GHz spectrum near 7075 , 7150 , and 8120 are suggestive because they coincide with high-velocity features at 22 GHz. However, none of these features rise above the 3 significance level, so we cannot confirm high-velocity masers in the 183 GHz spectrum of ESO 558-G009.

sources.summary: The paper fits ESO 558-G009 as an inclined megamaser disk, locating its black hole relative to line-of-sight masers and estimating azimuthal scatter for shifted masers.

quotes.quote: In this work we perform a Bayesian statistical fit to estimate the mass-to-distance ratio and the recessional redshift of 10 different black holes hosted at the centre of active galactic nuclei, namely the galaxies NGC 5765b, NGC 6323, UGC 3789, CGCG 074-064, ESO 558-G009, NGC 2960, NGC 6264, NGC 4388, J0437+2456 and NGC 2273. Our general relativistic method makes use of the positions in the sky and frequency shift observations of water megamasers circularly orbiting the central black hole on their accretion disks.

ESO 558-G009 The maser disk of this galaxy has an inclination angle of _0 = 98 ° MCPIX , for which the LOS maser features are in an upper y offset than the high-redshifted masers. The statistical Bayesian fit with four parameters found the BH position to be aligned with the LOS masers in the x offset and below the origin in the y offset. The scattering angle for the redshifted and blueshifted maser features in the azimuthal angle was = 7 °, giving a reduced ^2 of 1.188.

gmw_00006254.name: Mrk 1029
gmw_00006254.overview: Mrk 1029 is an irregular or spiral galaxy located approximately 124 Mpc from Earth [arXiv:1705.09762]. It features two faint, kiloparsec-scale spiral arms [arXiv:1705.09762], a 220 pc nuclear dust structure [arXiv:1705.09762], and hosts a supermassive black hole with a complex maser distribution [arXiv:2211.06486].

sources.summary: The paper describes Mrk 1029 as an Irr/S galaxy with faint kiloparsec spiral arms, moderate inclination, and a 220 pc nuclear structure traced by ellipse and dust-map features.

quotes.quote: Mrk 1029 (Fig. fig:3rdPage1 ) is an Irr/S galaxy (first position data: 1981Kojoian ; morphological identification as spiral: 2MASS, 2MASS ) located 124 Mpc from Earth (luminosity distance, 2000Mould ). It has a counterclockwise wound spiral structure (arms winding counterclockwise from inside out) consisting of two faint kpc-scale spiral arms. The galaxy seems to be moderately inclined.

As the nuclear region in the galaxy, we identify a 0.4 arcsec ( 220 pc) structure, where the position angle profile flattens and the ellipticity forms a distinct region with an inward increase. A feature in the surface brightness profile can also be identified in both the F814W and F110W filters. Spatially, the selected nuclear region follows a dust structure clearly visible on the structure map. An interesting feature of this galaxy is the absence of any structure in the dust on scales larger than 0.5 kpc.

sources.summary: Mrk 1029 is described as a geometrically and dynamically complicated maser galaxy; the paper fits its mass-to-distance ratio, black-hole mass, recession velocity, x offset, and maser-aligned BH position.

quotes.quote: Mrk 1029 is a galaxy with complicated geometry and dynamics, with an original linear maser distribution from the north-east to the south-west at a P.A. of 218 10 . For this galaxy we estimate three parameters, namely, the ratio M/D=(1.44 0.11) 10^ 4 M_ /Mpc that implies a BH mass M=(1.74 0.16) 10^ 6 M_ , the recession velocity v_ rec , and the x offset of BH position. These parameters have been fitted for a scattering angle of =14 and led to a reduced ^ 2 _ red =1.385 . For this galaxy, the recession velocities 9076 32 km s ^ 1 and 9160 61 km s ^ 1 , reported in huchra99, Vaucouleurs , respectively, are compatible with our estimated value 9099.33^ +12.71 _ 11.25 km s ^ 1 (see Table tab:Results4par ). Again, the resulting BH position is optimal in the sense that the fit yields its location among the systemic masers.

gmw_00006255.name: NGC 5495
gmw_00006255.overview: NGC 5495 is a barred Sc spiral galaxy [arXiv:1705.09762] that hosts a Seyfert 2 active galactic nucleus [arXiv:2211.06486]. The galaxy features a grand-design nuclear spiral [arXiv:1705.09762] and a confirmed north-south oriented disk maser system [arXiv:2302.02572, arXiv:2211.06486].

sources.summary: NGC 5495 is described as a confirmed disk maser where the 183 GHz detection is a faint systemic complex with lower luminosity than the 22 GHz maser.

quotes.quote: NGC 5495 The 22 GHz maser system in NGC 5495 was discovered by Kondratko_2006 , and VLBI observations presented in Gao_2017 confirm that it is a disk maser system. Our 183 GHz spectrum of NGC 5495 is shown in the middle righthand panel of fig:hex2 , along with a 22 GHz spectrum reproduced from Pesce_2015 (see Fig. 1 in that paper). The 22 GHz spectrum is an average of multiple epochs of spectral monitoring observations taken with the GBT.

The only component seen in the 183 GHz spectrum of NGC 5495 is the central set of systemic maser features, which span a velocity range of 15 around a central velocity of 6787 . The peak flux density of the systemic features is 9.9 mJy, which is a factor of 12 fainter than the typical peak flux density of the 22 GHz systemic masers. Adopting a Hubble law distance of 96 Mpc, the isotropic luminosity of the 183 GHz maser emission from NGC 5495 is L_ iso 163 L _ .

sources.summary: The paper identifies NGC 5495 as a barred Sc galaxy with a large bar, bright spiral arms, a 170 pc nuclear region, and a grand-design nuclear spiral.

quotes.quote: NGC 5495 NGC 5495 is a barred Sc galaxy (adapted from 1991Vaucouleurs ) with a luminosity distance of 97.5 Mpc 2000Mould . It is moderately inclined and hosts a large bar with two bright, counterclockwise wound spiral arms. The nuclear region's radius is 0.4 arcsec ( 170 pc), as indicated by flattened maxima in position angle and ellipticity profiles. A bar or spiral structure appears to be visible in dust within this nuclear region, so we classify it as either a disk or a ring. A two-arm grand-design nuclear spiral is also present (see discussion in Sect. sect:morphology ).

sources.summary: The paper treats NGC 5495 as a Seyfert 2 AGN with a north-south maser disk and estimates its mass-to-distance ratio, black-hole mass, recession velocity, and x offset.

quotes.quote: NGC 5495 is a Seyfert 2 AGN-type galaxy with an original north-south orientation maser disc with a P.A. of 176 5 . For this galaxy, we estimate the three parameters: the ratio M/D=1.15^ +0.21 _ 0.17 10^ 5 M_ /Mpc that implies a BH mass of M=1.10^ +0.21 _ 0.18 10^ 7 M_ , the recession velocity v_ rec , and the BH x offset, fitted without a scattering angle due to small reduced _ red ^ 2 =0.28 . Comparing the reported ^ 2 =0.22 in with the estimation in Table tab:Results4par , one can see that this general relativistic approach provides a slight improvement. The resulting BH position is optimal in the sense that the statistical fit renders it just behind the systemic masers (see Fig. fig:posteriors1 ).

gmw_00006256.name: Mrk 334
gmw_00006256.overview: Mrk 334 is a Seyfert 1.8 galaxy [arXiv:astro-ph/0605463] currently in the final stage of merging with a massive companion [arXiv:0909.2102, arXiv:astro-ph/0605463]. This interaction has triggered an intense circumnuclear starburst [arXiv:0909.2102, arXiv:astro-ph/0605463], a galactic superwind [arXiv:0909.2102], and variable X-ray flux [arXiv:2007.02616].

sources.summary: The paper describes Mrk 334 as a peculiar Seyfert galaxy in late-stage merger whose interaction redistributed disk mass, intensified nuclear activity, triggered an inner starburst, and drove a galactic superwind.

quotes.quote: We are presenting new results on kinematics and structure of the Mrk 334 Seyfert galaxy. Panoramic (3D) spectroscopy is performed at the 6-m telescope of the Special Astrophysical Observatory of the Russian Academy of Sciences using the MPFS integral-field spectrograph and scanning Fabry--P e rot interferometer. The deep images have revealed that Mrk 334 is observed during the final stage of its merging with a massive companion.

The merger has triggered mass redistribution in the disk resulting in an intensification of nuclear activity and in a burst of star formation in the inner region of the galaxy. The circumnuclear starburst is so intense that its contribution to the gas ionization exceeds that contribution of the AGN. We interpret the nuclear gas outflow with velocities of 200 as a galactic superwind that accompanies the violent star formation.

sources.summary: The paper fits Mrk 334 with Swift/XRT and NuSTAR spectra, finding flux variability and attributing it to a change in intrinsic power-law flux.

quotes.quote: Mrk 334: We fit the /XRT and \ FPMA/FPMB spectra of this source, assuming an absorbed power law plus reprocessed emission and an Apec component. The source shows flux variability. Due to the poor quality of the XRT spectrum we are not able to assess whether the variability is intrinsic or due to a change in absorption. Leaving N_ H free to vary results in unconstrained results. Thus, we assumed a change in the intrinsic power law flux.

sources.summary: The atlas treats Mrk 334 as an individually observed active or starburst galaxy, summarizing its prior classification and near-infrared continuum, emission-line, and absorption properties.

quotes.quote: Mrk 334 . This Seyfert galaxy from the CfA catalog, classified as 1.8, is located in an interacting system in an advanced stage of merger. It has a tidal arm visible in both the J and H images mar01 . The HST images in the F606W filter reported by pm02 reveal knots of emission quite near the nucleus, signaling at least some circumnuclear star formation. Support for this hypothesis comes from the L band spectroscopy of ima03 , where the 3.3 m PAH emission has been observed.

gmw_00006257.name: Mrk 993
gmw_00006257.overview: Mrk 993 is a relatively featureless spiral galaxy in the R band [arXiv:astro-ph/0612741]. It hosts an active galactic nucleus classified as a Seyfert 1.5 to 2 in optical bands, but it behaves as a genuine Seyfert 1 in the near-infrared [arXiv:astro-ph/0605463].

sources.summary: The atlas treats Mrk 993 as an individually observed active or starburst galaxy, summarizing its prior classification and near-infrared continuum, emission-line, and absorption properties.

quotes.quote: Mrk 993 . This CfA AGN hb92 has been classified as a Seyfert 1.5-2. Our spectrum displays a conspicuous broad component in He i 10830 , with FHWM=4500 , and in Pa (FHWM=3600 ). It means that in the NIR, this source can be considered a genuine Seyfert 1 object (see Fig. indiv1 ). The continuum emission decreases steeply towards longer wavelengths.

sources.summary: The paper describes Mrk 993 as a relatively featureless spiral whose disk and nuclear emission include H II regions, diffuse emission, and a ring-like structure, with no extraplanar emission detected.

quotes.quote: Mrk 993 (Figure f:mrk993 ) appears to be a relatively featureless spiral in the R band. The map shows emission from the nucleus, H ii regions scattered throughout the disk (as previously observed by col96 ), and traces of diffuse emission between the compact emission regions. The H ii regions are visible out to 53 (16.0 kpc) to the NE and 64 (19.3 kpc) to the SW of the nucleus. Also visible is an emitting ring-like structure of radius 14 ( 4.2 kpc) around the nucleus.

gmw_00006258.name: Mrk 124
gmw_00006258.overview: Mrk 124 is an active galactic nucleus [arXiv:astro-ph/0605463] that is optically classified as a narrow-line Seyfert 1 galaxy [arXiv:astro-ph/0605463]. Its near-infrared spectrum is dominated by bright permitted lines and forbidden high-ionization lines [arXiv:astro-ph/0605463].

sources.summary: The atlas treats Mrk 124 as an individually observed active or starburst galaxy, summarizing its prior classification and near-infrared continuum, emission-line, and absorption properties.

quotes.quote: Mrk 124 . Optically classified as a NLS1 galaxy by deg92 , the spectrum shown in Fig. indiv3 is the first one published on this source in the NIR region. It is dominated by bright permitted Pa , He i and O i and forbidden [ S iii ] lines. Forbidden high-ionization lines of [ S viii ] and [ Si vi ] are also detected. Pa shows a broad component of FWHM 2150 , while ver01 report a broad H component of FWHM in the range 1050-1400 .

gmw_00006259.name: Mrk 684
gmw_00006259.overview: Mrk 684 is a narrow-line Seyfert 1 galaxy [arXiv:astro-ph/0605463] that hosts an active galactic nucleus [arXiv:2007.11327]. It exhibits high star formation rates across the scale of the whole galaxy [arXiv:2007.11327] and features a 60-kiloparsec radio extension likely connected to its nucleus [arXiv:2007.11327].

sources.summary: The paper notes Mrk 684 as a low-threshold EESBH with whole-galaxy far-infrared star formation and a 60 kpc NVSS extension likely connected to the radio nucleus.

quotes.quote: This indicates that the radio emission in most of our EESBHs could be from AGN activities and only three sources (Mrk 684, SDSS J010712+140845 and SDSS J114008+030711) are below this threshold.

Five objects in our sample have the lowest requirement of _ SFR <10 M_ yr^ 1 kpc^ 2 in producing the observed radio emission, which are Mrk 42, Mrk 684, PG 1115+407, SDSS J010712.04+140845.0 and SDSS J114008.71+030711.4. Mrk 42 and Mrk 684 have a far-infrared luminosity L_ FIR 10^ 10 L_ , suggesting a high SFRs on the scale of the whole galaxy, that does not result in a high star formation rate surface density.

Mrk 684 shows an extension of 60 kpc to the north-west in the NVSS 1.4 GHz image (left panel of Figure fig:ksr ). The NVSS image reveals a two-component system where the second component is consistent in position with FIRST. A visual inspection of the SDSS- i image confirms that there is no obvious optical counterpart associated with the second component that also shows a structure of an AGN hotspot, implying a physical connection with the radio nucleus.

sources.summary: The paper identifies Mrk 684 as a narrow-line outlier with a large [OIII] width; it says HII subtraction and spectral resolution can make the NLR width significantly overestimated, so it excludes the object.

quotes.quote: It is clearly shown in Figures 1 and 2 that five NL objects, Mrk 507, Mrk 684, IRAS 15462-0450, MS 22102+1827, and PG 1404+226, having large [ OIII ] widths, deviate from other NL objects. Note that Mrk 507, Mrk 684, IRAS 15462-0450 are the three objects for which a narrow HII region contribution has been subtracted in the VVG sample. For spectra with a resolution of 3.4 used by VVG, the HII component may not be reliably separated from a narrow component of NLR with width 200---500 km s ^ 1 if an additional broad wing is present. Thus the widths of NLR in these three objects are likely to be significantly over-estimated.

sources.summary: The atlas treats Mrk 684 as an individually observed active or starburst galaxy, summarizing its prior classification and near-infrared continuum, emission-line, and absorption properties.

quotes.quote: Mrk 684 . oter85 classified this object as a NLS1. Indeed, optical spectroscopy reveals prominent Fe ii emission and broad Balmer lines with FHWM of only 1300 \ per88 . The NIR spectrum is dominated by permitted lines of H i and He i with FWHM 1150 . Permitted lines of Fe ii , O i and Ca ii (seen in emission) are also detected but with slightly narrower FWHM (900 ), suggesting that they are formed in the outer portions of the BLR. The NLR emission is almost absent, while [ S iii ] 9531 \ is likely to be present but is strongly blended with Pa 8 to make a secure detection.

gmw_00006260.name: UGC 4109
gmw_00006260.overview: UGC 4109 is a barred spiral galaxy that contains an offset neutral hydrogen hole [arXiv:1901.01164]. The galaxy also features a denser region on the opposite side of this hole and exhibits a regular velocity field [arXiv:1901.01164].

sources.summary: The paper finds UGC 4109 has an offset H I hole, a denser opposite-side region, and a regular velocity field despite no nearby companion explanation.

quotes.quote: UGC 4109 has also been observed by the VLA in C-configuration in Lemonias2014 . Named GASS 51390 in that study, they show the HI intensity map in their Figure 6. While this C configuation has lower spatial resolution than our data, we can clearly see there exists a hole in HI. Our observations reveal that the hole is slightly offset from the centre.

gmw_00006261.name: UGC 9244
gmw_00006261.overview: UGC 9244 is a barred spiral galaxy classified as SBbc [arXiv:1901.01164]. It features a long bar, two primary arms, and a third smaller arm that has been significantly distorted by an interaction with a close neighbor to the northeast [arXiv:1901.01164].

sources.summary: The paper describes UGC 9244 as an interacting barred spiral with a distorted arm, a long bar, high stellar mass, and redder optical colors.

quotes.quote: UGC 9244: this galaxy has some obvious interaction with a very close neighbour to the North-East. This has resulted in the northern most spiral arm being significantly distorted. UGC 9244 has two primary arms and a third smaller one. It was classifed as SBbc in the RC3. It hosts the third longest bar of the sample at 8.2 kpc, or 28 of the diameter of the disc.

gmw_00006262.name: UGC 6871
gmw_00006262.overview: UGC 6871 is a flocculent barred spiral galaxy [arXiv:1901.01164] that lies on the blue side of the green valley [arXiv:1901.01164]. The galaxy exhibits a distortion in one of its arms that appears to stretch outward [arXiv:1901.01164].

sources.summary: The paper describes UGC 6871 as a flocculent barred spiral with an arm distortion, offset H I hole, disturbed velocity field, and blue-green-valley colors.

quotes.quote: UGC 6871: is central in the range of optical colours and stellar masses of the HIRB sample. It has a mass of 10^ 10.6 and appears to be on the blue side of the green valley. Although UGC 6871 is clearly another flocculent spiral, there is a distortion in one of the arms at the lower left of the galaxy. It appears to be stretching out from the galaxy towards the end of the arm.

Curiously the RC3 classifies this as an SB0 (perhaps due to the limitations of photographic images blurring the flocculent arms together), while nair2010a classify it as Sc, missing the fairly obvious bar. In GZ2, 21 out of 39 classifiers indicated the presence of the bar.

gmw_00006263.name: UGC 7383
gmw_00006263.overview: UGC 7383 is a blue, lower-mass barred spiral galaxy with a stellar mass of 10^10.1 solar masses [arXiv:1901.01164]. The galaxy features a physically short bar and two defined, tightly wound spiral arms, showing no obvious evidence of interaction with nearby galaxies [arXiv:1901.01164].

sources.summary: The paper describes UGC 7383 as a blue, lower-mass barred galaxy with a short bar, tightly wound spiral arms, and no obvious interaction.

quotes.quote: UGC 7383: is an obviously blue galaxy. It also has a stellar mass of 10^ 10.1 , making it the least massive of the sample. Accordingly, UGC 7383 has one of the physically shortest bars of the sample at 5.1 kpc, which however still covers 30 of the extent of the optical disc. The optical image of this galaxy shows two defined, tightly wound spiral arms, without much evidence of interaction with any nearby galaxies. The RC3 classifies this as Sab, again missing the obvious bar, spotted by 22 out of 38 GZ2 classifiers.

gmw_00006264.name: UGC 8408
gmw_00006264.overview: UGC 8408 is a blue, low-mass barred spiral galaxy with a stellar mass of 10^10.2 solar masses [arXiv:1901.01164]. It features a distorted optical morphology with two main grand design spiral arms and a relatively short central bar [arXiv:1901.01164].

sources.summary: The paper describes UGC 8408 as NGC 5115, a blue low-mass barred spiral with the shortest bar and a distorted extra-arm morphology.

quotes.quote: UGC 8408: is also known as NGC 5115. This is the final HIRB galaxy and has the shortest actual bar length at 4.26 kpc, which is less than third of the length of the longest bar in the sample. While UGC 8408's bar still covers 21 of the disc's optical extent, this is the shortest relative bar length of the entire sample, which hints that it might be the most recently formed.

UGC 8408 is one of the `bluest' galaxies we observed, it is also the second least massive galaxy with a stellar mass value of 10^ 10.2 . There is some obvious distortion to the optical morphology of this galaxy. It appears to be a grand design spiral, with two main arms which are clearly defined.

gmw_00006265.name: UGC 9362
gmw_00006265.overview: UGC 9362 is a galaxy that exhibits ongoing star formation despite having very little H I gas above the typical star-formation threshold [arXiv:1901.01164]. Its velocity field appears somewhat distorted, with bluer velocities distributed through the middle velocities rather than collected on one side [arXiv:1901.01164].

sources.summary: The paper describes UGC 9362 as having little H I above the star-formation threshold but ongoing star formation and a distorted velocity field.

quotes.quote: Finally UGC 9362 is the only one of the six HIRB galaxies which does not reach this star forming in any significant area (there is a small part which does around RA: 14h33m18.5s, Dec: 3 54' but it is too small to be of any significance morphologically). This galaxy does however appear to be forming stars, which must be happening at a lower HI surface density than is typical. HI observations also provide velocity fields. we can see that the velocity field for four of the HIRB galaxies are rotating regularly (UGC 4109, UGC 9244, UGC 7383 and UGC 8408). The remaining two galaxy's velocities (UGC 9362 and UGC 6871) appear to be somewhat distorted. In UGC 9362, the `bluer' velocities are distributed through the middle velocities (green) rather than collected on one side.

gmw_00006266.name: Arp 270
gmw_00006266.overview: Arp 270 is a tightly interacting galaxy pair consisting of the spiral galaxy NGC 3395 and the irregular galaxy NGC 3396 [arXiv:2011.07485]. The system exhibits collision-driven star formation, disturbed gas kinematics, and strong 60-micron emission [arXiv:2011.07485, arXiv:1302.5011].

sources.summary: The review describes Arp 270 as a tightly interacting NGC 3395/3396 pair with external star-forming islands, disturbed gas kinematics, gas mixing, and collision-driven star formation.

quotes.quote: Arp 270 = NGC 3395/96 is a tightly interacting pair of galaxies of comparable luminosity: a spiral galaxy NGC 3395 and irregular galaxy (Irr or Sm) NGC 3396, strongly inclined to the line of sight. The relative motion of galaxies seems to occur in a plane perpendicular to the line of sight, because their central systemic velocities nearly coincide. There are numerous separate islands of star formation beyond the main body of galaxies. The most notable of them is the oblong emission region of about one kpc-size between galaxies.

Irregular velocity distribution and a high velocity dispersion of emitting gas exceeding 50 km/s give evidence of the local non-circular gas flows within a scale of about 1 kpc. There is a steep velocity gradient in the region between galaxies accompanied by gradual grow of velocity dispersion of gas to the periphery of galactic discs – most probably as the result of direct collision of peripheral gaseous systems of two galaxies.

sources.summary: The paper treats NGC 3395/3396 as a combined pair because overlapping apertures made per-galaxy emission hard to assign, and notes low UV attenuation and strong 60 micron emission.

quotes.quote: NGC 3395/3396: The apertures of this pair of galaxies overlapped sufficiently to make determination of the emission belonging to each galaxy problematic. We therefore opted to treat the system as a combined system. This pair of galaxies is distantly associated with Group 5. This group does not have UBV photometry. The SED shows little attenuation in the UV and strong 60 emission relative to the 100 emission.

gmw_00006267.name: Arp 194
gmw_00006267.overview: Arp 194 is a tightly interacting system of recently collided galaxies containing a northern and a southern component [arXiv:1607.08761, arXiv:2011.07485]. The collision perturbed the galaxies' peripheral structures and created a bridge of active star-forming regions between them [arXiv:1607.08761, arXiv:2011.07485].

sources.summary: The paper describes Arp 194 as a tightly interacting galaxy system whose collision produced disturbed components, a chemically structured bridge, and unusual star-forming regions between the galaxies.

quotes.quote: Arp 194 = VV126 is a tightly interacting system containing two main galaxies with active star-formation -- southern (S) and northern (N) ones, separated by 30 kpc (in projection). N-galaxy, in turn, consists of two apparently merging galaxies (Na-component possessing the distinct spiral arms and the less noticeable and redder component Nb, projected onto the eastern spiral arm of Na-galaxy). The peripheral structure of the galaxies, especially of the northern ones, is perturbed by the recent close approach or direct collision of N and S-galaxies.

Arp194 is of special interest due to the presence of the chain of star-formation regions between the galaxies which seldom occurs in interacting systems. Indeed, if galaxies retained their integrality after the close passage, the extended regions containing young stars are usually observed either in long tidal tails or inside the galactic discs, but not in the bridge between galaxies.

Monotonous variation of the observed velocity along the slits passing through the chain of emission regions between galaxies, as well as the trend of decreasing of oxygen abundance O/H with the distance from N and S-galaxies agrees with the conclusion that the emission regions in the bridge of Arp194 were formed in the flow(s) of gas lost by both galaxies after collision.

sources.summary: The paper describes Arp 194 as a recently collided galaxy system with active bridge star formation, disturbed gas, and a likely bound tidal dwarf galaxy lacking significant dark matter.

quotes.quote: Arp 194 is a system of recently collided galaxies, where the southern galaxy (S) passed through the gaseous disc of the northern galaxy (N) which in turn consists of two close components (Fig.2). This system is of special interest due to the presence of regions of active star-formation in the bridge between galaxies, the brightest of which (the region A) has a size of at least 4 kpc. We obtained three spectral slices of the system for different slit positions and estimated the radial distribution of line-of-sight velocity and velocity dispersion as well as the intensities of emission lines and oxygen abundance 12+ ( O/H ) .

We confirm that the largest emission island (region A) is most probably a gravitationally bound TDG with the age of 10^7 - 10^8 yr. There is no evidence of the significant amount of dark matter in this dwarf galaxy.

gmw_00006268.name: Arp 305
gmw_00006268.overview: Arp 305 is an interacting galaxy group dominated by a wide pair of moderate-luminosity spiral galaxies, NGC 4016 and NGC 4017 [arXiv:2011.07485]. This gas-rich system features extended ultraviolet emission, tidal tails, and a bridge containing a young tidal dwarf galaxy candidate [arXiv:0904.0670, arXiv:2011.07485].

sources.summary: The paper presents Arp 305 as a gas-rich interacting pair with extended UV star formation, tidal tails, young clumps, and numerical modeling of the encounter and candidate tidal dwarfs.

quotes.quote: As part of that study, we present a GALEX UV and SDSS and SARA optical study of the gas-rich interacting galaxy pair Arp 305 (NGC 4016/7). The GALEX UV data reveal much extended diffuse UV emission and star formation outside the disks. This includes a luminous star forming region between the two galaxies, and a number of such regions in tidal tails. We have identified 45 young star forming clumps in Arp 305, including several TDG candidates.

The two disks in Arp 305 are separated by 356 \ ( 86 kpc). Assuming an interaction velocity of 300 , the two galaxies passed close by each other about 300 Myr ago. This is consistent with our numerical modeling (see 4.2).

sources.summary: The review presents Arp 305 as an interacting spiral pair whose bridge contains a young tidal-dwarf candidate with disturbed gas velocities, high oxygen abundance, and mostly gaseous mass.

quotes.quote: Arp 305 is a small group of galaxies dominated by the wide pair of interacting spiral galaxies of moderate luminosity: NGC 4016 and NGC 4017. For the adopted distance 50 Mpc the projected separation of galaxies is 86 kpc. The largest concentration of HI between galaxies coincides with the optical chain of blue knots of total magnitude B 17 , immersed in a faint haze, most clearly visible in UV see Sengupta2017 . It is located about halfway between the galaxies and stretched along the line connecting them at least at 7 kpc.

We found that gas velocities in TDG are more disturbed than in the adjacent regions. At the same time, we observed neither noticeable rotation nor expansion of TDG: mean velocities of its two brightest clumps differ by no more than 20 km/s. The oxygen abundance of TDG found by izi Blanc2015 and O3N2 Marino2013 methods is 8.1 -- 8.3, which is too high for its absolute magnitude M_B 16 mag.

gmw_00006269.name: NGC 4656UV
gmw_00006269.overview: NGC 4656UV is a low-surface-brightness dwarf galaxy that is a close satellite of the edge-on galaxy NGC 4656 [arXiv:2011.07485]. It is a gravitationally bound, dark matter-dominated system with an axisymmetric exponential disc and enhanced brightness in the ultraviolet range [arXiv:2011.07485].

sources.summary: The review treats NGC 4656UV as a UV-bright low-surface-brightness dwarf near NGC 4656, arguing from kinematics and photometry that it is a bound axisymmetric galaxy.

quotes.quote: The unusual dwarf low surface brightness galaxy NGC 4656UV is a close satellite of the edge-on galaxy NGC 4656. It is barely visible in the SDSS image, however it has the the enhanced brightness in the UV range. Multiwavelength data analysis carried out by led these authors to the conclusions that NGC 4646UV is a low-metallicity TDG candidate whose major burst of star formation occurred within the last 260-290 Myr.

Our estimates of emission gas velocities of NGC 4656UV parallel with photometrical and HI data speak in favour of this system to be a dark matter-dominated gravitationally bound axisymmetric galaxy with the dynamic mass of about 2 10^9 solar masses, rather than a tidal dwarf candidate. It has quite axisymmetric exponential disc with the low central surface brightness ( _ 0 )_r = 24.1 mag/arcsec ^2 and the radial scale length of about 2 kpc. The parameters of NGC 4656UV are close to that observed for separate ultra-diffuse galaxies.

gmw_00006270.name: NGC 5812
gmw_00006270.overview: NGC 5812 is an isolated, nearly spherical elliptical galaxy [arXiv:2311.12598, arXiv:1212.1451] that features diffuse dust surrounding its core [arXiv:1212.1451]. It exhibits a disturbed velocity field [arXiv:2311.12598] and is actively interacting with a nucleated early-type dwarf companion [arXiv:2311.12598, arXiv:1212.1451].

sources.summary: The paper studies isolated elliptical NGC 5812 with GC and galaxy-light kinematics, finding disturbed morphology, uncertain symmetry, MOND-compatible masses, and no requirement for a massive dark halo.

quotes.quote: The isolated elliptical NGC 5812 is another object to investigate a possible role of isolation. We use globular clusters (GCs) and the galaxy light itself as dynamical tracers to constrain its mass profile. We identify 28 GCs with an outermost galactocentric distance of 20 kpc, for which velocities could be obtained. Furthermore, 16 spectra of the integrated galaxy light out to 6 kpc have been used to model the central kinematics.

These spectra provide evidence for a disturbed velocity field, which is plausible given the disturbed morphology of the galaxy. We construct spherical Jeans models for the galaxy light and apply tracer mass estimators for the globular clusters. With the assumptions inherent to the mass estimators, MOND is compatible with the mass out to 20 kpc. However, a dark matter free galaxy is not excluded, given the uncertainties related to a possible non-sphericity and a possible non-equilibrium state.

sources.summary: The paper describes NGC5812's companion as a nucleated early-type dwarf with a long tidal tail, old metal-poor colors, a measured radial velocity, and an elongated near-pericenter orbit.

quotes.quote: The companion of NGC5812 forms a long tidal tail testifying to its proximity to NGC 5812 lane13 . It is nucleated and of early-type. A Washington colour C-R=1.33 is typical for an old, moderately metal-poor population. The nucleus is not distinguishable in colour from the bulk of the stellar population. We put a slit of length 5 on the dwarf galaxy, but S/N turned out to be insufficient for a population study.

The radial velocity is 1665 km/s, differing by -260 km/s from the systemic velocity. The circular velocity at this projected distance 2.5' (20.3) kpc) is between 190 km/s (stellar mass only) and 270 km/s (MONDian expectation) and therefore we expect the dwarf on its orbit to be near its pericenter. As lane13 note, the plurality of tidal features might be the debris of previous orbits of the companion.

sources.summary: The paper models NGC 5812 as an isolated E0 elliptical with a small dwarf companion, diffuse central dust, and confirmed tidal interaction with [ MFB03 ] 1.

quotes.quote: In contrast, NGC 5812 appears to be very spherical (classified as E0 by deVaucouleurs91 and has an ellipticity of only 0.05, e.g. Bender88 ). Note that these galaxies are considered isolated in that they have no companion galaxies within 75 kpc, although NGC 5812 may have a small dwarf companion .
For NGC 5812 the age is estimated using < Fe > and the line strengths of H and Mg b in accordance with single-burst stellar population models .

A fairly spherical region of very diffuse dust also surrounds the core of NGC 5812, out to a radius of 7-8'' . Outside the diffuse dust in the central regions, the colour profile of each galaxy is very flat, and is fairly typical of field ellipticals at (C-R) 1.7 . Dwarf galaxy interaction with NGC 5812 The interaction between NGC 5812 and its dwarf companion galaxy, [ MFB03 ] 1, has been confirmed here for the first time. The coherent nature of the debris resulting from the interaction, as well the change in position angle of the isophotes with radius, and the tell-tail `S' shape of the isophotal contours of the region immediately surrounding the dwarf, confirm the tidal nature of this debris .

gmw_00006271.name: [ MFB03 ] 1
gmw_00006271.overview: [ MFB03 ] 1 is a nucleated dwarf elliptical companion galaxy to NGC 5812 [arXiv:1212.1451]. It has an apparent R-band magnitude of 16.6 and exhibits tidal arms that confirm an ongoing interaction with its host galaxy [arXiv:1212.1451].

sources.summary: The paper identifies [ MFB03 ] 1 as a nucleated dwarf companion of NGC 5812, measuring its magnitude and arguing its tidal debris confirms an ongoing interaction.

quotes.quote: Dwarf galaxy interaction with NGC 5812 It appears that, at least by visual inspection of Figure NGC5812galaxyfig , that a dwarf companion galaxy is interacting with NGC 5812; apparent tidal debris can be seen to both the North and South of the host galaxy in the upper panel and the bottom panel shows what appear to be tidal arms emanating from the dwarf companion. This dwarf is given the designation of [ MFB03 ] 1 by who classify it as a nucleated (dNE) dwarf.

From the flux enclosed within the outermost isophote of the model, we calculate the apparent magnitude for the dwarf galaxy to be m _R 16.6 . Moreover, in addition to the changing PA of the luminosity contours discussed above, the PA of the isophotes in the ellipse model increase steadily with radius from 80^ near the core to 15^ in the outer isophotes. This makes it very clear that the dwarf is undergoing a tidal interaction with NGC 5812, and to the best knowledge of the authors, this is the first definitive evidence that [ MFB03 ] 1 is interacting with NGC 5812.

gmw_00006272.name: J0958+3222
gmw_00006272.overview: The galaxy J0958+3222, also known as NGC 3067, is a spiral galaxy [arXiv:1002.5033] that exhibits starburst activity [arXiv:astro-ph/0004226]. It features infalling high-velocity gas [arXiv:1002.5033] and elongated X-ray emission [arXiv:astro-ph/0004226], with cold gas comprising a substantial fraction of its neutral sightline gas [arXiv:1607.03965].

sources.summary: The paper reports cold H I absorption and Ca II absorption associated with J0958+3222/NGC 3067, estimating cold gas as a substantial fraction of the sightline neutral gas.

quotes.quote: We detected an absorber associated with J0958+3222 (NGC 3067) and emission from six galaxies. We also found two Ca 2 absorption system in the archival SDSS data associated with galaxies J0958+3222 and J1228+3706, , although the sample was not selected based on the presence of metal lines. Our detection rate of absorbers with optical depths of 0.06 is 7 . We also find that cold phase ( 300 K) is 44( 18) of the total atomic gas in the sightline probing J0958+3222.

We detected in absorption towards sightline J0958 + 3224 probing foreground galaxy J0958+3222, also known as NGC 3067. This absorber was first discovered by haschick75 and subsequently re-observed by carilli92, keeney05 . Figure fig-hi_abs shows the absorption feature over the emission profile from the galaxy in the left panel and an emission subtracted profile in the right panel.

sources.summary: The paper uses 2MASX09582105+3222119 as a worked 2MTF example, listing its log-distance ratio, redshift, peculiar velocity, non-Gaussian velocity PDF, and Box-Cox transformed velocity.

quotes.quote: To clearly present the algorithm, we randomly choose one galaxy from the 2MTF catalogue, the 2MASS ID of this galaxy is `2MASX09582105+3222119', the log-distance ratio (and error), redshift and peculiar velocity (estimated using Eq. travp ) of this galaxy are listed in Table galexamp , we also listed its Galactic longitude and latitude b in the table.
The curve is bias from Gaussian significantly, i.e. the peculiar velocity of this galaxy does not have Gaussian error.

As shown in Fig. BCLBDs , =15.646 is the best estimated BC transformation parameter for this galaxy. (v) Gaussianize the peculiar velocity: Plugging the galaxy's peculiar velocity V= 460.15 km s ^ 1 and =15.646 as well as the above into Eq. boxc1 , we obtain the Y= 7.3 10 ^ 66 . This is the BC transformed `velocity' for this galaxy.

sources.summary: The paper uses HST imaging of the 3C 232/NGC 3067 pair to identify a Lyman-limit absorber as infalling high-velocity gas associated with the nearby spiral galaxy.

quotes.quote: Here we present new Hubble Space Telescope ( ) images of the QSO/galaxy pair, 3C 232/NGC 3067. The quasar spectrum contains a Lyman-limit (N _ H I = 1 10^ 20   cm^ 2 ) absorption system (LLS) at cz =1421   \ that is associated with the nearby SAB galaxy NGC 3067. Previous work identifies this absorber as a high-velocity cloud (HVC) in NGC 3067 but the kinematics of the absorbing gas, infalling or outflowing, were uncertain. The \ images presented here establish the orientation of NGC 3067 and so establish that the LLS/HVC is infalling.

sources.summary: The paper argues that NGC 3067 has real elongated X-ray emission, not produced by contamination from the adjacent quasar PSF.

quotes.quote: The radio-loud quasar 3C 232 ( z=0.534 ) is adjacent to the starburst galaxy NGC 3067. In this case, too, the effect of contamination by the quasar's PSF is small and the elongation seen by Arp (1996) in the 0.7--2.0 keV X-ray emission of NGC 3067 appears real; simulation shows that this degree of extension in the smoothed image is too great to be produced by chance, particularly as it is not directed towards the quasar (which is what we would expect for an artefact of the sort discussed in section psf ). Arp (1996) also reports filaments in smoothed 0.1-2.0 keV images connecting 3C 232 to stellar objects to the north and northeast.

Simulation shows that we would not expect a filament even at this level connecting 3C 232 with the northern stellar object by chance under the null hypothesis, which suggests that there is some real emission in between 3C 232 and the northern stellar object, but the data provide no significant evidence for such a filament rather than, for example, an intervening point source.

gmw_00006273.name: LAB1
gmw_00006273.overview: LAB1 is a Lyman-alpha nebula candidate located at a photometric redshift of approximately 3.3 [arXiv:2005.12707]. The object is composed of a main bright core and a faint halo, spanning an extent of 28 kpc by 59 kpc [arXiv:2005.12707].

sources.summary: The paper identifies LAB1 as a z~3.3 Ly-alpha nebula candidate with bright-core and halo morphology, broad-band photometry, and no detected X-ray or UV counterpart.
sources.paper_coordinate_text: The main clump, also called the bright core, is located at RA=149.97769, Dec=1.9358.

quotes.quote: We identified a Ly \ nebula candidate at z _ phot 3.3 covering an isophotal area of 29.4 arcsec ^ 2 . Its morphology shows a bright core and a faint core which coincides with the morphology of previously known Ly \ blobs. A first estimation of the Ly equivalent width and line flux agree with the values from the study led by several groups.

One object (LAB1) in particular caught our immediate interest, after we ran ATACAMA several times and independently of the parameter variations during the different tests.
